translation: O you who have believed, fulfill [all] contracts. Lawful for you are the animals of grazing livestock except for that which is recited to you [in this Qur'an] - hunting not being permitted while you are in the state of ihram. Indeed, Allah ordains what He intends
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَوْفُوا۟ بِٱلْعُقُودِ ۚ أُحِلَّتْ لَكُم بَهِيمَةُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ إِلَّا مَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ غَيْرَ مُحِلِّى ٱلصَّيْدِ وَأَنتُمْ حُرُمٌ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ مَا يُرِيدُ

### Context and Placement

Surah Al-Ma'idah stands as the fourth and final large Madani surah in the opening sections of the Quran. These four surahs—Al-Baqarah, Ali 'Imran, An-Nisa, and Al-Ma'idah—collectively complete the first seventh (hizb) of the Quran. This also marks the completion of what we might call the "Madani territory" before transitioning into predominantly Makki surahs.

This surah represents some of the final revelations given to the Muslim community, containing finishing touches on Islamic law (Shariah). Many of its instructions address matters that could wait until the end of the prophetic mission, offering parting advice and congratulations from Allah ﷻ along with guidance for the Ummah's future.

To understand Al-Ma'idah's place in the divine narrative, we should recognize the progression of these four Madani surahs:

**Al-Baqarah** (early Madani) established the foundations—learning from previous nations' mistakes, being given a new qiblah, and receiving a mission and agenda to work toward.

**Ali 'Imran** (mid-Madani) prepared the believers for difficult situations, particularly in battle, and began introducing the reality of hypocrites within the community.

**An-Nisa** (later Madani) addressed internal matters in detail—social values, ethics, and the major internal problem of hypocrisy (nifaq). Within these social values, family law received special attention since all communities are built upon families.

**Al-Ma'idah** (latest Madani) now discusses dietary laws, Hajj regulations, and other specific rulings. Why now? Because the major obstacles to Islam's mission have been overcome. Much of this surah comes after the conquest of Makkah, after the Treaty of Hudaybiyah, when it became clear that the disbelievers could no longer mount effective attacks against the Muslims.

### The Foundation: Fulfilling Contracts

Allah ﷻ begins with a profound constitutional principle: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَوْفُوا۟ بِٱلْعُقُودِ—"O you who believe, fulfill your contracts."

This phrase deserves to be displayed prominently in every business, every professional office, every workplace. The word عُقُود  is the plural of عَقْد, from which we derive the theological term aqeedah—a set of beliefs that bind us to Islam. Uqood refers to all agreements that are binding, all commitments that tie us to obligations.

These contracts encompass every dimension of human interaction:
- The marriage bond between husband and wife
- Employment agreements between employer and employee
- The social contract between government and citizens
- The terms of a driver's license or bank account
- Even unspoken agreements of courtesy and respect

We have unspoken social contracts constantly at play. When you sit on a bus and don't spread out to occupy multiple seats, when you show courtesy to fellow drivers on the road, when you respect others' personal space—these are all forms of uqood. We might call them the social contract, a general understanding among people to extend mutual courtesy, kindness, and respect for life, property, and dignity.

Some contracts are more serious than others, of course. Our covenant with Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, our commitment to the Quran, our daily prayer obligations—these represent the most sacred of contracts. A husband's loyalty to his wife, a wife's devotion to her husband—these too are binding uqood.

Every aspect of our lives involves contracts because we are social creatures. We don't live isolated on mountaintops; we exist in communities. Even now, as you read this commentary, there exists a contract between teacher and student. Certain expectations must be fulfilled on both sides, certain boundaries must not be crossed.

The Quran employs two critical terms regarding obligations: إِفْرَاط and تَفْرِيط. Ifrat means going beyond what you should—crossing a line you agreed not to cross. Tafreet means falling short of expectations—not meeting the minimum required of you. Both constitute violations of uqood. You can break an agreement either by exceeding your bounds or by failing to meet your obligations.

### Dietary Permissions and Their Wisdom

After establishing this foundational principle, Allah ﷻ immediately addresses a practical matter: أُحِلَّتْ لَكُم بَهِيمَةُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ—"Lawful for you are the grazing animals."

The term بَهِيمَةُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ (baheemat al-an'aam) refers to four-legged livestock—cattle, sheep, goats, and similar animals that graze on plant life. This category specifically excludes predators and carnivores like wolves, lions, bears, and cheetahs. The permitted animals are vegetarian creatures that feed off the earth's vegetation.

However, this permission comes with exceptions: إِلَّا مَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ—"except what is recited to you"—referring to specific prohibitions that will be detailed later in the surah, particularly in ayah 3.

Another crucial exception follows: غَيْرَ مُحِلِّى ٱلصَّيْدِ وَأَنتُمْ حُرُمٌ—"not making lawful the hunting [of animals] while you are in the state of ihram."

This restriction carries deep spiritual significance. Imagine traveling for months on foot toward Hajj with no convenience stores, no gas stations with snacks. These pilgrims would see wild animals along their journey—perhaps a deer or goat just wandering nearby. For someone who knows how to hunt, with a bow ready, the temptation would be real. The animal might not even be fleeing, seemingly presenting itself as an easy meal.

Yet Allah ﷻ commands: once you enter the state of ihram, you cannot hunt. Nobody is watching in the wilderness, but Allah ﷻ is testing the believer's taqwa (God-consciousness). This is a test of fulfilling uqood—your contract with Allah ﷻ—even when no human eyes can see you.

The ayah concludes with a reminder of divine authority: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ مَا يُرِيدُ—"Indeed, Allah judges as He wills." The decisions about what is permissible and what is forbidden rest with Him alone. His wisdom determines these boundaries, and our role is to submit to His judgment with trust.

translation: O you who have believed, do not violate the rites of Allah or [the sanctity of] the sacred month or [neglect the marking of] the sacrificial animals and garlanding [them] or [violate the safety of] those coming to the Sacred House seeking bounty from their Lord and [His] approval. But when you come out of ihram, then [you may] hunt. And do not let the hatred of a people for having obstructed you from al-Masjid al-Haram lead you to transgress. And cooperate in righteousness and piety, but do not cooperate in sin and aggression. And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is severe in penalty
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُحِلُّوا۟ شَعَـٰٓئِرَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا ٱلشَّهْرَ ٱلْحَرَامَ وَلَا ٱلْهَدْىَ وَلَا ٱلْقَلَـٰٓئِدَ وَلَآ ءَآمِّينَ ٱلْبَيْتَ ٱلْحَرَامَ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرِضْوَٰنًا ۚ وَإِذَا حَلَلْتُمْ فَٱصْطَادُوا۟ ۚ وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَـَٔانُ قَوْمٍ أَن صَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ أَن تَعْتَدُوا۟ ۘ وَتَعَاوَنُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلْبِرِّ وَٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ۖ وَلَا تَعَاوَنُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

### Respecting Sacred Symbols

This ayah presents a comprehensive code of conduct for believers in positions of strength and authority. The historical context is crucial: Makkah has just been conquered. Islam is now victorious. The Muslims who were once persecuted and driven from their homes have returned as liberators.

Allah ﷻ begins with multiple prohibitions, each building upon the theme of respecting sanctity:

**لَا تُحِلُّوا۟ شَعَـٰٓئِرَ ٱللَّهِ**—"Do not violate the sacred symbols of Allah." The word شَعَائِر (sha'a'ir) refers to things that remind us of Allah ﷻ—the Kaaba, the rituals of Ibrahim (AS), sacred places that connect us to divine remembrance. When something has sanctity, it is called haram (forbidden to violate). To make something halal when it should remain haram means to remove its sanctity and treat it casually. The believers must maintain the honor and reverence of Allah's sacred symbols.

**وَلَا ٱلشَّهْرَ ٱلْحَرَامَ**—"Nor [violate] the sacred month." The sacred months were established times when warfare was forbidden. Even in their new position of power, Muslims must respect these temporal boundaries.

**وَلَا ٱلْهَدْىَ**—"Nor [violate] the sacrificial animals." These are animals designated for sacrifice during Hajj, already herded and marked for that sacred purpose. Even if someone sees such an animal during their journey, they cannot claim it for themselves. It has been devoted to Allah ﷻ.

**وَلَا ٱلْقَلَـٰٓئِدَ**—"Nor [violate] the garlanded [animals]." The word قَلَائِد (qala'id) refers to garlands or bells placed around an animal's neck as a marker. These markings indicated that someone had pledged this animal for future sacrifice—perhaps making a vow that if Allah ﷻ blessed them in a certain way, they would sacrifice this animal at Hajj. Once marked for Allah ﷻ, the animal becomes untouchable.

### The Most Powerful Protection

Then comes perhaps the most remarkable instruction: **وَلَآ ءَآمِّينَ ٱلْبَيْتَ ٱلْحَرَامَ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرِضْوَٰنًا**—"And [do not violate the sanctity of] those coming to the Sacred House seeking bounty from their Lord and [His] pleasure."

This represents a new constitution for Makkah. Before Islam's victory, the pre-Islamic Arabs would violate sacred months when it suited them, even manipulating the calendar to justify attacks on caravans. But Allah ﷻ tells the victorious Muslims: anyone who comes to My House in peace must be left in peace.

There will be strangers coming to Hajj—people from distant tribes who have just accepted Islam, people the Makkan Muslims have never met. Don't be suspicious of them. Don't attack them. They're seeking favor and pleasure from their Master. That seeking of divine pleasure becomes their protection, regardless of their tribal affiliation or past history.

**وَإِذَا حَلَلْتُمْ فَٱصْطَادُوا۟**—"But when you come out of ihram, then [you may] hunt." Once the sacred state ends, the restrictions are lifted. This balanced approach shows that the prohibitions serve a specific spiritual purpose during ihram; they are not arbitrary constraints.

### Justice Even Toward Enemies

Now Allah ﷻ addresses what happens when victory comes to those who were once oppressed: **وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَـَٔانُ قَوْمٍ أَن صَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ أَن تَعْتَدُوا۟**—"And do not let the hatred of a people who prevented you from Al-Masjid Al-Haram lead you to transgress."

This instruction comes at a critical moment. The Muslims are now in a position of power. Those who were their enemies for years, who persecuted them and drove them from their homes, are now vulnerable. The natural human response would be revenge, retaliation, settling old scores.

Allah ﷻ says: Don't let your animosity—your شَنَآن, your hatred toward these people—compel you to transgress. Don't let it drive you to commit injustice. They stopped you from reaching Al-Masjid Al-Haram, but that doesn't give you the right to violate their rights now.

Someone might come to make Hajj from a tribe that was once your enemy. You recognize them. You remember what they did. Perhaps they damaged your favorite sword in battle, or worse. Allah ﷻ says: you cannot stop them. You cannot deny them access to His House. That would be transgression.

This principle is essential for understanding victory in Islam. Victors throughout history have been sources of tremendous corruption. When one side wins decisively, they often go door to door robbing people, killing indiscriminately, committing atrocities. This is the pattern of human conquest.

Allah ﷻ establishes a completely different standard for Muslim victory.

### Cooperation in Righteousness

**وَتَعَاوَنُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلْبِرِّ وَٱلتَّقْوَىٰ**—"And cooperate in righteousness and piety." Now is the time for cooperation, but only in specific directions. Cooperate in birr (good deeds, righteousness) and taqwa (God-consciousness, piety).

**وَلَا تَعَاوَنُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ**—"But do not cooperate in sin and aggression." Don't band together to commit sins. Don't gang up on anyone out of animosity.

This instruction becomes especially relevant for how victors treat prisoners of war. Throughout history, POWs have been beaten, humiliated, spat upon, tortured—and worse, as we've witnessed in recent conflicts. Allah ﷻ tells the Muslims: you will not cooperate with each other in any form of sin or animosity. You will not gang up on the weak and vulnerable, even if they were once your enemies.

**وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ**—"And be conscious of Allah." You may have overcome your enemy in battle, but you have not overcome Allah ﷻ. His watchfulness over you continues, and His standards for your behavior remain absolute.

**إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ**—"Indeed, Allah is severe in penalty." This reminder comes as a warning: if you abuse your position of power, if you transgress against others despite these clear prohibitions, Allah ﷻ is intense in taking vengeance and imposing consequences.

translation: Prohibited to you are dead animals, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been dedicated to other than Allah, and [those animals] killed by strangling or by a violent blow or by a head-long fall or by the goring of horns, and those from which a wild animal has eaten, except what you [are able to] slaughter [before its death], and those which are sacrificed on stone altars, and [prohibited is] that you seek decision through divining arrows. That is grave disobedience. This day those who disbelieve have despaired of [defeating] your religion; so fear them not, but fear Me. This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favor upon you and have approved for you Islam as religion. But whoever is forced by severe hunger with no inclination to sin - then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةُ وَٱلدَّمُ وَلَحْمُ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ وَمَآ أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ وَٱلْمُنْخَنِقَةُ وَٱلْمَوْقُوذَةُ وَٱلْمُتَرَدِّيَةُ وَٱلنَّطِيحَةُ وَمَآ أَكَلَ ٱلسَّبُعُ إِلَّا مَا ذَكَّيْتُمْ وَمَا ذُبِحَ عَلَى ٱلنُّصُبِ وَأَن تَسْتَقْسِمُوا۟ بِٱلْأَزْلَـٰمِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ فِسْقٌ ۗ ٱلْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن دِينِكُمْ فَلَا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَٱخْشَوْنِ ۚ ٱلْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِى وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمَ دِينًا ۚ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ فِى مَخْمَصَةٍ غَيْرَ مُتَجَانِفٍ لِّإِثْمٍ ۙ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

### Dietary Prohibitions and Cultural Transformation

The connection between this ayah's dietary restrictions and the preceding commands about justice and sacred symbols is not immediately obvious, but it reflects a profound social reality. The Muhajirun had returned to Makkah after a decade of exile. Before they were forced to leave, they had lived their entire lives in this city, consuming certain foods without a second thought. When an animal died from natural causes in the heat, they would simply cut it open and cook it. This was their culture, their norm.

Now, with Islam's victory and the completion of divine law, new restrictions arrive. These aren't just about food—they represent a complete transformation of culture and identity.

**حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةُ**—"Prohibited to you is the dead animal [carrion]." An animal that died of natural causes without proper slaughter.

**وَٱلدَّمُ**—"And blood."

**وَلَحْمُ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ**—"And the flesh of swine."

**وَمَآ أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ**—"And that which has been dedicated to other than Allah"—animals slaughtered while invoking names other than Allah's.

Then Allah ﷻ provides specific categories of improperly killed animals:

**وَٱلْمُنْخَنِقَةُ**—"And [that which is killed by] strangling." From the root خ ن ق (kh-n-q), referring to an animal that died from choking. Perhaps a goat tied to a fence accidentally wrapped its rope around itself, pulling tighter with each movement until it choked to death. Despite being a waste of precious meat, if it died this way, it becomes forbidden.

**وَٱلْمَوْقُوذَةُ**—"And [that killed by] a blow." An animal that died from being struck or injured—perhaps a rock fell on it, and it bled out or died from internal injuries over time.

**وَٱلْمُتَرَدِّيَةُ**—"And [that which has died by] falling." The root ر د ي (r-d-y) gives us تَرَدَّى (taradda), meaning to fall from a height. An animal that fell off a cliff and died from the fall.

**وَٱلنَّطِيحَةُ**—"And [that killed by] the goring of horns." From the root ن ط ح (n-t-h), related to animals striking with horns. An animal that died because another animal—perhaps a moose or antelope—attacked it with horns or in a fight.

**وَمَآ أَكَلَ ٱلسَّبُعُ**—"And [that which] wild animals have eaten." When predators attack an animal and leave it for dead, or partially consume it, what remains becomes forbidden.

**إِلَّا مَا ذَكَّيْتُمْ**—"Unless you [are able to] slaughter [it before its death]." Here Allah ﷻ provides a crucial exception. The word ذَكَّى (dhakka) comes from the root د ك و (d-k-w), connected to purification. While ذَبْح (dhabh) is the general term for slaughter, تَذْكِيَة (tadhkiyah) specifically refers to slaughtering smoothly and mercifully, causing instant death without pain to the animal.

If you come upon an animal being attacked by a lion, already injured and nearly dead, but you manage to properly slaughter it before it dies from its injuries—even though the predator was just chewing on it—then that animal becomes lawful. The proper slaughter purifies it.

**وَمَا ذُبِحَ عَلَى ٱلنُّصُبِ**—"And what has been sacrificed on altars [to idols]." Animals slaughtered at pagan altars or sacred stones.

**وَأَن تَسْتَقْسِمُوا۟ بِٱلْأَزْلَـٰمِ**—"And that you seek decision through divining arrows." This refers to a pre-Islamic practice where people would pool slaughtered meat together, then use arrows as a lottery system to determine who received which portions. They would shoot arrows, and whoever's arrow landed a certain distance, or in a particular spot, would win specific shares of meat. This game of chance in dividing meat is explicitly forbidden.

**ذَٰلِكُمْ فِسْقٌ**—"All of that is nothing but corruption." The word فِسْق denotes grave disobedience and rebellion against divine command. These practices aren't merely cultural preferences—they represent spiritual corruption.

### The Turning Point of History

Now comes one of the most powerful declarations in the entire Quran:

**ٱلْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن دِينِكُمْ**—"This day those who disbelieve have despaired of [defeating] your religion."

The disbelievers have lost all hope. They've realized they cannot bring Islam down. They've lost hope that their way of life will triumph over Islam's message. They're completely depressed about the victory of monotheism and the defeat of their system.

But notice what follows immediately: these detailed dietary restrictions. The connection reveals the disbelievers' last hope. They've lost the big battle—Islam is victorious, the political and spiritual war is over. But they're hoping that small cultural remnants of pre-Islamic life can survive within the Muslim community.

"Come on, we can't even shoot arrows for fun anymore? We can't even do a little lottery with meat? That was such a beloved tradition. It's just a harmless game. Why can't we keep that?"

"There's a perfectly good animal that died naturally—why waste it? What harm does it do to eat it?"

These disbelievers understood something crucial: if they could keep these "small things" alive—these minor cultural practices that seem insignificant compared to major theological issues—then their way of life could gradually seep back into the Muslim community. Small compromises lead to larger ones. Cultural identity preserved in details can eventually reshape the whole.

Allah ﷻ is teaching us that nothing is too small when it comes to maintaining the purity of Islamic identity. These dietary laws aren't arbitrary restrictions—they're boundaries that protect the believers from slowly sliding back into pre-Islamic culture and mindset.

**فَلَا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَٱخْشَوْنِ**—"So fear them not, but fear Me." The disbelievers no longer pose an external threat, but internal compromise poses a far greater danger. Don't fear their mockery, their pressure, their claims that you're being too strict or too extreme. Fear Allah ﷻ alone and His displeasure at abandoning His boundaries.

### The Completion of Divine Favor

**ٱلْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِى وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمَ دِينًا**—"This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favor upon you and have approved for you Islam as religion."

This declaration contains two distinct concepts of completion, each with profound implications:

**أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ**—"I have perfected your religion." The verb أَكْمَلَ indicates finishing something so that it requires no additional materials or components from outside. Like a completed building that needs no more bricks, no more paint, no more electrical work—it's entirely finished, a complete product. Your religion now stands complete; no further revelation is needed, no additional prophets will come, no new laws will be required. Everything necessary for guidance has been provided.

**أَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِى**—"And completed My favor upon you." The verb أَتَمَّ carries a different nuance. When you work on a long-term project intended to produce specific benefits, and you finally complete it to the point where it's generating those intended benefits—that's itmam. There's a sense of satisfaction, of being impressed with the final result after so much effort.

The great scholar Ibn Abbas (RA) explained this beautifully: Your religion has been brought to completion. The long struggle to establish it as a force on earth has been ongoing, but now the project has reached its conclusion. Makkah is conquered, the great enemy has been defeated, shirk (polytheism) has been destroyed. Now you're beginning to see the benefits of tawheed (monotheism), the fruits of your struggle. You're witnessing filth being removed from society. This is your deen's itmam.

"And I have completed My favor upon you"—you need no other blessing from anywhere else now. Everything you need for your success and empowerment is at your disposal. There's no need to look outside for ingredients that will give you strength or guidance. My Quran provides everything you need.

**وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمَ دِينًا**—"And I have approved for you Islam as religion." The word دِين (deen) means far more than "religion" in the narrow sense. It encompasses a complete way of life, a comprehensive system for dealing with all matters. Allah ﷻ is expressing His pleasure—His رِضَا—with Islam as the complete way of life for humanity.

Notice the placement of this declaration right after dietary restrictions. Allah ﷻ is saying: "Your religion is now complete. All My favors have been perfected upon you. I'm protecting you even from filth in your diet, covering the smallest details for you. These restrictions aren't limitations—they're the completion of My favor. You should be grateful that you're no longer exposed to spiritual and physical impurity."

### Beyond Physical Health

We often try to explain Islamic dietary laws purely through scientific reasoning: "We slaughter animals properly to drain the blood because it's more sanitary." While such health benefits may exist, this approach comes at the expense of understanding the spiritual dimension of halal and haram.

There are spiritual consequences to consuming haram, just as there are spiritual benefits to consuming halal. When we eat good, pure food, it's not just our bodies being nourished—the blessings of halal sustenance feed our souls. Such food brings our hearts closer to Allah ﷻ. Our nafs (inner self) is strengthened through halal. Our qalb (heart) grows in spiritual strength and sensitivity.

Conversely, the spiritual harms of consuming haram manifest in observable ways: difficulty sleeping, inability to focus in salah, hardness of heart, distance from divine guidance. These spiritual consequences are real and will show themselves in various aspects of life, even if we can't measure them in a laboratory.

### The Exception for Necessity

**فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ فِى مَخْمَصَةٍ**—"But whoever is forced [by necessity] in extreme hunger..."

After establishing all these prohibitions, Allah ﷻ immediately provides the exception for genuine emergency situations. The word مَخْمَصَة is particularly specific. Arabic has multiple words for hunger, but makhmasah describes a prolonged period of starvation with no hope of finding food—the point of being near death from hunger.

Allah ﷻ didn't say, "Whoever gets hungry" (جُوع), because then everyone would head to the nearest restaurant offering haram food at the slightest hunger pang. No, this is about genuine starvation. You're lost in the desert, you haven't eaten for days, death is approaching, and an animal you normally couldn't eat appears before you—in that life-or-death moment, you may consume what's necessary to survive.

**غَيْرَ مُتَجَانِفٍ لِّإِثْمٍ**—"Without any inclination to sin." This phrase is crucial. Even in that desperate situation, you can't approach the forbidden food with curiosity or desire—"Finally, I get to find out what this tastes like!" No. There must be no bias toward sin, no interest in the forbidden beyond pure survival. You take only what's absolutely necessary to stay alive, with reluctance and necessity, not enthusiasm.

**فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ**—"Then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful."

This conclusion reminds us that Allah's mercy encompasses even these extreme situations. His forgiveness is readily available for those forced into impossible circumstances. But notice: the mercy comes after establishing clear boundaries and the proper attitude toward them. Allah ﷻ is not giving permission for casual compromise—He's acknowledging human limitations in truly dire circumstances while maintaining the sanctity of His prohibitions.

The structure of this ayah teaches us how to hold multiple truths simultaneously: divine law is absolute and non-negotiable, yet divine mercy accommodates genuine human need. The boundaries are real and must be respected, but Allah ﷻ is Rahman and Raheem—infinitely compassionate toward His servants' genuine struggles.

translation: They ask you, [O Muhammad], what has been made lawful for them. Say, "Lawful for you are [all] good foods and [game caught by] what you have trained of hunting animals which you train as Allah has taught you. So eat of what they catch for you, and mention the name of Allah upon it, and fear Allah." Indeed, Allah is swift in account
commentary:
يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَآ أُحِلَّ لَهُمْ ۖ قُلْ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتُ ۙ وَمَا عَلَّمْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْجَوَارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّآ أَمْسَكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ

The Companions (RA) asked the Prophet ﷺ what is halal for them after learning what is haram. Allah's ﷻ response is profound in its simplicity: "All good and pure things are halal for you." This answer reveals the mercy of Allah ﷻ. Rather than providing an exhaustive list of permissible items, He establishes a principle. If Allah ﷻ had listed specific halal items, we might have concluded that everything outside that list is haram. Instead, the door remains wide open: anything that is طَيِّبَات (tayyibat) - good and pure - is permissible.

There's a beautiful lesson embedded here. When something is halal, it means Allah ﷻ knows it is good for us. The category of halal reflects what is inherently beneficial. This understanding should guide our relationship with everything in life. Even if something isn't explicitly declared haram, if it's inherently harmful or impure, wisdom dictates that we stay away from it. The things Allah ﷻ has made accessible to us are the things that are truly good and pure for our wellbeing.

### Hunting Dogs and Human-Animal Relationships

The ayah then addresses a specific question about hunting: "And whatever you taught from hunting dogs..." The Arabic word الْجَوَارِحِ (al-jawarih) refers to hunting animals, particularly dogs trained to pursue and hold prey without killing it - injuring enough to slow down a rabbit or deer, for instance, but not delivering a fatal blow.

Notice the elegant use of language here. The ayah uses the feminine plural pronoun تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ (tu'allimūnahunna - "you teach them") instead of the standard pronoun for animals. This grammatical choice reflects something profound about human-animal relationships. When people develop close bonds with animals - when they spend time training them, working with them, caring for them - the Quran acknowledges this relationship by elevating the animals' status in the language used to describe them. If you have horses you've trained and developed a connection with, you can refer to them with pronouns typically reserved for higher beings. Someone who trains a dog naturally develops a friendship with that animal, and the Quran recognizes this bond.

Dogs are mentioned in the Quran. This bears emphasis because sometimes we become more restrictive than the Quran itself. Some people react with alarm at the mere sight of a dog, as if they need to perform ritual purification just because one passed by. This isn't from our religion. Allah ﷻ created these creatures. Even pigs, which we're forbidden to consume, are still creatures of Allah ﷻ. We don't harbor hatred toward them. The animal itself isn't the issue - we simply don't eat it. We don't have a vendetta against these creatures, and if Muslims ever held power, we wouldn't launch a campaign to eliminate pigs from the earth. The prohibition is about consumption, not about the creature's existence.

### The Gift of Teaching

The phrase مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ (mimma 'allamakumullah) - "from what Allah ﷻ taught you" - connects to something greater. Allah ﷻ taught human beings language, and we use that language to teach and train even animals. This is an extraordinary ability that Allah ﷻ has enabled in us. The animal goes out, catches the prey, and holds it without letting it escape until you arrive to slaughter it properly.

The command وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ (wadhkurus-mallahi 'alayhi) - "mention the name of Allah ﷻ over it" - refers to the moment of slaughter. Even in taking an animal's life for food, we acknowledge Allah's ﷻ name, recognizing that all sustenance comes from Him and that life itself belongs to Him.

The ayah concludes with وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ (wattaqullah) - "Be mindful of Allah ﷻ," followed by the reminder that إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (innallaha sari'ul-hisab) - "Allah ﷻ is swift in taking account." This serves as both encouragement and warning: maintain consciousness of Allah ﷻ in all your affairs, knowing that He is quick in reckoning.

translation: This day [all] good foods have been made lawful, and the food of those who were given the Scripture is lawful for you and your food is lawful for them. And [lawful in marriage are] chaste women from among the believers and chaste women from among those who were given the Scripture before you, when you have given them their due compensation, desiring chastity, not unlawful sexual intercourse or taking [secret] lovers. And whoever denies the faith - his work has become worthless, and he, in the Hereafter, will be among the losers
commentary:
ٱلْيَوْمَ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتُ ۖ وَطَعَامُ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ حِلٌّ لَّكُمْ وَطَعَامُكُمْ حِلٌّ لَّهُمْ ۖ وَٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتُ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتُ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ إِذَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحِينَ وَلَا مُتَّخِذِىٓ أَخْدَانٍ ۗ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُۥ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ

### "This Day" - A Moment of Transition

The phrase ٱلْيَوْمَ (al-yawma) - "This day" - opens this ayah with significance. Weren't all good and pure things always halal for the believers? Why does Allah ﷻ say "this day" specifically? This phrasing reveals at least two profound meanings.

First, it marks a clarity of understanding. Now the Muslims have explicit knowledge of what is impure and forbidden. The boundaries are clear, so what remains outside those boundaries is the vast expanse of what is pure and permissible.

Second, and perhaps more profound, is the context of the Companions' lives at this moment in history. The Muslims had been in a relentless struggle to establish Islam, to give this religion victory on earth. If you had asked a Companion in those early years why they were alive, the answer would have been simple and singular: to help establish this message, to support Muhammad ﷺ in his mission, to be able to say one day, "I am among those who are with the Messenger of Allah ﷻ."

When you're on such a mission, life takes on a different character. You don't have time to build elaborate homes, expand businesses, or pursue leisure activities that might otherwise be permissible. You don't take weekends off for horse racing or vacations. Your nights and days are devoted entirely to this cause. Until the mission is accomplished, everything else waits. This was the reality of the Companions (RA).

### From Cause to Constitution to Religion

This reality reveals something critical about how Islam has been understood across generations. For the Companions (RA), Islam wasn't primarily a religion in the way we think of religion today. It was first and foremost a cause - a mission with urgency, bounded by the limited time that the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ would be on earth. Allah ﷻ had charged him to bring Islam to victory, and they had limited time to help him achieve this. They saw Islam as a cause that demanded everything from them.

Later, after the Prophet's ﷺ time, Islam became a constitution - a framework for civilization. It evolved into a complete civilization that governed societies. Then, over time, it deteriorated from that state. Today, for most of us, Islam has become a religion in a more limited sense - a set of beliefs and practices we follow in our personal lives.

But among us, there are always those who see Islam again as a cause. They feel that urgency to educate, to revive, to spread the good of Islam to the world. They carry that sense that Islam demands something more than just personal piety. These people see their free time differently. Their vacations and relaxation take a back seat, not eliminated entirely, but repositioned. Their time becomes increasingly devoted to making Islam a bigger reality on earth, to serving this religion, to taking it forward.

### The Tension Between Religion and Cause

This creates a natural tension in Muslim communities. Some see Islam as a religion - fulfill your obligations, take care of your family, live a good life, and that's enough. This view isn't entirely wrong; it has validity. But it doesn't negate the reality that Islam was originally what? A cause. And there will always be some who rise from the Ummah and say there's more to do, more to accomplish. The light of Allah ﷻ still needs to be spread, institutions need to be built, education needs to reach people, and we each have a role to play.

Those who see Islam as a cause make sacrifices that others around them, who see it primarily as a religion, may not understand. "Why are you studying so much? Why are you always involved in this activity or that project? Why are you doing all this work - teaching, learning, establishing institutions, building organizations? You can relax. Fulfilling your basic religious obligations is enough."

Some Islamic movements in the past century presented the idea that unless you see Islam as a cause, you haven't truly understood Islam - you're somehow deficient in faith. These movements suggested that if you don't see Islam as requiring total commitment, you're a traitor to the mission. But this view oversimplifies things. The Quran itself says that a group from among the believers should dedicate themselves to calling to good, not that the entire Ummah must operate at that level of intensity at all times. We can't condemn the entire Ummah for not living with that urgency.

The balance is this: those among us who see that something more should be done, who recognize that Allah ﷻ has given them talents that can contribute to advancing the religion - they should do their best. But they shouldn't condemn others who aren't operating at the same level. This balance reflects the spirituality of the Companions (RA). They were driven by mission, yes, but they didn't demand that everyone exist in that same intense state at every moment.

### The Mindset of Mission

When you're on a mission, you're driven. You lose sleep. Even with a work project you're excited about, you lose sleep constantly thinking about it. You're in the zone. The Companions (RA) were in that zone continuously. They had something to accomplish, and they were determined to get it done.

Even after Allah ﷻ told them "this day all good and pure things are made permissible for you" - signaling that they could now relax somewhat - what happened next? The ayah immediately mentions: وَطَعَامُ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ (wa ta'amul-ladhina utul-kitab) - "the food of the People of the Book is lawful for you."

Where do the People of the Book live? In Roman territories, Persian territories, distant lands. This permission wasn't theoretical. It was preparation. You'll be out there in those territories soon, eating their food, spreading Islam to those regions. The mission wasn't over. The retirement plan never materialized. Instead, they took on the Roman Empire, the Persian Empire. They crossed oceans. The Companions (RA) reached South India, reached China. They were always asking: What else can we bring Islam to? What's on the other side of that ocean? They never let go of the idea that Islam is a cause meant to spread. That generation remains the greatest for this reason.

### The Context of Dietary Laws

When we read وَطَعَامُ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ حِلٌّ لَّكُمْ وَطَعَامُكُمْ حِلٌّ لَّهُمْ ("the food of those who were given the Scripture is lawful for you and your food is lawful for them"), what comes to our minds today? Is McDonald's halal? Is Burger King halal? Is the pizza halal? What about the chicken, what about the beef?

But in its original context, this ayah meant something different. It was telling the Companions (RA): Now you can worry about food because your mission is advancing. You'll be in their lands soon, and you can eat what they prepare. The conversation wasn't about restaurant chains and ingredient lists. It was about the expansion of Islam and the practical realities of living and spreading the message in distant territories.

### Do We Have a Cause?

This raises a question for us today. Do we feel - especially our youth, our upcoming generation of American Muslims - that we have a cause? Or does it feel like we lack a clear sense of direction? There's so much energy and enthusiasm. Young people run around organizing events, conferences, seminars, charitable drives. These are all good things. But it's crucial to have some kind of overarching goal. What is this all working toward? What's our vision?

Perhaps we can't have one grand unified vision for all Muslims. That may not be realistic. But we can have grand visions for the many causes within the larger cause of Islam. If there's a charitable organization, it should have a grand scheme. If people are making efforts toward Islamic education, they should have a comprehensive vision. How will we institutionalize this? How will we make this a permanent fixture in the world, something that outlives us?

### Planting Trees for Future Generations

Islamic projects should be like planting trees. When you plant a tree, you see very little progress for a long time. Your small sapling looks insignificant. But anyone who plants a tree knows that it will eventually grow, give fruit, and those fruits will produce seeds. Those seeds will return to the earth and new trees will emerge. This tree will benefit your generation, and after you're gone, it will benefit generations to come. You just have to start somewhere.

The Islamic causes we see around us now in their beginning stages - if they're started with sincerity and dedication to Allah ﷻ - only He knows how far they'll reach. Only Allah ﷻ knows what they'll become. Throughout history, some efforts started so small and humble, but Allah ﷻ gave them momentum. When He places barakah (blessing) in something, it can literally transform the world. This has happened again and again. Humble beginnings, sincere intentions, and divine blessing have combined to create movements that changed the course of human history.

This is what "this day all good things are made permissible for you" meant to that first generation. Yes, enjoy what Allah ﷻ has made halal. Yes, the food of the People of the Book is now accessible as you spread across the earth. But never forget: you're still on a mission. The cause continues. And for us today, the question remains: What is our cause? What are we building? What trees are we planting for the generations that will come after us?

translation: O you who have believed, when you rise to [perform] prayer, wash your faces and your forearms to the elbows and wipe over your heads and wash your feet to the ankles. And if you are in a state of janabah, then purify yourselves. But if you are ill or on a journey or one of you comes from the place of relieving himself or you have contacted women and do not find water, then seek clean earth and wipe over your faces and hands with it. Allah does not intend to make difficulty for you, but He intends to purify you and complete His favor upon you that you may be grateful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا قُمْتُمْ إِلَى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ فَٱغْسِلُوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْمَرَافِقِ وَٱمْسَحُوا۟ بِرُءُوسِكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْكَعْبَيْنِ ۚ وَإِن كُنتُمْ جُنُبًا فَٱطَّهَّرُوا۟ ۚ وَإِن كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ أَوْ جَآءَ أَحَدٌ مِّنكُم مِّنَ ٱلْغَآئِطِ أَوْ لَـٰمَسْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا۟ مَآءً فَتَيَمَّمُوا۟ صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا فَٱمْسَحُوا۟ بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُم مِّنْهُ ۚ مَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ حَرَجٍ وَلَـٰكِن يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ

"O you who believe, when you rise for prayer, wash your faces and your hands up to the elbows, and wipe over your heads and your feet to the ankles."

This ayah establishes the fundamentals of wudu (ablution). The word الْمَرَافِقِ (al-marafiq) refers to the elbows, derived from the root meaning to lean and relax upon something.

There's a scholarly discussion regarding the washing of feet based on different qira'at (readings) of this ayah. Some readings connect "feet" grammatically to "wipe," while others connect it to "wash." The Sunni tradition recognizes both readings, while some schools of thought maintain only one. The mention of "up to the ankles" strengthens the position that washing is intended, because if it were simply wiping, there would be no need to specify the extent - you would just wipe over them. But when washing, you wash up to a specific point, just as you wash the hands up to the elbows.

The ayah then addresses the state of major ritual impurity: وَإِن كُنتُمْ جُنُبًا فَٱطَّهَّرُوا۟ - "And if you are in a state of janabah (major ritual impurity), then purify yourselves thoroughly." This refers to taking ghusl (a full ritual bath) after marital relations or other circumstances that require it.

Allah ﷻ then provides accommodations for difficulty: وَإِن كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ أَوْ جَآءَ أَحَدٌ مِّنكُم مِّنَ ٱلْغَآئِطِ - "And if you are sick, or on a journey, or one of you comes from the gha'it (toilet)."

The word الْغَآئِطِ literally means "the ditch." In the time of revelation, people would relieve themselves in a lower area for privacy, then cover the filth. This is the Quranic euphemism for using the bathroom.

أَوْ لَـٰمَسْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ - "Or you have touched women" refers to intimate contact with one's spouse that would require renewal of wudu or ghusl.

فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا۟ مَآءً فَتَيَمَّمُوا۟ صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا - "And you cannot find water, then seek out pure earth."

The word تَيَمَّمُوا۟ (tayammamu) means to aim for something, to seek it out thoroughly. Look for clean earth or dust. فَٱمْسَحُوا۟ بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُم مِّنْهُ - "And wipe over your faces and your hands with it."

Now comes the profound wisdom behind these laws: مَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ حَرَجٍ - "Allah does not intend at all to place upon you any hardship."

وَلَـٰكِن يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ - "However, He intends to purify you." This is the first purpose: purification of both body and soul.

وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ - "And to complete His favor upon you." This is the second purpose. How does ritual purification complete Allah's ﷻ favor? When you make wudu, when you purify yourself, you are preparing to stand in Salah. And Salah itself is a manifestation of Allah ﷻ completing His favor upon you.

لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ - "So that you may be grateful."

This represents a complete reversal of how most people think. We often think: "I'm praying to Allah ﷻ, so Allah ﷻ should appreciate my effort." But Allah ﷻ is teaching us the opposite perspective: "You're praying to Me - you should appreciate that. You should thank Me that I made you from among the people who get to pray."

When you pray in congregation, when you make Salah, you shouldn't think "I've done Allah ﷻ a favor." Rather, you should be grateful that Allah ﷻ has honored you by making you from the people of prayer. This is the shift in consciousness that transforms our entire relationship with worship.

translation: And remember the favor of Allah upon you and His covenant with which He bound you when you said, "We hear and we obey"; and fear Allah. Indeed, Allah is Knowing of that within the breasts
commentary:
وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَمِيثَـٰقَهُ ٱلَّذِى وَاثَقَكُم بِهِۦٓ إِذْ قُلْتُمْ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ

"And remember the favor of Allah upon you and His covenant which He bound you with when you said, 'We hear and we obey.'"

When discussing the Bani Isra'il earlier in the Quran, Allah ﷻ said نَقَضُوا۟ مِيثَـٰقَهُمْ - "They violated their covenant." The emphasis there was on their possession of the covenant, highlighting their betrayal.

Here, when addressing the believers, Allah ﷻ says مِيثَـٰقَهُ - "His covenant," the one that belongs to Him, the one with which He bound you. The emphasis shifts to make us more conscious of Allah ﷻ Himself, more aware that this is His covenant, His claim upon us.

Notice the beautiful structure: وَمِيثَـٰقَهُ ٱلَّذِى وَاثَقَكُم بِهِۦٓ - "His covenant which He bound you with it." When did we make this covenant? إِذْ قُلْتُمْ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا - "When you said, 'We hear and we obey.'"

You might ask: when did I say that? The answer is that Allah ﷻ has made this declaration on your behalf. The moment these words come from your mouth - whether in shahada, in acceptance of Islamic teachings, or in any declaration of faith - you are bound. Once you've uttered "we hear and we obey," you are tied to a contract with Allah ﷻ.

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - "And be mindful of Allah, be fearful of Allah."

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ - "Indeed, Allah is fully knowledgeable of what is within the chests." He knows what's in your heart, what's in your innermost thoughts and intentions. You cannot hide anything from Him.

translation: O you who have believed, be persistently standing firm for Allah, witnesses in justice, and do not let the hatred of a people prevent you from being just. Be just; that is nearer to righteousness. And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is Acquainted with what you do
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُونُوا۟ قَوَّٰمِينَ لِلَّهِ شُهَدَآءَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَـَٔانُ قَوْمٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا۟ ۚ ٱعْدِلُوا۟ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ

This powerful ayah addresses a fundamental aspect of faith that distinguishes true believers. You'll notice a significant difference between the beginning of this surah and Surah An-Nisa. In An-Nisa, there was heavy emphasis on our relationships with people - inheritance laws, business dealings, family relations, rights of spouses. Here in Al-Maidah, we find more emphasis on our relationship with Allah ﷻ - dietary laws, prayer, purification. When we reach this ayah about justice, even though it concerns human interactions, notice how it's framed entirely in terms of our relationship with Allah ﷻ.

كُونُوا۟ قَوَّٰمِينَ لِلَّهِ - "Be people who stand firmly for Allah."

The word قَوَّٰمِينَ (qawwamin) is an intensive form, like other intensive patterns in Arabic. It doesn't just mean to stand once, but to stand repeatedly, persistently, consistently. Become people who stand up for justice, then stand up again, then stand up again, then stand up again. This is a continuous commitment, not a one-time action.

But here's what makes this profound: لِلَّهِ - "for Allah." Any decent human being says we should fight for justice. It's built into human nature - the fitrah that Allah ﷻ placed inside all of us. There are countless causes of justice that aren't necessarily led by Muslims: human rights, animal rights, economic rights, civil rights. The pursuit of justice is a human pursuit, pre-programmed inside all of humanity.

Allah ﷻ is saying that natural inclination is already there, but He wants you to pursue justice not merely because it's a human cause. Above and beyond that basic human instinct, pursue it for His sake. Your motive shouldn't only be justice itself - any human being would fight for that. You people are believers in Allah ﷻ, so you should stand up for justice for His sake.

شُهَدَآءَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ - "Witnesses to justice, witnesses with fairness."

وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَـَٔانُ قَوْمٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا۟ - "And do not let the hatred of a people prevent you from being just."

Remember the earlier ayah: "Stand up for justice even if it's against yourself or your family." That addressed one end of the spectrum - you could fail to be just because you're too biased toward someone you love. Now Allah ﷻ addresses the other end: you could also be unfair because you're too biased against someone.

Why mention this here specifically? Because of the historical context. Earlier in An-Nisa, the context was inheritance law and wealth distribution - situations where you might give preference to some over others out of love or family ties. Here, Muslims have just become victorious over their former enemies. When you're supposed to administer justice among people who were recently your enemies, what's more likely to interfere with fairness - love or hatred? Hatred.

شَنَـَٔانُ قَوْمٍ - "The animosity of a people," any people, any nation. Don't allow your hatred toward any group - no matter who they are or what they've done - to drive you away from justice.

ٱعْدِلُوا۟ - "Be just!" This is a command, emphatic and direct.

هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ - "It is closer to taqwa, closer to consciousness of Allah ﷻ."

Throughout the Quran, both in the Makkan and Madinan periods, Allah ﷻ consistently ties justice to being fearful of Him, to having taqwa. Why this connection? Because when we're truly fearful of Allah ﷻ, we know there's a Day of Judgment coming. We know there will be ultimate justice. And if we know there's a day of ultimate justice from Allah ﷻ, it makes us watch ourselves when we're tempted toward unfairness and injustice here on this earth. The awareness of divine accountability keeps us just.

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ - "And be mindful of Allah. Indeed, Allah is fully aware of what you do."

Notice the precise word choice at the end of this ayah: خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ - "Aware of what you do." In the Quran, you'll find different phrases used in different contexts. Sometimes Allah ﷻ says عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ - "Knowledgeable of what is in the hearts," and sometimes خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ - "Aware of what you do."

There are matters of the heart - intention, sincerity, iman, taqwa. When the context involves these internal states, Allah ﷻ typically mentions Himself first, then the internal matter: "Allah is knowledgeable of what is in the hearts."

But when the context is about actions - things you do with your hands, decisions you make, justice you establish - then the action is mentioned first: "Allah is aware of what you do." 

The message is clear: Be cautious in your actions on account of Allah ﷻ. He is watching what you do, and you will be held accountable for every act of justice or injustice you commit.

translation: Allah has promised those who believe and do righteous deeds [that] for them there is forgiveness and great reward
commentary:
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ۙ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ has promised those who believed and did righteous deeds that they will have forgiveness and a tremendous reward.

In the previous ayahs, Allah ﷻ addressed matters of justice and accountability. Now He shifts to discuss iman and righteous deeds. Notice how these two concepts appear together—they are inseparable. One is a manifestation of the other. True faith naturally produces good actions, and righteous deeds are evidence of genuine belief.

The promise here is specific: لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ (lahum maghfirah) - "for them is forgiveness." The pronoun "them" emphasizes exclusivity. This forgiveness and tremendous compensation belong uniquely to those who combine faith with action.

translation: But those who disbelieve and deny Our signs - those are the companions of Hellfire
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

And those who disbelieved and denied Our signs - those are the companions of Hellfire.

The contrast is immediate and stark. While the previous ayah described those who believed, this ayah addresses those who rejected faith entirely. The word كَذَّبُوا۟ (kadhdhabu) means they didn't just disbelieve—they actively lied against and rejected Allah's miraculous signs.

When Allah ﷻ mentions "Our signs" in this surah, we must remember the context. This passage appears after extensive discussion about justice and standing firm with truth. Those who fail to uphold justice, who lie against Allah's revelations, become أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (ashaab al-jaheem) - companions of the blazing fire. The word jaheem emphasizes the intensity of the blaze they will face.

translation: O you who have believed, remember the favor of Allah upon you when a people determined to extend their hands [in aggression] against you, but He withheld their hands from you; and fear Allah. And upon Allah let the believers rely
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ هَمَّ قَوْمٌ أَن يَبْسُطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فَكَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنكُمْ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ

O you who have believed, remember the favor of Allah upon you when a people determined to extend their hands against you, but He restrained their hands from you. And fear Allah, and upon Allah let the believers rely.

Allah ﷻ commands the believers to remember His favor during a time of extreme vulnerability. The phrase هَمَّ قَوْمٌ (hamma qawm) uses the verb hamma, which conveys intense desire and determination. An entire nation desperately wanted to reach out and destroy you—the image of يَبْسُطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ (yabsutu ilaykum aydiyahum) - "extend their hands toward you" suggests choking, strangling, complete annihilation.

This is a direct reference to the Battle of Khandaq (the Trench), when the Muslims faced a coalition of enemies who surrounded Medina. Only a ditch separated them from total destruction. Yet Allah ﷻ intervened: فَكَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنكُمْ (fakaffa aydiyahum ankum) - "He restrained their hands from you."

The imagery of hands is significant throughout this passage. In Surah An-Nisa, Allah ﷻ told the Muslims to restrain their own hands from fighting during the Makkan period. Now the tables have turned. After the Battle of Khandaq, the enemy's hands became tied. The Quraysh lost credibility with their allied tribes and could no longer mobilize armies effectively. The subsequent Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, though appearing disadvantageous to the Muslims at first, strategically tied the hands of the Quraysh even further. During the ceasefire period, Muslims established connections with multiple tribes, isolating the Quraysh politically and militarily.

The ayah concludes with two commands: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهِ (wattaqullah) - "be conscious of Allah," and وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (wa 'alallahi falyatawakkalil mu'minoon) - "and upon Allah exclusively should the believers place their trust." These aren't separate thoughts but deeply connected. The nation that wanted nothing more than to kill you has been neutralized by Allah's intervention. Therefore, maintain your consciousness of Him and trust Him completely.

translation: And Allah had already taken a covenant from the Children of Israel, and We delegated from among them twelve leaders. And Allah said, "I am with you. If you establish prayer and give zakah and believe in My messengers and support them and loan Allah a goodly loan, I will surely remove from you your misdeeds and admit you to gardens beneath which rivers flow. But whoever of you disbelieves after that has certainly strayed from the soundness of the way
commentary:
۞ وَلَقَدْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَبَعَثْنَا مِنْهُمُ ٱثْنَىْ عَشَرَ نَقِيبًا ۖ وَقَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مَعَكُمْ ۖ لَئِنْ أَقَمْتُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَيْتُمُ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَءَامَنتُم بِرُسُلِى وَعَزَّرْتُمُوهُمْ وَأَقْرَضْتُمُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا لَّأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَلَأُدْخِلَنَّكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ فَمَن كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ

And Allah had already taken a covenant from the Children of Israel, and We appointed from among them twelve leaders. And Allah said, "I am with you. If you establish prayer and give zakah and believe in My messengers and support them and loan Allah a goodly loan, I will surely remove from you your misdeeds and admit you to gardens beneath which rivers flow. But whoever of you disbelieves after that has certainly strayed from the soundness of the way."

Immediately after mentioning the Muslims' recent victory and safety from their enemies, Allah ﷻ shifts to discuss another nation that was granted victory before them. This is not a random transition—it's a warning wrapped in a historical lesson.

Allah ﷻ had taken a covenant (مِيثَـٰقَ - meethaq) from the Children of Israel. He divided them into twelve tribes, each with a نَقِيب (naqeeb) - a family head or leader. The structure mirrors what would happen to the Muslim Ummah as it grows and establishes itself across different territories, each with its own amir.

Then comes Allah's promise: إِنِّى مَعَكُمْ (innee ma'akum) - "I am with you." But this divine companionship comes with conditions:

First: لَئِنْ أَقَمْتُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ (la'in aqamtumu as-salah) - "if you establish the prayer." Not just perform it, but truly establish it with all its spiritual and social dimensions.

Second: وَءَاتَيْتُمُ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ (wa ataytumu az-zakah) - "and give zakah," the financial obligation that purifies wealth and supports the community.

Third: وَءَامَنتُم بِرُسُلِى (wa amantum birusulee) - "and believe in My messengers"—not just past messengers, but those who would come in the future as well.

Fourth: وَعَزَّرْتُمُوهُمْ (wa 'azzartumuhum) - "and support them." The word عَزَّرْ ('azzar) is particularly beautiful. While it means to help, it carries a special connotation of helping someone out of deep respect and honor. It's the kind of help where you grab your teacher's bag because you wouldn't want them to carry it, or when you take the groceries from your parents' hands out of reverence. By using this word, Allah ﷻ highlights that messengers aren't just people we believe in—they're individuals we should be driven to support out of profound respect.

Fifth: وَأَقْرَضْتُمُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا (wa aqradtumullaha qardan hasanan) - "and loan Allah a goodly loan"—spend in His cause with excellence and sincerity.

In return, Allah ﷻ makes a powerful triple oath: لَّأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَلَأُدْخِلَنَّكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ (la'ukaffiranna 'ankum sayyi'atikum wa la'udkhilannakum jannaat) - "I will surely, surely, surely remove your evil deeds and I will definitely, definitely, definitely enter you into gardens beneath which rivers flow." The emphasis through repetition shows the certainty and magnificence of this promise.

The ayah concludes with a warning: فَمَن كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (faman kafara ba'da dhalika minkum faqad dalla sawaa' as-sabeel) - "whoever disbelieves after that has gone far astray from the right path." The word كَفَرَ (kafara) here specifically means refusing these divine instructions—establishment of prayer, giving of zakah, showing respect to messengers. These weren't minor requests; they were the core of the covenant.

translation: So for their breaking of the covenant We cursed them and made their hearts hard. They distort words from their [proper] usages and have forgotten a portion of that of which they were reminded. And you will still observe deceit among them, except a few of them. But pardon them and overlook [their misdeeds]. Indeed, Allah loves the doers of good
commentary:
فَبِمَا نَقْضِهِم مِّيثَـٰقَهُمْ لَعَنَّـٰهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَا قُلُوبَهُمْ قَـٰسِيَةً ۖ يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِۦ ۙ وَنَسُوا۟ حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ وَلَا تَزَالُ تَطَّلِعُ عَلَىٰ خَآئِنَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنْهُمْ ۖ فَٱعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَٱصْفَحْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

So for their breaking of the covenant We cursed them and made their hearts hard. They distort words from their proper usages and have forgotten a portion of that of which they were reminded. And you will still observe deceit among them, except a few of them. But pardon them and overlook. Indeed, Allah loves the doers of good.

Notice the sharp contrast with Surah An-Nisa. In that surah, Allah ﷻ mentioned multiple violations by the Children of Israel—crime after crime after crime. Here, the focus is on one major transgression: فَبِمَا نَقْضِهِم مِّيثَـٰقَهُمْ (fabima naqdhihim meethaqahum) - "their violation of the covenant." Just this one violation brought severe consequences.

The punishment was twofold. First: لَعَنَّـٰهُمْ (la'annahum) - "We cursed them," removing them from divine mercy. Second: وَجَعَلْنَا قُلُوبَهُمْ قَـٰسِيَةً (wa ja'alna quloobahum qasiyah) - "We made their hearts hard." When the heart hardens, it becomes impenetrable to truth, sealed off from guidance.

The logical question arises: if multiple crimes in Surah An-Nisa brought certain punishments, shouldn't one crime bring less severe consequences? The answer reveals Allah's justice—even one violation of the covenant produced devastating results. This serves as a stark warning to the newly victorious Muslim Ummah: don't follow the same path.

The hardening of hearts led to further corruption. يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِۦ (yuharrifoona al-kalima 'an mawadi'ihi) - "they distort words from their proper places." They began changing Allah's words, moving them from their intended meanings and contexts.

Then comes something even more troubling: وَنَسُوا۟ حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ (wa nasu hazzam mimma dhukiru bihi) - "they forgot a portion of what they were reminded of." The word حَظ (hazz) doesn't just mean any portion—it refers to a good portion, something beneficial, like a good slice of cake or a fortunate share. In Arabic, when someone has great good fortune, they say لَهُ حَظٌّ عَظِيم (lahu hazzun 'azeem) - "he has a great portion."

They forgot an entire beneficial chunk of the Torah, and not just something mentioned once. The word ذُكِّرُوا۟ (dhukiru) indicates something they were reminded of repeatedly. What concept appears over and over throughout revelation? The Akhirah—the afterlife. The Quran reminds us of the Akhirah through direct descriptions of Judgment Day, through the names of Allah ﷻ, through stories of past nations. When Allah ﷻ says He is Al-Aziz (the Mighty), when will we see the full manifestation of His might? When He says He is As-Sami' (the All-Hearing), when will we understand the complete reality of His hearing? When He is Al-Ghafoor (the Forgiving), we taste some of His forgiveness now, but the full experience awaits the Hereafter. Even the divine names continuously point us toward the Akhirah.

Jewish theology, as it developed, eliminated the afterlife almost entirely. The focus became purely worldly—when the earth is free of gentiles, paradise will be here. This represents an astounding corruption: how do you remove the Akhirah from prophetic teachings when every prophet emphasized it? Every messenger was both a bearer of good news and a warner—good news about what? The Akhirah. Warnings about what? The Akhirah. The Bani Israel received so many prophets, which means they received countless reminders about the afterlife, yet they managed to eliminate it entirely.

The ayah continues: وَلَا تَزَالُ تَطَّلِعُ عَلَىٰ خَآئِنَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ (wa la tazalu tattali'u 'ala kha'inatin minhum) - "you will continue to discover treachery from them." This addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly. At this point in Islamic history, Muslims had a peace treaty with the Jewish tribes of Medina—a joint defense pact stating that if Medina was attacked, everyone would defend together. Yet some Jewish leaders were making secret agreements with the Quraysh, undermining the treaty.

Allah's guidance to the Prophet ﷺ is remarkable: فَٱعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَٱصْفَحْ (fa'fu 'anhum wasfah) - "pardon them and turn away." This is domestic policy wisdom. You have other pressing matters to deal with. Don't get consumed by their schemes. This advice remains relevant—yes, deception will continue, but your response should be to overlook it and focus on your mission.

The ayah ends with encouragement: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (innallaha yuhibbul-muhsineen) - "Indeed, Allah loves the doers of excellence." Following Allah's guidance in this matter, maintaining composure despite provocation, represents ihsan—excellence in character and trust in Allah ﷻ. When you practice ihsan, you earn Allah's love, and when Allah ﷻ loves you and takes care of you, what do you need to worry about?

translation: And from those who say, "We are Christians" We took their covenant; but they forgot a portion of that of which they were reminded. So We caused among them animosity and hatred until the Day of Resurrection. And Allah is going to inform them about what they used to do
commentary:
وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا نَصَـٰرَىٰٓ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَهُمْ فَنَسُوا۟ حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَأَغْرَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ ٱلْعَدَاوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ وَسَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ

And from those who say, "We are Christians" We took their covenant, but they forgot a portion of that of which they were reminded. So We caused among them animosity and hatred until the Day of Resurrection. And Allah is going to inform them about what they used to do.

The pattern continues with another community. وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا نَصَـٰرَىٰٓ (wa minal-ladheena qalu inna nasara) - "from those who said, 'We are Christians'"—literally, "we are the helpers," referring to those who claimed to be helpers and followers of Prophet Isa (AS).

أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَهُمْ (akhadhna meethaqahum) - "We took their covenant from them as well." Like the Children of Israel, they too received a covenant with clear terms and responsibilities.

فَنَسُوا۟ حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ (fanasu hazzam mimma dhukiru bihi) - "they also forgot a significant portion of what they were reminded of." The repetition of this exact phrase emphasizes the parallel between the two communities. Both were given prophets, both were reminded repeatedly of core truths, both forgot essential teachings.

Were the Christians not reminded of the Akhirah? Did they not receive teachings about who Allah ﷻ truly is? If there's one thing every messenger accomplishes, it's teaching people about Allah ﷻ. To claim allegiance to a messenger while forgetting the very first thing he came to teach—Tawheed, the oneness and nature of Allah ﷻ—represents a monumental failure.

But there's a third party that needs to examine itself: the Muslim Ummah. We too have forgotten a huge portion of what we've been taught. In casual conversation, the average Muslim has little idea what the Quran actually says. You'll hear people say, "The Quran talks about justice, so that means we should all be equal..." But can you show me where it says that? Can you find the ayah? Too often, Muslims engage in debates about Islam without any real knowledge of the Quran's actual content—not even the basics. We've forgotten much of what was given to us.

The consequence for the Christians was severe: فَأَغْرَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ ٱلْعَدَاوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (fa'aghrayna baynahumul-'adawata wal-baghda'a ila yawmil-qiyamah). The verb أَغْرَيْنَا (aghrayna) comes from the imagery of releasing hunting dogs. When hunters release hounds to chase prey, the dogs pursue relentlessly. Allah ﷻ released animosity and deeply rooted hatred between them like hunting dogs after prey—except here, the "dogs" are 'adawah (animosity) and baghda' (intense hatred), and they pursue the Christians themselves, causing them to hate each other until the Day of Resurrection.

The historical evidence is overwhelming. During the Spanish Inquisition, Christians tortured other Christians with unthinkable cruelty. They would tie people to beds and slowly pull their bodies apart, asking, "Have you converted to Judaism? Your ancestors were Jewish—you're hiding among us!" Even as the victim's shoulders dislocated and muscles tore, they continued, believing they were saving souls. The conflict between Protestants and Catholics produced similar violence. There are extensive historical records documenting the brutality within Judeo-Christian civilization—the kind of deep, merciless hatred communities developed for one another.

وَسَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ (wa sawfa yunabbi'uhumullahu bima kanu yasna'oon) - "Soon Allah will inform them thoroughly about what they used to manufacture." The word يَصْنَعُونَ (yasna'oon) is significant—it means to manufacture or fabricate. This includes not just the theological fabrications they created, but literally the devices of torture they manufactured during various inquisitions. The Roman and Spanish Inquisitions developed elaborate instruments specifically designed to inflict maximum suffering—devices difficult to process when you consider they were used on fellow human beings. Allah ﷻ will inform them completely of everything they manufactured, both ideologically and physically.

translation: O People of the Scripture, there has come to you Our Messenger making clear to you much of what you used to conceal of the Scripture and overlooking much. There has come to you from Allah a light and a clear Book
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا كُنتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَن كَثِيرٍ ۚ قَدْ جَآءَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ نُورٌ وَكِتَـٰبٌ مُّبِينٌ

O People of the Scripture, there has come to you Our Messenger making clear to you much of what you used to conceal of the Scripture and overlooking much. There has come to you from Allah a light and a clear Book.

This ayah feels like a continuation of the conversation from Surah An-Nisa, addressing the People of the Book directly. يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (ya ahlal-kitab) - "O People of the Scripture."

قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا (qad ja'akum rasuluna) - "Our Messenger has come to you." The possessive "Our" emphasizes that this messenger comes with divine authority and backing.

يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا كُنتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (yubayyinu lakum katheeran mimma kuntum tukhfuna minal-kitab) - "clarifying for you much of what you used to hide from the Scripture." The Prophet ﷺ exposes many of the concealments and distortions introduced into previous scriptures.

A powerful historical example illustrates this. Western scholars have studied Islam for centuries. As early as the 1500s and 1600s, the Catholic Church commissioned Islamic studies, sending ministers to document Islam for missionary purposes. They created encyclopedias cataloging what they perceived as contradictions in Islamic teachings, which they called "the mistakes of Muhammad."

One classical "mistake" they identified involved Fir'awn (Pharaoh) and Haman. In the Quran, Fir'awn tells Haman to build him a tower reaching the sky so he can confront the God of Musa (AS). Biblical scholars said, "We have detailed Jewish history in the Old Testament, and there's no record of any Haman working for any Pharaoh. However, we do have a tower story—the Tower of Babel—but that's in the Book of Esther, about a completely different empire 1,400-2,000 years later. In the Babylonian Empire in Iraq, King Xerxes had a minister named Haman who built a tower. Muhammad must have stolen from Jewish and Christian sources and mixed up his stories."

In the late 1800s, Maurice Bucaille, before becoming interested in science, studied comparative religion. He investigated this criticism carefully. First, he examined the Book of Esther and found that Jewish scholars themselves stated in their own commentaries that this book has no historical value—it's fables and made-up stories meant to teach moral lessons. So the criticism amounted to: "The Quran is historically inaccurate because it doesn't match something that's already inaccurate according to your own sources."

But Bucaille went further. He turned to Egyptian sources. The ancient Egyptians documented their kingdoms in hieroglyphics, but the Egyptian language had been dead for 2,000-3,000 years. In the 1850s and 1860s, the French began reviving Egyptology and developed methods to translate hieroglyphic records. They discovered that many hieroglyphic inscriptions on walls were actually administrative records documenting who worked in the government ministries.

Bucaille traveled to Austria, where these records were kept, and asked the leading French Egyptologist, "I'm looking for someone named Haman who worked for Pharaoh during the historical era of Moses (AS)." The scholar replied, "Where did you get this name?" Bucaille answered, "It's in the Quran." The Egyptologist was shocked: "That's 2,600 years later! Why would you look for this name there?" Bucaille insisted, "Just look for it."

They found in the records from the Pharaoh of Moses's era a minister named Haman whose title was "Minister of Stone Construction." This doesn't definitively prove it's the same person the Quran mentions, but it provides remarkable historical corroboration that Allah ﷻ is clarifying what they had hidden or forgotten.

وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَن كَثِيرٍ (wa ya'fu 'an katheer) - "and He pardons much." The Prophet ﷺ doesn't expose all their lies and corruptions—just enough to establish truth. He overlooks many other distortions out of mercy.

قَدْ جَآءَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ نُورٌ (qad ja'akum minallahi noor) - "There has come to you from Allah a light." Imagine trying to read a book in complete darkness—you can't see anything. Now light arrives, and suddenly you can read clearly. That light is the Quran. It illuminates what was always there but couldn't be seen properly.

Some scholars say نُورٌ (noor) refers to the Prophet ﷺ himself, because one of his titles is Siraj—a lamp that gives light. In the sense that he delivers guidance and serves as a source of teaching, he can be called a light. However, we must be careful not to take this interpretation too far into directions that go beyond what the text intends.

وَكِتَـٰبٌ مُّبِينٌ (wa kitabun mubeen) - "and a clear Book." The Quran is مُّبِين (mubeen)—clear, manifest, making things plain. This Book doesn't require layers of interpretation to access its basic guidance. It clarifies, it illuminates, it makes truth evident for anyone willing to see.

translation: By which Allah guides those who pursue His pleasure to the ways of peace and brings them out from darknesses into the light, by His permission, and guides them to a straight path
commentary:
**يَهْدِى بِهِ ٱللَّهُ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ رِضْوَٰنَهُۥ سُبُلَ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ وَيُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِۦ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ**

Allah ﷻ guides by means of the Quran—specifically by means of this Book—whoever would follow His pleasure. The phrase مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ رِضْوَٰنَهُۥ (man ittaba' ridwanahu) captures a beautiful reality: whoever pursues Allah's pleasure will find guidance. This ayah teaches us that every person will have their own unique journey toward Islam. Allah ﷻ doesn't expect complex prerequisites from human beings. The one fundamental requirement is sincere intention: "I just want to please God."

When a person solemnly makes that decision, Allah ﷻ will choose one road for one person, another for another, and yet another for someone else. These are the سُبُلَ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ (subul as-salam)—pathways to peace, pathways to safety. All of these different routes lead them to the same destination: a place where they will be safe.

The ayah continues with a powerful image: وَيُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ (He will bring them out from various shades of darkness into the light). Notice the plural "darknesses" contrasted with the singular "light." People may have been in different types of darkness—ignorance, confusion, spiritual emptiness—but by means of these pathways, Allah ﷻ extracts them and brings them to one unified light. This happens بِإِذْنِهِۦ (bi-idhnihi)—by His permission alone.

The ayah concludes: وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (and He will guide them to a straight path). This is remarkable: Allah ﷻ doesn't merely rescue people from darkness.
