Once they emerge into the light, He then shows them where to go next—to a straight path. This beautiful imagery captures the complete nature of divine guidance: extraction from error, illumination with truth, and then direction toward a clear way forward.

translation: They have certainly disbelieved who say that Allah is Christ, the son of Mary. Say, "Then who could prevent Allah at all if He had intended to destroy Christ, the son of Mary, or his mother or everyone on the earth?" And to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them. He creates what He wills, and Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
**لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۚ قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا إِنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُهْلِكَ ٱلْمَسِيحَ ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُۥ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ۗ وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۚ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ**

The ayah opens with emphatic certainty: لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ (laqad kafara)—no doubt about it, I swear to it. Those who said that Allah ﷻ is the Messiah, son of Maryam, have disbelieved. This addresses a theological claim that exists in various forms: that Jesus (AS) is the son of God, or that Jesus (AS) is Lord, or the belief that God Himself came in the form of a human being. The concept appears in different traditions—the idea of God taking the shape of a human being, descending in human form.

The severity of this claim becomes clear in how Allah ﷻ responds. He asks: فَمَن يَمْلِكُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا (Who is going to have any authority against Allah in any way, shape or form? Who could make a complaint?) إِنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُهْلِكَ ٱلْمَسِيحَ ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ (If He decides to destroy the Messiah, son of Maryam). 

Notice the powerful rhetorical shift. In the previous passage about Isa (AS), Allah ﷻ stated that they didn't kill him at all. Now He asks: who could stop Me if I decided to destroy him? The verb used here—يُهْلِكَ (yuhlika)—implies not just death, but violent destruction. Who could prevent this if Allah ﷻ willed it? Not only that, but وَأُمَّهُۥ (and his mother), وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا (and whoever is on the earth altogether).

This statement is so offensive to Allah ﷻ that He essentially says: Who would come after Me if I decided to destroy all of you for making this attribution? The answer is obvious: no one. وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (To Allah alone belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth). The use of "kingdom" here is particularly pointed, since they call Isa (AS) "King"—King Jesus, Lord Jesus. Allah ﷻ corrects this: He is the King of the heavens and the earth, وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا (and whatever is between them).

The ayah concludes with two decisive statements. First: يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ (He creates whatever He wants). Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't even use the pronoun "man" (whoever), but "ma" (whatever)—emphasizing that Isa (AS) is just a creation. Second: وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (Allah has complete control over all things). There is no negotiating with this reality.

translation: But the Jews and the Christians say, "We are the children of Allah and His beloved." Say, "Then why does He punish you for your sins?" Rather, you are human beings from among those He has created. He forgives whom He wills, and He punishes whom He wills. And to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, and to Him is the [final] destination
commentary:
**وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ نَحْنُ أَبْنَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱللَّهِ وَأَحِبَّـٰٓؤُهُۥ ۚ قُلْ فَلِمَ يُعَذِّبُكُم بِذُنُوبِكُم ۖ بَلْ أَنتُم بَشَرٌ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقَ ۚ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ**

Both the Jews and Christians claimed: نَحْنُ أَبْنَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱللَّهِ وَأَحِبَّـٰٓؤُهُۥ (We are the children of God and His beloved ones). This claim of special status suggests immunity from divine judgment. The response Allah ﷻ commands His messenger ﷺ to give is devastating in its simplicity: فَلِمَ يُعَذِّبُكُم بِذُنُوبِكُم (Then why does He punish you for your sins?).

Notice the verb tense here encompasses past, present and future: Why did He punish you? Why does He punish you now? Why will He punish you on the Day of Judgment? If you truly held this privileged status, how could punishment even be a possibility? The rhetorical question exposes the absurdity of the claim.

Allah ﷻ then states the truth plainly: بَلْ أَنتُم بَشَرٌ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقَ (Rather, you are nothing but mortal beings from among those He created). You're just human. You're nothing but flesh—mortal beings created like all the other species He created. No special contracts, no divine obligations toward you beyond what He chooses.

The ayah continues: يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ (He forgives whoever He wants, and He punishes whoever He wants). They claimed immunity based on being "God's children," as if God couldn't possibly punish His own children. Allah ﷻ responds: I can punish whoever I want. You're not My children in the sense you imagine. You can't make claims on Me as if there are laws binding Me to provide for you. You're creation, and I can do whatever I want with you.

Consider the relationship between an owner and his property. When someone owns livestock and the animal stops being productive, no one questions their right to slaughter it. When your phone breaks, you can dispose of it as you wish—it's your property. You can get eggs from your chickens or make chicken soup; you can milk your cow or slaughter it. Why? Because it's your property. It has no rights to claim against you. Similarly, Allah ﷻ is making clear: You belong to Me as My creation. You cannot bind Me with obligations based on false claims of special status.

The ayah concludes by reaffirming: وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا (To Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them), وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (and to Him is the final return).

translation: O People of the Scripture, there has come to you Our Messenger to make clear to you [the religion] after a period [of suspension] of messengers, lest you say, "There came not to us any bringer of good tidings or a warner." But there has come to you a bringer of good tidings and a warner. And Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
**يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلَىٰ فَتْرَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ مَا جَآءَنَا مِنۢ بَشِيرٍ وَلَا نَذِيرٍ ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بَشِيرٌ وَنَذِيرٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ**

Allah ﷻ addresses the People of the Book directly: يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (O People of the Book), قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا (Our messenger has already come to you). He is clarifying for all of you يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ—making things clear, removing confusion.

This comes عَلَىٰ فَتْرَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ (after a fatra from the messengers). The word فَتْرَةٍ (fatra) literally means a period of weakness or dwindling. Messengers had stopped coming for a long time—a gap of over 600 years since the last messenger. From Isa (AS) all the way to Muhammad ﷺ, no prophets had been sent. This was a long interval without divine guidance through prophethood.

Allah ﷻ explains the purpose behind sending this final messenger: أَن تَقُولُوا۟ مَا جَآءَنَا مِنۢ بَشِيرٍ وَلَا نَذِيرٍ (lest you say, "No giver of good news came to us, nor any warner"). This removes any possible excuse. You might have claimed you didn't have guidance, that no warner reached you, that you didn't know better because no messenger came. That excuse is now eliminated.

The ayah then delivers its decisive conclusion: فَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بَشِيرٌ وَنَذِيرٌ (Now a giver of good news and a warner has come to you). Notice the order: بَشِيرٌ (bearer of good news) comes first, then نَذِيرٌ (warner). The implication is powerful. By mentioning the good news first, Allah ﷻ is indicating: He came to you with good news first. You didn't take advantage of it. That's why the message is now becoming harsh, why the tone shifts to warning. The good news alone wasn't sufficient for you to respond.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (Allah has complete control over all things). He had the power to leave you without guidance in that gap between messengers, and He has the power to send you this final messenger to complete His argument against you.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when Moses said to his people, "O my people, remember the favor of Allah upon you when He appointed among you prophets and made you possessors and gave you that which He had not given anyone among the worlds
commentary:
**وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ جَعَلَ فِيكُمْ أَنۢبِيَآءَ وَجَعَلَكُم مُّلُوكًا وَءَاتَىٰكُم مَّا لَمْ يُؤْتِ أَحَدًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ**

The narrative now shifts to a powerful historical account. Musa (AS) addresses his people: يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ (O my people, remember the favor of Allah upon you). Before asking them to fulfill a difficult command, he reminds them of what they've been given.

First: إِذْ جَعَلَ فِيكُمْ أَنۢبِيَآءَ (when He made among you prophets). You are descendants of prophets. Yaqub (AS) was made a prophet. Yusuf (AS) was made a prophet. You come from a great, noble ancestry—a lineage of prophetic guidance.

Second: وَجَعَلَكُم مُّلُوكًا (and He made you kings). This doesn't mean they were all literal monarchs in the conventional sense. Rather, when the family of Yaqub (AS)—the original twelve sons—first left their homeland and moved to Egypt with Yusuf (AS), they arrived in a position of privilege. Yusuf (AS) was a minister, and his family received excellent positions and opportunities. As generations passed, the Israelites became deeply entrenched in the Egyptian government and ministry. They rose to positions of significant authority.

This elevated status is what made their later persecution so severe. When a new dynasty conquered Egypt and displaced the previous ruling class, what typically happens? The new rulers target the elite class of the former regime. Since the Israelites held special high status under the previous kingdom, the new Pharaonic dynasty made it a point to especially enslave and humiliate them. They went from positions of authority to the depths of slavery.

But before that dark period, Allah ﷻ had blessed them abundantly, which is why Musa (AS) says: وَءَاتَىٰكُم مَّا لَمْ يُؤْتِ أَحَدًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (And He gave you what He had not given to anyone from among the worlds). This many blessings, this much guidance, this much opportunity—nations before you never received what you received. Remember this before you face what comes next.

translation: O my people, enter the Holy Land which Allah has assigned to you and do not turn back [from fighting in Allah 's cause] and [thus] become losers
commentary:
**يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ ٱلْمُقَدَّسَةَ ٱلَّتِى كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَرْتَدُّوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَارِكُمْ فَتَنقَلِبُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ**

After reminding them of their blessings, Musa (AS) delivers the command: يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ ٱلْمُقَدَّسَةَ (O my people, enter the sacred land). This refers to Jerusalem, though some scholars mention this could also include Jericho. The key phrase is: ٱلَّتِى كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ (which Allah has written for you).

Some scholars understand this as returning to their ancestral homeland—the place they lived before they migrated to Egypt. This is the land that has been designated for you, written for you by divine decree.

The command comes with an urgent warning: وَلَا تَرْتَدُّوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَارِكُمْ (and do not turn back on your heels). Don't beat a retreat. Don't run back. Don't turn around. فَتَنقَلِبُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (or you will become losers). 

The implication is severe: if you miss this opportunity, you will never get it back. This is your moment. Move forward, or lose everything. The window for this divine gift is now, and hesitation means permanent loss.

translation: They said, "O Moses, indeed within it is a people of tyrannical strength, and indeed, we will never enter it until they leave it; but if they leave it, then we will enter
commentary:
**قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّ فِيهَا قَوْمًا جَبَّارِينَ وَإِنَّا لَن نَّدْخُلَهَا حَتَّىٰ يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا فَإِن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّا دَٰخِلُونَ**

Their response reveals the depth of their spiritual weakness: يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّ فِيهَا قَوْمًا جَبَّارِينَ (O Musa, indeed there are in it a people of tyrannical strength). The word جَبَّارِينَ (jabbarin) doesn't just mean "strong" or "giants." A جَبَّار (jabbar) is someone who, when fighting, shows no mercy. He goes all out. These are tyrannical, overwhelming warriors.

They continue: "You're asking us to go in there? Have you seen how tall those people are? We can see them from here! Those guys are huge!" Their fear overwhelms their faith. They present their counteroffer: وَإِنَّا لَن نَّدْخُلَهَا حَتَّىٰ يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا (We will never enter it until they leave from it).

Then they add, almost generously: فَإِن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّا دَٰخِلُونَ (But if they leave from it, then we will be ones who enter). In other words: "Let's just wait here. Eventually they'll walk away, and then we can take over their homes. That would be much better. We're totally with you—just after they leave."

This response exposes how pampered they had become. When you're used to miraculous intervention at every turn, you lose your capacity for struggle. They had seen the sea part before them. Manna descended from the sky when they were hungry. Water gushed from a rock when they were thirsty. Quails appeared for meat in the middle of the desert. They became accustomed to being taken care of, to having everything provided without effort.

When people become too comfortable, when everything is done for them, they become spiritually lazy. They expect solutions to appear without any exertion on their part. "Can't Allah just make them leave? He hooked us up before. Can't He do it again?"

translation: Said two men from those who feared [to disobey] upon whom Allah had bestowed favor, "Enter upon them through the gate, for when you have entered it, you will be predominant. And upon Allah rely, if you should be believers
commentary:
**قَالَ رَجُلَانِ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمَا ٱدْخُلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْبَابَ فَإِذَا دَخَلْتُمُوهُ فَإِنَّكُمْ غَـٰلِبُونَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَتَوَكَّلُوٓا۟ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ**

In the midst of this collective cowardice, two men spoke up: قَالَ رَجُلَانِ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ (Two men from among those who feared). The key word here is يَخَافُونَ (yakhafoona)—they feared. But whom did they fear? Not the جَبَّارِينَ (tyrants), but Allah ﷻ. These were men who had proper fear—fear of their Lord, not fear of creation.

Allah ﷻ describes them further: أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمَا (Allah had bestowed His favor upon both of them). Why mention this? Because they spoke up. The phrase مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ suggests there were others who truly feared Allah ﷻ as well, but they hadn't yet found their voice. Perhaps they were shell-shocked by the level of absurdity displayed by their fellow Israelites and couldn't immediately respond.

But these two men did speak, and their counsel was simple and direct: ٱدْخُلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْبَابَ (Enter upon them through the gate). Don't try to sneak in by scaling walls. Don't look for a back entrance. Charge them through the front gate. You have the numbers—there were between 50,000 and 100,000 fighting men among them, some estimates suggest as many as 600,000 had made the exodus from Egypt. This is an overwhelming force. Storm the city gates directly.

They continue: فَإِذَا دَخَلْتُمُوهُ فَإِنَّكُمْ غَـٰلِبُونَ (And when you enter it, you will surely be victorious). Notice the certainty: "when" you enter, not "if." Victory is assured—but only if you actually enter.

Then comes the key to everything: وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَتَوَكَّلُوٓا۟ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (And upon Allah, so place your trust, if you are believers). What's wrong with you? Put your reliance on Allah ﷻ. Tawakkul—complete trust and dependence on Allah ﷻ—is inseparable from true faith. If you claim to be believers, then act like it.

translation: They said, "O Moses, indeed we will not enter it, ever, as long as they are within it; so go, you and your Lord, and fight. Indeed, we are remaining right here
commentary:
**قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّا لَن نَّدْخُلَهَآ أَبَدًا مَّا دَامُوا۟ فِيهَا ۖ فَٱذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَرَبُّكَ فَقَـٰتِلَآ إِنَّا هَـٰهُنَا قَـٰعِدُونَ**

The people completely ignore the counsel of those two righteous men. They don't even acknowledge them. Instead, they turn back to Musa (AS)—notice they don't even say يَا رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ (O Messenger of Allah). Just يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ (O Musa), as if he were merely one of them, stripped of his prophetic authority.

Their response is even more defiant than before: إِنَّا لَن نَّدْخُلَهَآ أَبَدًا مَّا دَامُوا۟ فِيهَا (We will never enter it, ever, so long as they are in it). The word أَبَدًا (abadan) means absolutely never—there is no possibility, zero chance. "We're not interested in your motivational speeches. We're not interested in what those two guys said. Thank you for that input, but we're not going."

Then comes one of the most shocking statements in the Quran: فَٱذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَرَبُّكَ فَقَـٰتِلَآ (So you and your Lord go and fight). The audacity of this statement is staggering. "You go. And take your Master with you." Some scholars note you could even add here: "Take your staff too—it's worked well for you before. Maybe you can just wave it and take care of business."

The ayah concludes with their final, unmovable position: إِنَّا هَـٰهُنَا قَـٰعِدُونَ (We are right here, sitting). هَـٰهُنَا (hahuna) means right here—not moving one step, not one inch. And قَـٰعِدُونَ (qa'idoon) is different from جَالِسُونَ (jalisoon). جَالِسُونَ means sitting temporarily; قَـٰعِدُونَ means stationed, entrenched, long-term. "We are seated, stationed, and this conversation is over. We're not moving."

translation: [Moses] said, "My Lord, indeed I do not possess except myself and my brother, so part us from the defiantly disobedient people
commentary:
**قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى لَآ أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِى وَأَخِى ۖ فَٱفْرُقْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ**

This is the same Musa (AS) who helped them cross the sea. The same prophet who gave them reminder after reminder. They had already worshiped the calf and nearly been annihilated entirely. They had made their share of catastrophic mistakes. But this response—this was the straw that broke the camel's back.

Musa (AS) doesn't even address them anymore. He turns directly to Allah ﷻ, because Allah ﷻ appointed him over them. His resignation is complete: قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى لَآ أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِى وَأَخِى (He said: My Master, I have no authority except over myself and my brother). "My brother I can grab and take with me. My brother will listen to me. But that's it. That's all the authority I have."

Then comes his dua—a prayer of separation: فَٱفْرُقْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (So cause a separation between us and the corrupt nation). The word فَٱفْرُقْ (fafraq) comes from the same root as "divorce"—make a complete break between us. "I want nothing to do with these people anymore. Separate me from this corrupt nation."

translation: [Allah] said, "Then indeed, it is forbidden to them for forty years [in which] they will wander throughout the land. So do not grieve over the defiantly disobedient people
commentary:
**قَالَ فَإِنَّهَا مُحَرَّمَةٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ۛ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً ۛ يَتِيهُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ فَلَا تَأْسَ عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ**

Allah ﷻ responds immediately: قَالَ فَإِنَّهَا مُحَرَّمَةٌ عَلَيْهِمْ (He said: Then it will be forbidden to them). The land they refused to enter will now be مُحَرَّمَةٌ (muharramah)—absolutely forbidden, completely prohibited, thoroughly deprived from them.

For how long? أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً (Forty years). This is what's known as the forty years of diaspora, the forty years of wandering. يَتِيهُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (They will wander aimlessly in the earth)—lost in the desert, searching for water, searching for anything, just wandering without direction for four decades.

It was during this period that Musa (AS) himself passed away. Allah ﷻ fulfilled his dua and separated him from them through death. Harun (AS) also died during this time. So the Israelites were left without these great prophets, wandering in the wilderness for forty years as a consequence of their refusal to trust Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes with a command to Musa (AS), and by extension to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ: فَلَا تَأْسَ عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (So do not grieve over the corrupt nation). "Don't be sad about them anymore, Musa. Your dua has been accepted. You have nothing to do with them. You're done. There's no reason you should worry about them."

This same message applies to the Prophet ﷺ and the believers after the victory at Badr. Yes, victory has happened. But that doesn't mean corruption and hypocrisy are dead. Don't worry about the corrupt people. Don't grieve over those who refuse guidance despite seeing the truth. Your job is to convey the message and trust in Allah's decree. The rest is up to Him.

translation: And recite to them the story of Adam's two sons, in truth, when they both offered a sacrifice [to Allah], and it was accepted from one of them but was not accepted from the other. Said [the latter], "I will surely kill you." Said [the former], "Indeed, Allah only accepts from the righteous [who fear Him]
commentary:
۞ وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ٱبْنَىْ ءَادَمَ بِٱلْحَقِّ إِذْ قَرَّبَا قُرْبَانًا فَتُقُبِّلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِمَا وَلَمْ يُتَقَبَّلْ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخَرِ قَالَ لَأَقْتُلَنَّكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنَّمَا يَتَقَبَّلُ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ: "Recite to them the story of the two sons of Adam (AS) with truth." The phrase بِٱلْحَقِّ (bil-haqq) carries two meanings. First, it means to tell the story truthfully, without embellishment or distortion. Second, it indicates that this story is being told with purpose—every listener should understand there is a reason they are hearing this account.

This is the story of Habil and Qabil, the two sons of Adam (AS). The word قُرْبَان (qurban), meaning sacrifice or offering, comes from the root قَرَبَ (qaraba), which means to come near or to approach. A qurban is not merely an act of ritual sacrifice; it represents an attempt to draw closer to Allah ﷻ. The sacrifice itself is a means of seeking nearness to the Creator.

The ayah tells us: "When both of them offered a sacrifice, it was accepted from one of them and not accepted from the other." In the time of Adam's sons, when Allah ﷻ accepted a sacrifice, fire would descend from the heavens and consume the offering. This was the visible sign of divine acceptance. So both brothers made their offerings, and one watched as fire took his sacrifice, while the other waited—and nothing came. The rejection was clear and public.

What follows is a psychological unraveling rooted in jealousy and low self-esteem. When people struggle with inadequacy, they often look outward rather than inward. They fixate on what others have: Why is he taller? Why does he look better? Why does everyone prefer him? Why am I not the favored one? This comparative mindset breeds resentment that can grow into something dangerous.

We have seen this pattern before in the Quran—in the story of Yusuf (AS) and his brothers, where jealousy led to attempted murder. We see it in the very beginning of creation, when Iblis refused to prostrate to Adam (AS) out of jealous pride. This kind of envy can lead a person down a dark path, ultimately resulting in behavior they never imagined themselves capable of.

The rejected brother's jealousy festered until he declared: "I will surely kill you." The response from the righteous brother is immediate and profound: إِنَّمَا يَتَقَبَّلُ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ—"Allah only accepts from those who have taqwa." This statement cuts to the heart of the matter. The problem was not favoritism or unfairness from Allah ﷻ. The issue was the spiritual state of the person making the offering. Allah ﷻ accepts deeds based on the consciousness of Him that accompanies those deeds, not merely on the external action itself.

---

translation: If you should raise your hand against me to kill me - I shall not raise my hand against you to kill you. Indeed, I fear Allah, Lord of the worlds
commentary:
لَئِنۢ بَسَطتَ إِلَىَّ يَدَكَ لِتَقْتُلَنِى مَآ أَنَا۠ بِبَاسِطٍ يَدِىَ إِلَيْكَ لِأَقْتُلَكَ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

The righteous brother responds to the death threat with remarkable moral clarity: "If you extend your hand to kill me, I will not extend my hand to kill you. Indeed, I fear Allah, Lord of all the worlds."

This statement reveals several profound principles. First, it demonstrates that self-defense, while permitted, is not always the path chosen by the most God-conscious individuals. Habil refuses to engage in combat even when his life is threatened. He will not become a murderer, even to save himself from being murdered.

Second, his reasoning is explicit: إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ—"I fear Allah, Lord of all the worlds." His fear of Allah ﷻ is not abstract or philosophical; it governs his behavior in the most extreme circumstances. Even faced with imminent death, his consciousness of Allah ﷻ prevents him from crossing the boundary into taking another life.

Notice how he addresses Allah ﷻ as رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ—the Master and Nurturer of all nations and peoples. This is not just about personal piety; it is a recognition that all human beings belong to Allah ﷻ, and taking a life is an assault on His creation.

---

translation: Indeed I want you to obtain [thereby] my sin and your sin so you will be among the companions of the Fire. And that is the recompense of wrongdoers
commentary:
إِنِّىٓ أُرِيدُ أَن تَبُوٓأَ بِإِثْمِى وَإِثْمِكَ فَتَكُونَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Habil continues with a stark warning: "I want you to bear my sin and your sin, and thus be among the inhabitants of the Fire. That is the recompense of the wrongdoers."

This is not a plea for mercy or an attempt to appeal to his brother's better nature. It is a direct, unflinching statement of consequence. If Qabil murders him, he will not only carry the sin of murder but will also inherit the burden of the sins Habil himself committed. The murdered person's sins are transferred to their unjust killer—a frightening spiritual reality.

Some might expect Habil to soften his words, to say something compassionate as he faces death. But the reality of the moment is harsh, and his words reflect that harshness. He does not say, "I will pray for you, brother." He says, in effect: "Go ahead and kill me. You will burn in Hell for this."

This blunt honesty serves as a final warning. The consequence of this murder is not just worldly justice but eternal punishment. وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ—"That is the recompense of the wrongdoers." This is what awaits those who commit dhulm, oppression and injustice of the highest order.

---

translation: And his soul permitted to him the murder of his brother, so he killed him and became among the losers
commentary:
فَطَوَّعَتْ لَهُۥ نَفْسُهُۥ قَتْلَ أَخِيهِ فَقَتَلَهُۥ فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ

Despite the warning, despite knowing the consequences, Qabil's nafs—his ego, his inner self—compelled him toward the murder. The word طَوَّعَتْ (tawwa'at) means that his nafs made him volunteer for this action, made him agree to it, convinced him it was acceptable. His inner self overpowered his better judgment.

فَقَتَلَهُۥ—"So he killed him." The deed is done simply, starkly. There is no detailed description of the violence, no dramatic narrative. The focus is on the spiritual reality: a brother has murdered his brother.

فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ—"And he became among the losers." Not just a loser in this world, but among الْخَـٰسِرِينَ, those who suffer ultimate loss. This is the first murder in human history, and it establishes a pattern: when a person kills another unjustly, they do not gain anything. They only lose—spiritually, eternally.

---

translation: Then Allah sent a crow searching in the ground to show him how to hide the disgrace of his brother. He said, "O woe to me! Have I failed to be like this crow and hide the body of my brother?" And he became of the regretful
commentary:
فَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ غُرَابًا يَبْحَثُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُرِيَهُۥ كَيْفَ يُوَٰرِى سَوْءَةَ أَخِيهِ ۚ قَالَ يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰٓ أَعَجَزْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِثْلَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْغُرَابِ فَأُوَٰرِىَ سَوْءَةَ أَخِى ۖ فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ ٱلنَّـٰدِمِينَ

After the murder, Qabil is confronted with a body—the first corpse in human history. He does not know what to do with it. There is no precedent for death, no cultural practice of burial.

Allah ﷻ sends a crow that begins scratching at the earth, digging. Through this simple creature, divine instruction comes: this is how you deal with death. You cover the body, you return it to the earth. It is profound that the first burial is taught not through revelation to a prophet, but through the behavior of a bird.

To this day, the crow remains a symbol of death in many cultures. This association traces back to the very beginning—the first death in humanity, and the crow that appeared to teach the living what to do.

Watching the crow, Qabil cries out: يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰٓ—an expression of anguish and regret. "Woe to me! Have I become so incapable that I cannot even be like this crow and cover the body of my brother?"

His shame is not merely about failing to figure out burial on his own. It is the deeper humiliation of being taught by a bird, of needing divine intervention through the lowest of means because his own humanity has been compromised by his crime.

فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ ٱلنَّـٰدِمِينَ—"And he became among those filled with regret." Allah ﷻ has already called him one of the losers. Now we learn he is filled with nadamah, profound regret and shame. But this regret, by itself, means nothing. Feeling guilty does not erase the crime. Being unable to sleep at night because of what you have done does not restore the life you have taken.

This is an important distinction. Remorse is a natural human response to wrongdoing, but it is not the same as repentance (tawbah). Tawbah involves turning to Allah ﷻ, seeking forgiveness, and fundamentally changing one's path. Mere regret, without that turn toward the Divine, is spiritually empty. Even disbelievers feel guilt. Even those who have no consciousness of Allah ﷻ can be tormented by their crimes. But guilt without transformation leads nowhere.

---

translation: Because of that, We decreed upon the Children of Israel that whoever kills a soul unless for a soul or for corruption [done] in the land - it is as if he had slain mankind entirely. And whoever saves one - it is as if he had saved mankind entirely. And our messengers had certainly come to them with clear proofs. Then indeed many of them, [even] after that, throughout the land, were transgressors
commentary:
مِنْ أَجْلِ ذَٰلِكَ كَتَبْنَا عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنَّهُۥ مَن قَتَلَ نَفْسًۢا بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسَادٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّمَا قَتَلَ ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا وَمَنْ أَحْيَاهَا فَكَأَنَّمَآ أَحْيَا ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ إِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لَمُسْرِفُونَ

The transition in this ayah is striking. We move directly from the story of Adam's sons to legislation given to the Children of Israel. Why this immediate jump? Because Bani Israel were chosen by Allah ﷻ to be the model nation, the people who would demonstrate to the rest of humanity what a guided life looks like. Earlier in this surah, Allah ﷻ reminded them: "I have favored you over all nations." With that favor came responsibility—the responsibility to uphold the highest standards of justice and human dignity.

The principle established is absolute and uncompromising: مَن قَتَلَ نَفْسًۢا بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسَادٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّمَا قَتَلَ ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا—"Whoever kills a soul without just cause—either in retaliation for murder or for corruption in the land—it is as if they have killed all of humanity."

One unjust murder is equivalent to murdering every human being who has ever lived. This is not hyperbole. It reflects the sacred value of each individual life and the catastrophic spiritual consequence of destroying that life without right.

The inverse is equally powerful: وَمَنْ أَحْيَاهَا فَكَأَنَّمَآ أَحْيَا ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا—"And whoever saves a life, it is as if they have saved all of humanity."

This principle applies universally. Saving a life is saving a life, regardless of the faith, nationality, or background of the person involved. When a natural disaster strikes and people rush to save others from collapsing buildings, their courage and compassion are worthy of recognition. When paramedics revive someone having a heart attack, their skill and dedication deserve gratitude. When firefighters rescue families from burning homes, their bravery deserves honor.

Islam does not require us to withhold appreciation for good actions simply because they are performed by non-Muslims. We are not called to be blind to human decency. Being loyal to our faith does not mean hating everyone outside of it. For centuries, Muslims lived alongside Christians, Jews and others in mutual respect and cooperation. Neighbors of different faiths shared meals, conducted business together, and coexisted peacefully. The current climate of animosity and suspicion toward everyone outside the Ummah is a recent and harmful development, not an authentic expression of Islamic values.

We must distinguish between loyalty to our principles and unnecessary hostility toward others. Thanking someone who helped you does not compromise your faith. Making dua for the guidance of those who showed you kindness is not betrayal—it is both gratitude and compassion.

The ayah concludes with a sobering observation: وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ إِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لَمُسْرِفُونَ—"Our messengers came to them with clear proofs, yet even after that, many of them continued to commit excesses in the land."

The word مُسْرِفُونَ (musrifun) refers to those who go overboard, who are excessive in their crimes. They were told: one life for one life. But they did not stop at justice—they exceeded it. If one of their people was killed, they would kill ten, a hundred, an entire village in retaliation. They would not limit themselves to proportional justice; they would seek vengeance that far exceeded the original wrong.

This is what nationalism and tribalism do. They devalue the lives of those outside the group. "Our citizen is worth more than theirs. Our people matter more than theirs." This thinking allows for collective punishment, for massacres justified as retaliation, for endless cycles of violence.

Before we critique the historical failures of Bani Israel, we must examine the current state of the Muslim world. Are Muslims killing other Muslims today? Yes. Is Muslim blood treated as cheap and expendable in many places? Tragically, yes. Authoritarian regimes have turned weapons on their own populations. Sectarian violence has torn communities apart. Reckless behavior by the wealthy and powerful goes unpunished while the vulnerable suffer.

The sanctity of life that Allah ﷻ established in this ayah has been forgotten in too many places. We cannot point fingers at past nations while ignoring present realities. The call of this ayah is for us: to recognize the profound value of every human life, to reject the excesses that lead to mass bloodshed, and to return to the justice and mercy that Islam demands.

Human life is sacred. This is not a conditional statement. It does not apply only to some lives and not others. Every soul carries the weight of all humanity, and every saved life reflects the value of every life that has ever been or will ever be lived.

translation: Indeed, the penalty for those who wage war against Allah and His Messenger and strive upon earth [to cause] corruption is none but that they be killed or crucified or that their hands and feet be cut off from opposite sides or that they be exiled from the land. That is for them a disgrace in this world; and for them in the Hereafter is a great punishment
commentary:
إِنَّمَا جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحَارِبُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَسَادًا أَن يُقَتَّلُوٓا۟ أَوْ يُصَلَّبُوٓا۟ أَوْ تُقَطَّعَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُم مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ أَوْ يُنفَوْا۟ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لَهُمْ خِزْىٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ

### The Context: From Sanctity to Severity

To properly understand this ayah, we must appreciate both its textual and historical context. Allah ﷻ has just concluded a powerful discussion about the sanctity of human life through the story of the sons of Adam (AS). The divine principle was established for the Children of Israel: taking one innocent life is equivalent to killing all of humanity, and saving one life is like saving all of humanity. Yet even after receiving such profound guidance, the conclusion was stark: "Even after that, many of them are musrifoon" - those who go beyond limits.

The word musrif describes someone who exceeds appropriate boundaries. While the Children of Israel were commanded to engage in necessary battles, they went far beyond what was required, targeting civilians and continuing conflicts long past necessity. This historical pattern sets the stage for what follows.

### A Dramatic Shift in Circumstance

Now we encounter what appears to be a complete reversal - Allah ﷻ prescribing the taking of life with severe punishments. But this is not a contradiction. The Muslims have transitioned from a position of vulnerability to one of authority. Just years earlier, they faced near annihilation at the Battle of the Trench. Now, Makkah has been conquered, and Allah ﷻ is establishing the final elements of Shariah, including how to deal with those who persist in hostility.

The peaceful conquest of Makkah should not obscure a critical reality: many Makkans were war criminals who had tortured innocent believers, conspired with enemies in Madinah, and sent assassins against the Prophet ﷺ. A peaceful takeover does not automatically grant immunity to those who committed such crimes or who continue to plot against the Muslim community.

### Understanding Yuhariboon: Those Who Wage War

إِنَّمَا جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحَارِبُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ - "The only recompense for those who wage war against Allah and His Messenger..."

The word yuhariboon (wage war) comes from harb, which derives from harbah - a concealed dagger used for robbery. This linguistic root reveals something profound about the mindset of these enemies. Their opposition wasn't primarily religious conviction but economic self-interest. Makkah was the economic capital of Arabia, and control of the Kaaba meant control of pilgrimage trade, hospitality services, and commerce. When Islam took over Makkah, it dismantled this exploitative economic system built around idol worship.

The phrase "wage war against Allah and His Messenger" is deliberately literal here. This is not a metaphorical reference to opposing the teachings of Islam. These were people who physically opposed the Prophet ﷺ in the flesh, who had the opportunity for peace but chose continued hostility. Even after witnessing the overwhelming victory of Islam, they refused to lay down their arms.

### The Severity of Punishment

وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَسَادًا - "and strive to spread corruption in the land..."

The word yas'awna (they strive) comes from sa'y - the same word used for running between Safa and Marwah during pilgrimage. These individuals weren't passively resistant; they were actively, energetically working to create chaos and corruption.

The prescribed punishments are deliberately severe: execution, crucifixion, amputation of hands and feet from opposite sides, or banishment from the land. The word yusallaboo (crucified) carries connotations of torture before death, of being hung upside down and beaten. This is intentionally harsh language.

Why such severity? This was Allah's ﷻ public service announcement to those still plotting against the Muslim community. After extending unprecedented mercy - forgiving crimes that would normally warrant capital punishment, welcoming former enemies - Allah ﷻ draws a final line. For those who spit on that mercy and continue their hostility, there would be no more clemency.

These were surgical strikes against specific individuals, not blanket policies. The punishment matches the crime: those who terrorized communities through highway robbery, assassination plots, and organized violence would face corresponding consequences. This is not standard Islamic foreign policy - this is crisis management for war criminals who refused every opportunity for peace.

### The Dual Punishment

ذَٰلِكَ لَهُمْ خِزْىٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ - "That is disgrace for them in this world, and for them in the Hereafter is a great punishment."

Notice the word لَهُمْ (for them) - exclusively for them. This is a particular kind of humiliation reserved for this specific category of criminals. And the worldly punishment is merely an appetizer. The structure of the ayah makes clear that facing consequences here does not exempt them from the Hereafter. Their accountability to Allah ﷻ remains, and there awaits them a tremendous punishment.

This combination serves multiple purposes: it establishes justice in the worldly realm, acts as a powerful deterrent to others considering similar actions, and makes clear that ultimate justice belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

translation: Except for those who return [repenting] before you apprehend them. And know that Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَقْدِرُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

### The Door of Repentance Remains Open

Just when the severity of the previous ayah might overwhelm us, Allah ﷻ immediately follows with an exception that reveals His mercy: "Except those who repent before you apprehend them..."

The condition is critical - من قبل أن تقدروا عليهم (before you have them in your power). This functions exactly like modern criminal justice systems: if someone turns themselves in voluntarily before being caught, they are dealt with differently than someone captured while fleeing. The sincerity of repentance is demonstrated by voluntary surrender.

This exception applies to multiple scenarios. Someone might hear the public announcement of these punishments and decide to embrace Islam, laying down their weapons in genuine submission. Others might recognize the futility of continued resistance and seek amnesty, dropping their swords and saying, "I was at war with you, but I've changed my mind. Don't pursue me - I'm coming back peacefully."

The profound mercy here is that Allah ﷻ doesn't prescribe that Muslims show forgiveness - He announces His own. فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - "Know that Allah is Forgiving and Merciful." By mentioning His own forgiveness rather than commanding human forgiveness, Allah ﷻ reminds the Muslim community: if Allah is so forgiving, how can you afford to be harsh? You may need to execute justice regarding war crimes, but don't let your hearts become hardened. Remember why you're doing this - to establish a land where Allah ﷻ can be worshipped freely, where justice prevails.

This is the beautiful balance of Islam. In the midst of discussing enemies and harsh punishments, Allah ﷻ redirects our focus to our own spiritual state. Don't forget your purpose. Don't lose your compassion in the pursuit of justice.

translation: O you who have believed, fear Allah and seek the means [of nearness] to Him and strive in His cause that you may succeed
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱبْتَغُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ٱلْوَسِيلَةَ وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ

### The Progression of a Believer

After discussing matters of state, warfare, and justice, Allah ﷻ pivots completely to address the internal spiritual development of believers. This transition is masterful - we've been thinking about external enemies, and now Allah ﷻ refocuses us on our relationship with Him.

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - "O you who have believed, have taqwa of Allah."

Notice that Allah ﷻ addresses "those who have iman" and then commands them to have taqwa. This distinction is crucial. Having iman is not identical to having taqwa. You can become Muslim without yet developing true God-consciousness. Iman is the foundation, but taqwa is an active, ongoing state of awareness - being cautious about displeasing Allah ﷻ, watching yourself, being mindful of your choices.

### Understanding Al-Waseelah

وَٱبْتَغُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ٱلْوَسِيلَةَ - "And seek the means of nearness to Him."

The word al-waseelah has been subject to much misunderstanding and misuse in Islamic discourse, so we must examine it carefully. In classical Arabic, waseelah and its sister word sababun (rope) both indicate means to an end. Sababun literally referred to the rope thrown over a palm tree to climb up and pick fruit - it became the means to reach the food. Waseelah is more nuanced; it refers specifically to what you do to get close to someone.

According to Ibn Manzur's Lisan al-Arab, one of the most authoritative classical Arabic lexicons, waseelah means:
- The status or rank you achieve with a king - closeness to authority through your actions
- A rank or position of honor
- Pursuing closeness itself

He explains that someone who has waseelah with Allah is someone who performed good deeds that brought them nearer to Him. A mutawassil is someone passionately inclined toward Allah, always concerned with pleasing Him, constantly mindful of Him.

Some contemporary interpreters have misused this word to justify practices where people call upon deceased saints or spiritual figures as intermediaries, claiming these individuals are the waseelah to Allah ﷻ. But this understanding contradicts the linguistic and Quranic usage of the term. The Prophet ﷺ himself provided us with countless means to draw near to Allah ﷻ: the adhkar we recite entering and leaving our homes, the duas before eating and after finishing meals, the supplications for traveling, for entering and leaving the masjid, and hundreds more.

The waseelah is the beautiful way of life the Prophet ﷺ taught us. It's good company that reminds us of Allah ﷻ. It's regular reminders from the Quran and Sunnah. It's charity and spending in Allah's path. These are all means by which we draw closer and closer to our Creator.

### The Path to Success

وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ - "And strive in His way so that you may succeed."

The word jahidu (strive) shares its root with jihad - exerting your utmost effort. The phrase "so that you may succeed" uses la'allakum tuflihoon, where falah implies success that comes after tremendous labor and struggle. This isn't easy success - it's earned success.

We can understand this ayah as presenting a beautiful progression in a believer's spiritual journey:

**First Stage - Taqwa:** Begin with fear and consciousness of Allah ﷻ. Develop awareness that prevents you from disobeying Him. This is the minimum requirement - without some level of taqwa, we are failures in our purpose.

**Second Stage - Seeking Waseelah:** Once you have basic taqwa, don't let your relationship with Allah ﷻ remain based solely on fear. Transform it into one of love. Draw near to Him. When you love someone, you want to be close to them. Seek out every opportunity, every action, every moment that brings you nearer to Him. Your obedience should shift from fearful compliance to passionate pursuit.

**Third Stage - Striving in His Path:** When you've tasted that closeness, when you've fallen in love with Allah ﷻ and His path, you become a mujahid in His way. You struggle with your heart and soul because you know it makes Him happy. You're not just avoiding sins; you're actively, enthusiastically pursuing everything He wants from you.

Others understand this ayah more cohesively: the waseelah itself is the struggle in Allah's path. This was the lived reality of the Companions (RA) - their entire lives were this cause. They would fight battles during the day and stand in prayer all night. To them, there was no separation between military struggle and spiritual devotion. Both were forms of worship, both were means of drawing near to Allah ﷻ.

The ayah concludes with hope: لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ - "so that you may succeed." True success requires immense labor. Allah ﷻ is teaching us that spiritual growth, drawing near to Him, and striving in His path will eventually lead to success - but only if we put in the work, only if we persevere, only if we remain committed to this journey regardless of the obstacles we face.

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve - if they should have all that is in the earth and the like of it with it by which to ransom themselves from the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, it will not be accepted from them, and for them is a painful punishment
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّ لَهُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا وَمِثْلَهُۥ مَعَهُۥ لِيَفْتَدُوا۟ بِهِۦ مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَا تُقُبِّلَ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

This ayah carries a powerful message about the finality of disbelief. Allah ﷻ begins with إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ (those who disbelieve), establishing the subject with emphasis. Notice the grammatical structure here—when you reach the predicate, it appears as though the subject has shifted. This happens when Allah ﷻ expresses His complete rejection of the disbelievers: "Those who disbelieve, if they had everything on earth altogether, and even another one like it..."

The ayah presents a hypothetical scenario: even if the disbelievers possessed everything in the earth—all its wealth, resources and treasures—and then had a duplicate of the entire planet's worth on top of that, it still wouldn't be enough. They would want to offer all of this لِيَفْتَدُوا۟ بِهِۦ (as a ransom) to escape the punishment they'll face.

Notice Allah ﷻ specifically says مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (from the punishment of the Day of Standing), rather than saying punishment of the Fire directly. Why? Because before they even reach the Fire, there is Yawm al-Qiyamah—the Day of Judgment itself. Those who waged war against Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ will witness the colossal weight of their deeds being measured. When they see the magnitude of their crimes against Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, they will desperately wish they had the weight of an entire planet, or even two planets, to counterbalance their evil deeds.

But Allah ﷻ declares: مَا تُقُبِّلَ مِنْهُمْ (it would not be accepted from them). The use of مَا here indicates absolute negation—not in any way, shape or form whatsoever. No ransom will be taken from them, no matter how valuable.

The ayah concludes: وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (and for them exclusively is going to be painful punishment). The structure emphasizes that this punishment is specifically reserved for them—a uniquely painful torment awaits.

translation: They will wish to get out of the Fire, but never are they to emerge therefrom, and for them is an enduring punishment
commentary:
يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ وَمَا هُم بِخَـٰرِجِينَ مِنْهَا ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ

This ayah continues describing the state of the disbelievers in the Fire. They will desperately want to escape—يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ (they will want to come out of the Fire). But Allah ﷻ immediately crushes this hope: وَمَا هُم بِخَـٰرِجِينَ مِنْهَا (and they are not going to be coming out of it).

The grammatical construction here is particularly powerful. The particle مَا isn't just negation—it's emphatic refutation. On top of that, Allah ﷻ uses the continuous form بِخَـٰرِجِينَ with the ب prefix, which reinforces permanence. This nominal form conveys that they will never, ever come out. Not once, not temporarily, not eventually—never.

The ayah concludes: وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ (and they have an established punishment). The word مُّقِيمٌ (standing, permanent, installed) tells us this punishment isn't temporary or fluctuating. It's fixed, permanent and enduring.

translation: [As for] the thief, the male and the female, amputate their hands in recompense for what they committed as a deterrent [punishment] from Allah. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
وَٱلسَّارِقُ وَٱلسَّارِقَةُ فَٱقْطَعُوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمَا جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَسَبَا نَكَـٰلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ

This ayah marks a transition in the surah, introducing specific criminal justice laws for the Muslim community. It feels like a continuation of themes from Surah An-Nisa, which addressed social values and laws governing relationships. Here, we receive clear guidance on criminal justice.

The command is direct: وَٱلسَّارِقُ وَٱلسَّارِقَةُ فَٱقْطَعُوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمَا (the male thief and the female thief, cut their hands). This serves as جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَسَبَا (a compensation for what they earned themselves). The word كَسَبَا indicates they earned this consequence through their own actions.

The ayah then describes this punishment as نَكَـٰلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ (a means of deterring others from Allah). The word نَكَال comes from a root meaning to terrify or deter. It's a public consequence that makes others think twice before committing the same crime. When people witness the severity of the punishment, they're forced to ask themselves: "Is this theft worth my hand?" This creates a powerful deterrent effect that protects society.

There's a beautiful story that illustrates the precision of Quranic language. Al-Kisai, the great grammarian and linguist who lived during the time of Caliph Harun al-Rashid, used to teach Arabic classes in Baghdad attended by hundreds of scholars. His methodology was meticulous—for every grammar rule, he would cite an ayah from the Quran, then a statement from a Companion (RA), then classical poetry, then expressions from the Arabs. This A-B-C-D pattern made his lessons systematic and comprehensive.

One day, a Bedouin traveler sat in on his lecture. While everyone else was dressed as scholars, this man was clearly from the desert. As Al-Kisai recited this ayah quickly as an example, he pronounced the ending as إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ but in his haste, it sounded like he said "Rahim" with a different grammatical ending than what appears in the Quran.

The Bedouin raised his hand and asked, "Who said this?" 

The entire audience was shocked—who was this stranger questioning the great Al-Kisai? The sheikh himself was taken aback and responded, "This is the speech of Allah ﷻ."

The Bedouin protested, "God forbid He would ever say something like that! This is from the Quran, the word of Allah ﷻ? No way."

The Muslim scholars in attendance nearly attacked the man, thinking he had denied the Quran. But Al-Kisai stopped them, demonstrating his wisdom: "Let's establish the case against him properly. Open the Mushaf, show him the ayah. If he still denies it after seeing it, then we'll deal with him accordingly."

They opened the Quran and showed him. The Bedouin looked at it and said, "Did I memorize this? No. But when you recited it the way you did, it didn't make sense. You're saying 'The male and female thief, cut their hands as compensation for what they earned'—and then you're saying Allah ﷻ forgives them? That doesn't connect properly!"

This story reveals something profound about the Arabs and their understanding of language. They didn't know formal grammar rules or technical linguistic analysis, but they had an innate sense of how words fit together, how meanings connect, and how messages bind into coherent wholes. The Bedouin could sense that something was off in how the ayah flowed, even without technical knowledge.

The story also teaches us that we should pay attention to how ayahs conclude and how ideas connect within their context. Every word choice, every grammatical form, every transition carries meaning and purpose.

The ayah concludes with two critical divine names: وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (and Allah is the Ultimate Authority, the All-Wise). Why are these names specifically mentioned here? 

عَزِيزٌ (the Ultimate Authority) reminds us that this law comes from the One who has absolute authority. In pre-Islamic Arabia, theft was common—almost casual. Going out and "acquiring" things was normalized. But now Islam completely clamped down on this behavior with the harshest penalties. The authority to implement such severe criminal law comes from Allah ﷻ alone, and the Muslim community is now authorized to execute these punishments.

حَكِيمٌ (the All-Wise) assures us that whether we fully understand it or not, this law is full of wisdom. Some might view it as barbaric or excessive, but Allah's ﷻ laws exist to benefit and protect us, not to harm us. Consider contemporary society where shoplifting and theft are treated lightly. How many murders are associated with what starts as "just stealing"? How many car chases end with innocent people killed or paralyzed? How much additional crime is born from what begins as simple theft? One person steals, runs from police, and in the chase, crashes into a family, destroying lives forever.

The wisdom in this law creates real deterrence. In modern criminal justice systems, what happens to criminals? They go to jail, where rent is free, food is provided, and if you have connections, life isn't even that difficult. Some criminals treat it as an occupational hazard—they'll "do some time," add it to their street credibility, collect another tattoo representing another stint inside. We've actually created systems that enable perpetual criminal behavior. Someone choosing that life becomes accustomed to it: "I'll do a little time, no big deal."

Islam's approach puts a serious check on criminal behavior. At the expense of one person who commits such a crime and faces this consequence, countless others are protected. That one visible punishment makes society think before acting. People weigh their choices carefully: "What do I prefer more—that extra hundred dollars or my hand?" This creates genuine safety for communities.

translation: But whoever repents after his wrongdoing and reforms, indeed, Allah will turn to him in forgiveness. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
فَمَن تَابَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ظُلْمِهِۦ وَأَصْلَحَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

After establishing the severe punishment for theft, Allah ﷻ immediately opens the door of mercy. This is the balance of Islamic law—justice with mercy, accountability with hope.

فَمَن تَابَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ظُلْمِهِۦ (whoever makes tawbah after having done wrong)—perhaps someone was caught stealing but didn't lose their hand for some reason, or perhaps they did face the punishment but afterward genuinely reformed. وَأَصْلَحَ (and corrected their ways, straightened out their life), then what?

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِ (certainly Allah turns back toward them in repentance). Allah ﷻ Himself gives them a second chance.

This teaches us something crucial: Muslims can be quick to judge, especially in matters of visible sin. Imagine walking down the street and seeing someone with one hand. How many of us would immediately think "criminal" and cross to the other side? But Allah ﷻ is teaching us that even someone whose criminal past is literally visible on their body can make tawbah and be forgiven by Him. If Allah ﷻ has opened the door of forgiveness for them, how can we dare close it?

Compare this to contemporary society where people carry permanent criminal records. No matter how much genuine reform someone goes through, their record follows them. A teenager makes a terrible mistake—gets drunk, drives, crashes into a tree, gets arrested. Or joins friends in robbing a convenience store. He spends a year or two in juvenile detention or prison, and now it's on his permanent record. He can't get a job. Schools won't accept him. He lives with his parents, depressed, with no opportunities. Then criminal elements approach him: "Nobody's going to give you a job anyway. I've got something for you." We've actually opened pathways to continued criminal behavior by refusing to forgive those with a past.

Allah ﷻ is teaching us differently. Yes, background checks have value, but we shouldn't pass absolute judgment on people based on their past. Everyone makes mistakes—some bigger than others. The ayah concludes: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (Indeed, Allah is Exceedingly Forgiving, Especially Merciful). These names tell us that Allah's ﷻ capacity for forgiveness is vast, and His mercy is particular and specific to each person's situation.

translation: Do you not know that to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth? He punishes whom He wills and forgives whom He wills, and Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ يُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

Allah ﷻ now connects these laws to a fundamental truth about His sovereignty. أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ (Did you not already know?) uses the questioning form to emphasize something we should already understand.

أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (that Allah exclusively owns the dominion of the heavens and the earth). Notice how ownership and theft are connected. When someone steals, they're taking someone else's property—the store owner should be offended. But Allah ﷻ is saying something deeper: "I am the actual Owner." When someone steals, they're taking from what Allah ﷻ has placed somewhere and trying to relocate it without His permission. The offense isn't just against another person—ultimately, the offense is against Allah's ﷻ ownership and authority.

And because He is the true Owner, He alone has the right to determine consequences: يُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ (He punishes whomever He wills and He forgives whomever He wills). This teaches us something profound about the relationship between worldly justice and divine judgment.

In this world, there are justice systems, courts, punishments and social consequences. But what you experience in this world is not necessarily a guarantee of how you will be judged in the next world. Someone might be punished for their crime here, judged by society, rejected by people, never forgiven by the community—but none of that proves Allah ﷻ won't forgive them. This is a critically important lesson.

Sometimes people pass judgment on us. Sometimes we pass judgment on ourselves: "I'm a loser. I'm useless. I'm a hypocrite. I'm this, I'm that." This self-judgment can occasionally be healthy for accountability, but often it becomes destructive. People keep telling you how evil you are, how bad you are, listing your failures. But their judgment—or even your own harsh self-assessment—is no proof of how Allah ﷻ sees you. You cannot use human judgment to extrapolate your standing with Allah ﷻ.

Unfortunately, some religious leadership is immature in how they deal with people's sins and struggles. Someone approaches a scholar: "Brother, I've committed a sin." "What sin?" "It's haram." "Yeah, it's haram. Actually, it's pretty haram. One of the super harams—a combo package!" "Do you think I'm going to Hell?" "Yes, I believe so." "I'm trying to make tawbah..." "Yeah, I don't know if it's accepted. You're probably going to Hell. Nice talking to you, though."

This is how some people deal with those who are struggling. You cannot shut the doors of hope on people. Someone asks, "I'm going through a difficult time in my life. Is it because Allah ﷻ hates me?" People actually say things like this. Or they ask, "Is Allah ﷻ putting me through these difficulties to punish my sins here so I don't have to pay for them in the Afterlife?"

Who are you to make that judgment call? You cannot make such definitive statements. Everyone goes through trials, but why they're experiencing those specific trials is known only to Allah ﷻ. He didn't send you a personal memo explaining: "Here's why you lost your job. Here's why you were diagnosed with this illness." You don't know.

All you can do is consider the possibilities: "Perhaps this is because of my sins, and if it is, I'd better make tawbah. Or perhaps this is just a test to increase my status with Allah ﷻ. Or perhaps this is preparing me for something greater ahead." Keep all possibilities open and expect good from Allah ﷻ. That's all we can do.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (and Allah has complete control over all things). His power encompasses everything—punishment, forgiveness, trials, blessings, this world and the next. We must remember that our limited understanding cannot capture the fullness of His wisdom and His plans. What we must do is turn to Him in repentance, trust in His mercy, and never lose hope in His forgiveness, no matter what we've done or what people say about us.

translation: O Messenger, let them not grieve you who hasten into disbelief of those who say, "We believe" with their mouths, but their hearts believe not, and from among the Jews. [They are] avid listeners to falsehood, listening to another people who have not come to you. They distort words beyond their [proper] usages, saying "If you are given this, take it; but if you are not given it, then beware." But he for whom Allah intends fitnah - never will you possess [power to do] for him a thing against Allah. Those are the ones for whom Allah does not intend to purify their hearts. For them in this world is disgrace, and for them in the Hereafter is a great punishment
commentary:
۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلرَّسُولُ لَا يَحْزُنكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْكُفْرِ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَلَمْ تُؤْمِن قُلُوبُهُمْ ۛ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ ۛ سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِقَوْمٍ ءَاخَرِينَ لَمْ يَأْتُوكَ ۖ يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَوَاضِعِهِۦ ۖ يَقُولُونَ إِنْ أُوتِيتُمْ هَـٰذَا فَخُذُوهُ وَإِن لَّمْ تُؤْتَوْهُ فَٱحْذَرُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن يُرِدِ ٱللَّهُ فِتْنَتَهُۥ فَلَن تَمْلِكَ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يُرِدِ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُطَهِّرَ قُلُوبَهُمْ ۚ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ

**The Prophet's ﷺ Grief and Allah's ﷻ Reassurance**

Allah ﷻ begins this ayah with a direct address to His Messenger ﷺ: "O Messenger, let not those grieve you who hasten into disbelief." This divine consolation comes at a crucial time in the Prophet's ﷺ mission. The sadness the Prophet ﷺ felt wasn't rooted in fear that these people might somehow succeed in their disbelief—that concern had already been resolved. Rather, his grief stemmed from witnessing people reject guidance even when the path forward was crystal clear. The door to guidance stood wide open before them, and all roads leading to destruction had been cut off. They had no excuse left except to accept the path to Paradise and reject the path to Hellfire. Yet they still chose the wrong path. This is what made the Prophet ﷺ sad—how could people persist in such clear error?

But Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ not to worry about them anymore.

**The Hypocrites Among the Muslims**

The ayah then describes the first group: مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَلَمْ تُؤْمِن قُلُوبُهُمْ - "Those who say with their mouths, 'We believe,' but their hearts have not believed." This addresses a natural phenomenon that occurred as Islam gained strength. When the Muslim community was small, the quality of faith was exceptionally high. Everyone who embraced Islam did so with conviction, often at great personal cost. But after victory came and large numbers of people began entering the faith, maintaining that quality became challenging.

People started joining for various reasons—some genuine, others superficial. Perhaps a cousin embraced Islam, so they followed suit. Perhaps Islam simply seemed like the winning side. These people claimed faith with their tongues, but their hearts hadn't truly accepted it. They were like sports fans who switch allegiance to whichever team is winning. But the Prophet ﷺ could see right through this superficiality. Allah ﷻ is reminding him not to let this cause him grief.

**The Jewish Conspirators**

The ayah continues: وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ - "And among the Jews." This refers to those from the Jewish community who were causing problems through deception and conspiracy. The description that follows is devastating: سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِقَوْمٍ ءَاخَرِينَ لَمْ يَأْتُوكَ - "Those who listen eagerly to falsehood, listening to other people who have not come to you."

These individuals were presenting themselves as part of the believing community or at least neutral parties. But behind the scenes, they were conspiring with the aggressive Quraysh who remained on the outskirts, still attempting to fight the Muslims. They served as spies and informants for those who refused to submit to the truth.

**The Distortion of Scripture**

Allah ﷻ then reveals something profound about their manipulation of scripture: يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَوَاضِعِهِۦ - "They distort words from their proper places." To understand the gravity of this statement, we need to appreciate the historical context. The Torah had been completely lost at certain points in history. During the Babylonian exile, when the Jews were held as slaves, they didn't even have access to their scripture. The prophet Ezra (AS), known in Arabic as Uzayr, was given the monumental task of recompiling the Torah through divine revelation. He restored what had been lost and revived the nation.

The preservation of scripture was itself a tremendous blessing and required enormous effort. The destruction of Jerusalem by various empires—the Assyrians, Babylonians, and later others—had been so complete that all books were burned and the nation scattered. After all that divine mercy in restoring the scripture, these people were still moving the words from their proper places, still refusing to keep the revelation as it was given.

**The Instructions to Manipulate**

The ayah reveals their specific strategy: يَقُولُونَ إِنْ أُوتِيتُمْ هَـٰذَا فَخُذُوهُ وَإِن لَّمْ تُؤْتَوْهُ فَٱحْذَرُوا۟ - "They say, 'If you are given this, then take it; but if you are not given it, then beware.'" This needs historical context to fully appreciate. During the time of the Prophet ﷺ, Muslims were in a position of strength, which created curiosity about Islam—not just among Makkans, but also among the Christians and Jews of Arabia.

Jewish followers were coming to their rabbis saying, "We heard that the Quran confirms what came before it from the Torah. We listened to this man who claims to be a prophet, and it sounded just like that passage we read from the Torah last week." The religious leaders felt threatened by this. So they altered their scripture and instructed their people: "If you find this modified version in their book, fine. If not, watch out—they're a deviant, misguided group."

They were deliberately misguiding their own religious following. This teaches us something critical about the power of religious leadership. It's an unsheathed sword—leaders can take people in any direction they want. The unfortunate nature of a sleeping nation is that masses of people become gullible.

**The Contrast with an Awakened Community**

What does it mean for a nation to be awake? The early Muslim community had direct access to the Quran. They understood things directly from revelation. When they saw something contradictory, they didn't have to accept anything blindly—not even from each other. It was a transparent understanding of religion.

But what happens when a nation falls asleep? People stop educating themselves in religion. They just go ask someone else. And they ask whoever their father used to ask, and whoever their grandfather used to ask, staying within a certain line. This has happened within Islam too. Masses of people adopt the mindset: "We do things this way. Our shaykh says this is right." 

Now, scholars absolutely have their place—but they truly fulfill that place when they're dealing with an educated Muslim community that knows how to ask educated questions. Our culture was never meant to be one where the scholar speaks and everyone else stays quiet. That's actually Christian culture, Jewish culture, Hindu culture—where you take from religious leadership without even knowing where they got it from. We've always been a culture of transparent scholarship.

**The Problem of Unverified Religious Material**

This transparency requires verification and citation. In the Arab world, when a book is published and a hadith is quoted, there's a bibliography with page numbers. "This is from Sahih Bukhari, page such-and-such. This is from Sahih Muslim. Here are the scholars who accept it. Here are the scholars who have concerns about it." There's at least a bibliography.

But in the Indo-Pak subcontinent, if you go to an Islamic bookstore and pick up a book of adhkar or wisdom or manners, nearly everything is attributed to the Prophet ﷺ without any source. "Here are the benefits of reciting this ayah: if you recite it 25,000 times, your mortgage will be paid off without you worrying about it." Where's the hadith? Where did this come from? It's not there. Why? Because people are assumed to be blind followers who won't verify anyway.

Even if someone isn't going to verify, it's sound academic practice in a religion that feeds on authenticity to at least have the concern to put something on paper with references. This practice has been largely abandoned in certain communities.

One example illustrates this perfectly. A family member once asked about a famous dua they used to make, something they remembered from childhood. After searching extensively, the dua was finally found in a book of accepted prayers. But what was it? Bits and pieces of different ayahs cobbled together, followed by random disconnected Arabic letters. At the end was the "source": a story about a shaykh traveling to Hajj by sea who supposedly saw the Prophet ﷺ in a vision during a storm and was told to recite this dua 1,000 times. By the time they finished, their three-month journey was over and they were safely at the coast. No one knew who the shaykh was or when this happened.

Then came the "benefits": if you're bald, your hair will grow back. If you lost your keys, you'll find them. If you want a boy, recite this. If you want to get married, this is the solution. Allergies, financial problems, everything—one solution for all problems.

Beyond the lack of scholarly verification, there are serious theological issues with such stories. They suggest someone saw the Prophet ﷺ while awake and received direct instructions, which contradicts our belief system. Seeing the Prophet ﷺ in a dream is acceptable, but receiving new religious instructions while awake is problematic.

**The Path Forward**

This is how the movement of words from their proper places begins—with very innocent-seeming things. Perhaps multiple authors contributed, thinking "this piece of an ayah would fit nicely here." Before long, people are reciting random combinations of Quranic letters as protection from traffic tickets or expired insurance.

We don't necessarily have to wage war against all that material—there's simply too much of it. If we spent our lives saying "don't do this," we'd never be done. What we should do instead is create a more mature, educated Muslim mind that looks for things in the proper way. We don't have to argue about those other things, but we can understand them as unfortunate detours along the road of the Ummah's progress and focus our energy on building proper understanding.

**Those Whom Allah ﷻ Leaves in Trial**

The ayah continues with a sobering reality: وَمَن يُرِدِ ٱللَّهُ فِتْنَتَهُۥ فَلَن تَمْلِكَ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا - "And whomever Allah ﷻ wills to put in trial, you will never be able to do anything for him against Allah ﷻ." The worst trial, the greatest fitna, is that you keep finding ways to reject revelation. This trial doesn't leave the person—it possesses them, defines them. The expression مَالِكَ لَهُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا means "you will have no say in his matter, no authority to help him."

This expression appears in a famous hadith where even Fatimah (RA), the beloved daughter of the Prophet ﷺ, was told: "O Fatimah, daughter of Muhammad! Fear Allah, for I will not be able to help you at all when it comes to Allah ﷻ. You must have your own record of deeds." If anyone deserved a free pass based on family connection, it would be the Prophet's ﷺ own daughter—daughters hold a special place in their father's heart. Yet even she was told she must stand on her own faith and actions.

Some people today claim status because they're from the family of the Prophet ﷺ, as if that grants them special privileges. But those who truly honor that lineage understand it as a responsibility to serve, not a badge to display. They see their connection to the Prophet ﷺ as motivation to constantly look out for other Muslims, to show love and make dua for the Ummah. That's the proper understanding—using any blessing as a means to serve others, not as a free pass through judgment.

**Hearts That Cannot Be Purified**

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يُرِدِ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُطَهِّرَ قُلُوبَهُمْ - "Those are the ones whose hearts Allah ﷻ does not intend to purify." What prevents the purification of hearts? The ayah has already told us: سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ - "listeners to falsehood." Notice it doesn't just say they're liars or that they lie. It says they listen to lies. When you listen to lies over and over again, it becomes a serious problem.

We see this with young people today who spend hours on YouTube watching videos that contradict Islam, then watch more videos to find answers to the doubts created by the first videos, then more videos, then more. This constant consumption of falsehood starts affecting the heart. Does it mess with your thinking? Absolutely. Allah ﷻ highlights the problem of سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ right after mentioning that He doesn't want to purify their hearts. When you listen to falsehood all the time, it prevents you from becoming a person with a clean heart. It taints your heart.

There's another critical issue here. We already read earlier in Surah Al-Maidah that when the religion is being mocked, you should leave that gathering. You should have the sensitivity to walk away.
