If you're constantly listening to people criticize the Quran and insult the Prophet ﷺ, you're violating Allah's ﷻ clear instruction. When you openly—out of curiosity or whatever else—continue to disobey that command, does your sensitivity about respecting Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ go down? It does. And with it, your heart deteriorates.

This isn't just theory. We see the practical reality of this among many young people in the Muslim community today. They sit in those gatherings. They watch those videos. They hang out with friends who say these kinds of things, and they just listen. Nothing bothers them anymore. In the beginning, they were disturbed by it. But slowly, their friendship became worth more to them than defending Allah's ﷻ Messenger ﷺ and the Quran. They tolerated insults in the name of maintaining relationships. And they paid the price—the price of their iman. That's literally the cost.

**The Consequences in This World and the Next**

لَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ - "For them in this world is disgrace, and for them in the Hereafter is a great punishment." The word خِزْيٌ (khizy) refers to deep humiliation and disgrace. They will face humiliation in this worldly life, and then in the afterlife, they will have a tremendous punishment.

translation: [They are] avid listeners to falsehood, devourers of [what is] unlawful. So if they come to you, [O Muhammad], judge between them or turn away from them. And if you turn away from them - never will they harm you at all. And if you judge, judge between them with justice. Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly
commentary:
سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ أَكَّـٰلُونَ لِلسُّحْتِ ۚ فَإِن جَآءُوكَ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُمْ أَوْ أَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ فَلَن يَضُرُّوكَ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَإِنْ حَكَمْتَ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ

**Devourers of the Unlawful**

Allah ﷻ repeats the description سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ - "listeners to falsehood," then adds: أَكَّـٰلُونَ لِلسُّحْتِ - "devourers of the unlawful." The word السُّحْت (as-suht) is significant. The Quran uses two main terms for prohibited things: haram and suht. Haram encompasses all kinds of forbidden matters—things you say, things you do, foods you eat. It's a general term for impermissible actions.

Suht, however, specifically relates to impermissible dealings with money: earning money through forbidden means, spending money on forbidden things, making prohibited investments, buying or selling haram items. All of this falls under suht. It's particularly mentioned in the context of the Israelites because they had become obsessed with riba (usury) and other corrupt financial practices.

Here's the striking contrast: they were meticulous about the laws of kosher food, but completely unconcerned about how they earned the money to buy that kosher meat. Has this happened among Muslims as well? Absolutely. People are particular about eating halal meat, but they might take cash from a liquor store register to buy that meat. They're not eating haram, but they're eating from suht earnings. The money itself is unlawful, so what's purchased with it is tainted.

**The Option to Judge or Ignore**

فَإِن جَآءُوكَ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُمْ أَوْ أَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ - "So if they come to you, then judge between them or turn away from them." Even though Allah ﷻ has exposed what these people do behind the scenes—their conspiracies, their distortions, their consumption of ill-gotten gains—when they come to the Prophet ﷺ seeking a verdict, he's given two options: either pass judgment between them with justice, or ignore them entirely.

The choice to ignore is significant because these people are manipulative. They will try to make you feel that there will be consequences for refusing to take their case. They'll imply threats or suggest you'll regret not helping them. But Allah ﷻ reassures His Messenger ﷺ: وَإِن تُعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ فَلَن يَضُرُّوكَ شَيْـًٔا - "And if you turn away from them, they will not harm you at all." Don't worry about their threats or manipulation. They can't actually do anything to hurt you.

**The Command for Justice**

وَإِنْ حَكَمْتَ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْقِسْطِ - "And if you judge, then judge between them with justice." The word القِسْط (al-qist) is powerful. It doesn't just mean being just—it means ensuring that you don't become unfair. It's an active effort to maintain fairness, to guard against any inclination toward injustice.

Even when dealing with people who lie, who manipulate scripture, who conspire against you, who consume unlawful wealth—even then, if you choose to judge their case, you must do so with complete fairness. Your judgment cannot be colored by their character or their past actions. The justice of Islam doesn't change based on who stands before you.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ - "Indeed, Allah ﷻ loves those who act justly." This is the ultimate motivation. It's not about whether these people deserve your fairness. It's not about whether they would show you the same consideration. It's about the fact that Allah ﷻ loves those who strive their absolute best to remain fair in all circumstances. That love of Allah ﷻ is worth more than any worldly consideration, worth more than any personal grievance, worth more than any desire for revenge. When you maintain justice even with those who wrong you, you earn something priceless: the love of Allah ﷻ.

translation: But how is it that they come to you for judgement while they have the Torah, in which is the judgement of Allah? Then they turn away, [even] after that; but those are not [in fact] believers
commentary:
وَكَيْفَ يُحَكِّمُونَكَ وَعِندَهُمُ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ فِيهَا حُكْمُ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَمَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question that exposes a profound contradiction: "And how is it that they make you the decision maker?" The word يُحَكِّمُونَكَ (yuḥakkimūnaka) means to make someone the judge, to make them the one who passes verdicts. The question Allah ﷻ asks is striking in its directness: why would they come to you seeking judgment?

The answer reveals their hypocrisy: وَعِندَهُمُ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ فِيهَا حُكْمُ ٱللَّهِ - "And they have in their possession the Torah, in which there is the verdict of Allah." This is the heart of the matter. They possess revelation that contains Allah's judgments, yet they seek alternatives. They come all the way to the Prophet ﷺ not out of genuine submission to divine guidance, but as part of their pattern of shopping for verdicts that suit their desires.

The ayah continues: ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ - "Then they turn away even after that." This describes their established pattern. They have had the Torah for centuries, even millennia, yet they haven't truly availed themselves of it. When its verdicts don't align with their preferences, they simply turn away. The use of ثُمَّ (then) emphasizes the severity of this turning away - it's not immediate rejection, but a calculated abandonment after having been given clear guidance.

The ayah concludes definitively: وَمَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - "And those are not believers at all." The grammatical structure here is emphatic, using the negative particle مَا along with the ب (ba) of emphasis. These individuals may claim faith, they may go through certain motions, but their behavior of seeking judgments only when convenient and rejecting divine verdicts when they conflict with their desires strips away any claim to genuine belief.

translation: Indeed, We sent down the Torah, in which was guidance and light. The prophets who submitted [to Allah] judged by it for the Jews, as did the rabbis and scholars by that with which they were entrusted of the Scripture of Allah, and they were witnesses thereto. So do not fear the people but fear Me, and do not exchange My verses for a small price. And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed - then it is those who are the disbelievers
commentary:
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَا ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ فِيهَا هُدًى وَنُورٌ ۚ يَحْكُمُ بِهَا ٱلنَّبِيُّونَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلرَّبَّـٰنِيُّونَ وَٱلْأَحْبَارُ بِمَا ٱسْتُحْفِظُوا۟ مِن كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَانُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ شُهَدَآءَ ۚ فَلَا تَخْشَوُا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ وَٱخْشَوْنِ وَلَا تَشْتَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِى ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ

Allah ﷻ declares: إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَا ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ - "Indeed, We sent down the Torah." This assertion establishes that the Quran's validity is equivalent to what the Torah originally was. The emphasis on "We sent it down" connects it to the same divine source as the Quran itself.

The description continues: فِيهَا هُدًى وَنُورٌ - "In it was guidance and light." There's an important grammatical consideration here. This can be understood as describing the Torah's original state - that it contained guidance and light when it was first revealed. Alternatively, it acknowledges that even after alterations, some guidance and light remain within it. Notice that the definite article (al-) is not used here - it's not الهدى والنور (the guidance and the light), which is how the Quran is described. The Quran alone is the comprehensive guidance and complete light for all nations. The Torah had guidance and light, but for a specific people at a specific time.

The ayah then describes who implemented this Torah: يَحْكُمُ بِهَا ٱلنَّبِيُّونَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا۟ - "The prophets judged by it, those who had submitted." Though the verb appears in present tense, it describes an ongoing historical reality - multiple prophets, one after another, used the same book to pass judgments. This is remarkable when you consider it: while we have one Prophet ﷺ who explained and implemented the Quran for us, the Jewish people had prophet after prophet after prophet explaining and applying the same Torah. If any nation should have had crystal clarity about how their law should be applied, it was them. Each prophet brought the same message, made the same interpretations, reached the same conclusions. There were no differences among them - they were prophets, not jurists who might have varying opinions. They were all on the same page, all divinely guided to the same understanding.

These prophets judged لِلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ - "for those who were Jews" - but the guidance extended beyond just the masses. وَٱلرَّبَّـٰنِيُّونَ وَٱلْأَحْبَارُ - "And the Rabbaniyyun and the Ahbar." These terms identify distinct groups within the community. الرَّبَّـٰنِيُّونَ (Rabbaniyyun) comes from رب (Rabb), referring to godly people, those spiritually inclined who lived in constant recognition of their Master. The closest parallel in our tradition would be the Muttaqun, those who are always conscious of Allah's presence. الْأَحْبَارُ (Ahbar) comes from حِبْر (hibr), which means ink - these were the scholars, the people of the pen, those dedicated to research and study.

This classification reveals something profound: the Torah, as explained by the prophets, addressed the needs of all segments of society. There was content for the general masses, spiritual guidance for those seeking closeness to Allah ﷻ, and deep scholarly material for those pursuing academic understanding. Similarly, the Quran stands at the center of all these needs. Someone seeking spiritual progress finds their path in the Quran and the Prophet's teachings. Someone seeking deep scholarship finds endless depths to explore. When we abandon this central source, we feel a void that we try to fill with other creative practices, but nothing can truly replace what Allah ﷻ has provided.

The ayah describes their responsibility: بِمَا ٱسْتُحْفِظُوا۟ مِن كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ - "with what they were entrusted to guard of Allah's Book." The word ٱسْتُحْفِظُوا۟ implies they were asked to protect it, made responsible for its preservation. The prophets guarded the Book by implementing it, by passing verdicts according to it. وَكَانُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ شُهَدَآءَ - "And they were witnesses over it." They testified through their actions that this Book contained the solutions their community needed.

Then comes the commanding counsel: فَلَا تَخْشَوُا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ وَٱخْشَوْنِ - "So do not fear the people, but fear Me." This addresses a universal challenge faced by those who uphold divine law. When you stand by Allah's verdict, it's often unpopular. It might break with established cultural practices. From a legal perspective, implementing what is halal and haram can be unpopular in families, in communities, even in religious institutions. The one who loses a case doesn't like the verdict - they resent whatever law led to their loss.

This applies equally to spiritual matters. Religious communities develop their own traditions, their own practices - some authentic, others innovations. When you come with the authenticated practices from revelation and say these should replace those other customs, people become defensive, even hostile. Within the Jewish tradition, various spiritual movements developed - the Kabbalah, for instance - with their own distinct practices. When prophets came with divine guidance contradicting these traditions, they faced significant resistance. Allah ﷻ tells His messengers: don't be intimidated by them.

The warning continues: وَلَا تَشْتَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِى ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا - "And do not sell My verses for a small price." What is this price? It's the price of acceptance, of popularity, of maintaining access and influence. One could interpret the religion in a convenient way that doesn't offend anyone, doesn't challenge anyone's comfortable practices, doesn't require anyone to change. You could spend your entire life discussing Islam without ever engaging in meaningful commanding of good or forbidding of evil. There's so much safe territory to cover - why touch the sensitive issues that might make you unpopular?

This challenge exists in every age. Religious leaders, speakers, and scholars get invited to platforms - mosques, conferences, venues - through which they reach their audiences. As long as they speak in safe generalities, the invitations continue. The moment they touch a nerve, mention specific problematic practices that have become widespread, or challenge the status quo regarding how institutions are financed or operated, suddenly the invitations stop. What should they do? Some rationalize: "To benefit the people, I need to play it smart. If I speak about controversial issues, my access will be cut off. Better to stay quiet on those matters so I can continue doing some good."

But this isn't the way of the prophets. We must be true to what we believe. Those who represent Islam on any platform must maintain integrity. This doesn't mean their job is to needlessly offend people - that's another extreme we see, where some think being harsh and judgmental is synonymous with upholding truth. That's not the prophetic way either. When the Prophet ﷺ was told "don't grieve over them," it indicated that his natural inclination was to grieve, not to judge harshly. His concern came from love. Even when he had to address difficult issues, it was always constructive, always rooted in genuine care for people's wellbeing.

Unfortunately, we often see one of two extremes: either complete silence on important issues, or harsh, angry condemnation that lacks mercy and wisdom. When someone attacks other scholars or members of other schools of thought, the first question should be: have you picked up the phone and called them? Have you visited them for a sincere conversation? If Allah ﷻ commanded us to speak respectfully even to those who claim He has a son, surely we can have respectful dialogue with Muslims of different scholarly orientations. But these conversations don't happen. Instead, there's an assumption of evil intent, and entire lectures are devoted to condemning others without any attempt at genuine engagement.

Why does this happen? When religion becomes an industry, when religious figures see their audience as a market share to be protected from competitors, the dynamics change completely. Like companies that attack competitors in commercials, some religious figures attack others to protect their following, their view counts, their influence. This is the reality of selling Allah's verses for a small price.

The ayah concludes with a severe warning: وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ - "And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed, then those are the disbelievers." This statement will be repeated three times across these ayahs, each time with a different descriptor (disbelievers, wrongdoers, rebellious). The scholars have written volumes about how to understand this judgment. The immediate context is clear: a community that has been given divine revelation but deliberately chooses to live contrary to it, preferring other lifestyles and other verdicts over what Allah ﷻ has commanded - this is kufr, this is ingratitude and denial of divine guidance.

translation: And We ordained for them therein a life for a life, an eye for an eye, a nose for a nose, an ear for an ear, a tooth for a tooth, and for wounds is legal retribution. But whoever gives [up his right as] charity, it is an expiation for him. And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed - then it is those who are the wrongdoers
commentary:
وَكَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِيهَآ أَنَّ ٱلنَّفْسَ بِٱلنَّفْسِ وَٱلْعَيْنَ بِٱلْعَيْنِ وَٱلْأَنفَ بِٱلْأَنفِ وَٱلْأُذُنَ بِٱلْأُذُنِ وَٱلسِّنَّ بِٱلسِّنِّ وَٱلْجُرُوحَ قِصَاصٌ ۚ فَمَن تَصَدَّقَ بِهِۦ فَهُوَ كَفَّارَةٌ لَّهُۥ ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

This ayah provides a concrete case study, a practical example of the divine law that was given to the Jewish people: وَكَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِيهَآ - "And We ordained upon them in it" - meaning in the Torah. This is the law of just retribution: أَنَّ ٱلنَّفْسَ بِٱلنَّفْسِ - "that a life for a life." The word نَفْس (nafs) here means a person, a soul, a life.

Then comes the detailed specification: وَٱلْعَيْنَ بِٱلْعَيْنِ - "and an eye for an eye," وَٱلْأَنفَ بِٱلْأَنفِ - "and a nose for a nose," وَٱلْأُذُنَ بِٱلْأُذُنِ - "and an ear for an ear," وَٱلسِّنَّ بِٱلسِّنِّ - "and a tooth for a tooth." This is the famous principle of equivalent justice, ensuring that punishment matches the crime, preventing both excessive retaliation and inadequate accountability.

The principle extends further: وَٱلْجُرُوحَ قِصَاصٌ - "and all wounds are subject to qisas," to legal retribution. The word قِصَاص (qisas) comes from the root meaning to track or follow precisely - the punishment follows the crime exactly, neither exceeding it nor falling short.

But then comes a beautiful dimension of mercy within this justice: فَمَن تَصَدَّقَ بِهِۦ - "Then whoever forgives it as charity." Imagine someone has cut off your ear, and according to the law, you have the right to exact the same injury upon them in a court setting. But you choose to say, "No, I forgive him." This is صَدَقَة (sadaqah), an act of charity.

The reward for this forgiveness is immediate and spiritual: فَهُوَ كَفَّارَةٌ لَّهُۥ - "it is an expiation for him." The word كَفَّارَةٌ (kaffarah) comes from كَفَرَ, which literally means to cover or bury. Through this act of forgiveness, some of your own sins are buried, covered over, expiated. You won't have to answer for them on the Day of Judgment. This creates a powerful incentive for forgiveness even within a system of strict justice.

Some scholars suggest a dual meaning here - that the one who committed the wrong, if given the option to offer charity instead of facing physical retribution, could also find expiation through that act. Both interpretations encourage mercy and forgiveness within the framework of justice.

The ayah concludes with the second variation of the warning: وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ - "And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed, then those are the wrongdoers." Notice the specific word used here: ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (the wrongdoers), the unjust ones. Why this particular term in this ayah? Because this ayah deals specifically with justice - with qisas, with ensuring fair retribution and protecting people's rights. When you abandon Allah's system of justice for something else, you become unjust by definition. One of the primary benefits of Sharia is establishing justice in society. To reject it is to reject justice itself.

translation: And We sent, following in their footsteps, Jesus, the son of Mary, confirming that which came before him in the Torah; and We gave him the Gospel, in which was guidance and light and confirming that which preceded it of the Torah as guidance and instruction for the righteous
commentary:
وَقَفَّيْنَا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم بِعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْإِنجِيلَ فِيهِ هُدًى وَنُورٌ وَمُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَهُدًى وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ

The discussion of the Torah and its prophets continues into the next phase of revelation: وَقَفَّيْنَا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم - "And We sent in their footsteps." The word أَثَر (athar) refers to traces, like footprints left in desert sand. This imagery is powerful - it suggests immediate succession, following directly in the same path. Allah ﷻ sent بِعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ - Isa (AS), the son of Maryam.

By using the phrase قَفَّيْنَا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم - sending him in their footsteps - Allah ﷻ establishes that Isa (AS) was a prophet just like the prophets of the Israelites before him. He wasn't a completely different kind of prophet that was unrecognizable or outside their tradition. He was part of the same legacy, the same chain of prophecy. This is important because the Jews rejected him outright, but this expression emphasizes he was following the exact same prophetic pattern they had always known.

His mission is described as: مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ - "confirming what was before him of the Torah." The phrase لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ literally means "what was between his hands" or "in front of him," but as a time reference, it indicates what preceded him. Just as in English we say "he stands before me" meaning in front of me in space, but "what came before" meaning earlier in time - Arabic uses بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ in the same way.

Isa (AS) came as a confirmer of the Torah. This is crucial to understand because Christians later developed the belief that Christ came to dissolve the Law, that through his blood, the Law was abandoned and replaced by grace. But the Quran makes it clear: he came مُصَدِّقًا - as a confirmer, upholding the laws, the halal and haram, the judgments of the Book. If you read even what's preserved in the New Testament of what Isa (AS) said to the Jews and their rabbis, you find him constantly, unapologetically calling them out for manipulating and playing with Allah's law.

Then Allah ﷻ says: وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْإِنجِيلَ - "And We gave him the Injeel" - what is known as the Gospel or New Testament. فِيهِ هُدًى وَنُورٌ - "In it is guidance and light." Just as with the Torah, Allah ﷻ describes the Injeel as containing guidance and light. But notice what follows immediately: وَمُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ - "and confirming what was before it from the Torah."

This is repeated twice in this ayah - once describing Isa (AS) himself as a confirmer of the Torah, and then describing the Injeel itself as a confirmer of the Torah. The Injeel did not come to replace or abolish the Torah; it came to confirm it, to support it, to continue the same divine guidance.

The ayah concludes by describing the specific nature of the Injeel's guidance: وَهُدًى وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ - "and guidance and counsel for those who have taqwa." The word مَوْعِظَةً (maw'idhah) implies spiritual counsel, heart-penetrating advice. This indicates the Injeel's particular emphasis - while it confirmed the law of the Torah, its special character was more spiritual in nature, focused on internal reformation and consciousness of Allah ﷻ. It was هُدًى - guidance for the path - and مَوْعِظَةً - deep spiritual counsel - specifically لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ - for those who want to be mindful of Allah, who don't want to displease Him, who are working to develop taqwa.

This sequence of ayahs establishes the continuity of divine revelation: the Torah with its comprehensive law, implemented by prophet after prophet, followed by Isa (AS) and the Injeel, which confirmed that law while adding spiritual depth and counsel. All of it points to the same reality: Allah ﷻ has always guided humanity through revealed law, and those who turn away from it, regardless of which scripture they were given, have abandoned the path of faith.

translation: And let the People of the Gospel judge by what Allah has revealed therein. And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed - then it is those who are the defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَلْيَحْكُمْ أَهْلُ ٱلْإِنجِيلِ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ

Allah ﷻ commands: "Let the people of the Injeel pass verdicts according to what Allah ﷻ sent down in it." This directive establishes a profound reality—the Injeel, the revelation given to Isa (AS), also contained the rule of Allah ﷻ. The Gospel was calling the followers of Isa (AS) to obey the laws of Allah ﷻ.

There is a deep irony here. Many Christians today claim they don't know the law of God, asserting that divine law has been dissolved or is no longer applicable. They maintain certain ethical principles—kindness, goodness to parents, truthfulness, honesty—but the comprehensive legal framework revealed to Musa (AS) and reaffirmed in the Injeel has been completely abandoned. The dietary laws, the Sabbath regulations, the detailed instructions about halal and haram—these have been set aside.

When someone violates Allah's ﷻ command by refusing to implement what they've been ordered to do, choosing instead to follow something else, this constitutes more than simple disobedience. If you leave the justice of Allah ﷻ and become oppressive, that's dhulm. But when you're commanded to do something and you respond with "I don't have to do it"—that's fisq. That, by definition, is corruption.

This is why we find the word فَاسِق (fasiq) used with Iblis. The Quran states: فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ—"He violated the commandment of his Master." The deviated Christian theology that Muslims encountered during the time of the Prophet ﷺ is being labeled as fisq. The reason? They abandoned the law of Allah ﷻ, claiming, "I don't need to obey the law; I'm already saved."

translation: And We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], the Book in truth, confirming that which preceded it of the Scripture and as a criterion over it. So judge between them by what Allah has revealed and do not follow their inclinations away from what has come to you of the truth. To each of you We prescribed a law and a method. Had Allah willed, He would have made you one nation [united in religion], but [He intended] to test you in what He has given you; so race to [all that is] good. To Allah is your return all together, and He will [then] inform you concerning that over which you used to differ
commentary:
وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَمُهَيْمِنًا عَلَيْهِ ۖ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ عَمَّا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنَا مِنكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَمِنْهَاجًا ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِى مَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۖ فَٱسْتَبِقُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

Allah ﷻ declares: "We have sent the Book down to you with purpose"—meaning the Quran. This Book مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ—"confirms what is before it from the Book." The Quran itself validates and confirms what came before it from previous scriptures.

We can think of the Injeel almost as an additional set of surahs that joined with the Torah. For the Jewish people, it was a continuous and completed revelation. Part of it was revealed to Musa (AS), then an additional installation came with Dawud (AS) in the Zabur, and the final installation for them came with Isa (AS). With that, the book for the Children of Israel was complete.

### The Quran as Muhaymin

The Quran came as confirmation, but Allah ﷻ uses a remarkable word to describe it: مُهَيْمِنًا عَلَيْهِ (muhayminan alayhi). This word appears twice in the Quran—once as a name of Allah ﷻ (Al-Muhaymin), and once as a name for the Quran itself, right here in this ayah.

Let's examine this word. مُهَيْمِن comes from the root هَيْمَنَ (haymana), though some scholars argue it derives from أَمَنَ (amana)—relating to safety and peace. The Arabs used to say about a bird protecting its young by spreading its wings over them: هَيْمَنَ. The word literally means to protect, to guard, to safeguard, to keep peaceful. Some connect it to أَمِنَ, linking it to concepts of peace, safety, and protection.

In this profound verse, Allah ﷻ establishes a philosophy for how we interact with previous revelation. We now have an opportunity—especially with Christian scholars becoming Muslim—to create a new era of Quranic scholarship. This involves giving the Quran its rightful role as muhaymin over previous revelations, not through superficial engagement but through serious, dedicated scholarship.

### Understanding Previous Revelation

When Jewish history appears in the Quran, we often have opinions from certain Sahaba (RA) about what happened and the details of those events. But the Quran discusses these matters very briefly. It tells us the Children of Israel crossed the water, but doesn't specify how long it took, where they came from, or how far behind Pharaoh was. The Quran mentions nine signs given to Musa (AS), but offers just a word for each: frogs, blood, locusts.

Some Sahaba (RA) had been People of the Book before accepting Islam—some were Christian, others Jewish. These companions often offered explanations based on their previous knowledge. Later generations sometimes consulted Jewish sources and rabbis, gathering what became known as الإسرائيليات (Isra'iliyyat)—historical accounts from Jewish and Christian sources.

The Prophet ﷺ gave us guidance in a hadith about these sources: we can take from them so long as they fill gaps in historical knowledge without contradicting what we have in our Book. We take such information with caution, knowing that Allah ﷻ says about the People of the Book: يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِ—"They remove words from their places"—meaning alterations have been made.

The critical principle comes from the word muhaymin. We don't go to the Torah or Gospel to validate what the Quran says. The Quran is not protected by what those books say—rather, those scriptures are validated by what the Quran declares. The Quran is the muhaymin, the protector and guardian. In Western Oriental studies, because the Torah came first chronologically, some argue the Quran should be validated by it. The Muslim position is the opposite: the Torah will be validated by the Quran. So long as we understand which book holds the dominant position—which is the secure, protected text—there's no harm in engaging such research at a high scholarly level.

### The Command to Judge

فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ—"Pass judgment between them with what Allah ﷻ sent down." وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ—"Don't follow their empty desires" عَمَّا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ—"in regards to whatever has come to you from the truth."

### Shir'ah and Minhaj

لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنَا مِنكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَمِنْهَاجًا—"For everyone, for every nation that received revelation, We have made from among you a shir'ah and a minhaj." These are beautiful, significant words that warrant deep reflection.

شِرْعَة (shir'ah) comes from شَرَعَ, meaning "beginning." It's also used for a journey—specifically, a path taken in a journey that leads to water. For the Arab, water equals life. When you start on a journey that will give you life, you are on a shir'ah. The law of Allah ﷻ is something that will give you life.

This connects beautifully to another ayah: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ—"O you who believe, respond to Allah ﷻ and the Messenger when He calls you to what will give you life." Obedience to Allah ﷻ is like someone in the desert finding the way to water.

Shir'ah also means the beginning of a path. In other words, Shariah is just your beginning and your entry into Islam—it isn't Islam in its entirety. It's the first step. When someone becomes Muslim, you teach them to pray. That's the beginning of their journey, their orientation. The orientation and foundation of our relationship with Allah ﷻ is Shariah. But Shariah is not the end of our relationship; it's the beginning. Once you abide by the Shariah, you start experiencing its sweetness and drawing closer to Allah ﷻ. Then you start looking for wasilah—the means to get even closer, as Allah ﷻ commands: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَابْتَغُوا إِلَيْهِ الْوَسِيلَةَ.

The laws of our religion are a starting point. It's a tragedy when we reduce all of religion to merely laws. Laws are your orientation, your foundation.

مِنْهَاج (minhaj) refers to the entire path—and inherent in its meaning is that this path must be clear. The path leading to water (shir'ah) may or may not be clear; it might be difficult. So Allah ﷻ didn't just say "I give you a shir'ah, access to water." He added وَمِنْهَاجًا—"and I made sure the path is clear."

Minhaj also means the way in which you traverse, the method by which you travel. The modern translation would be "methodology." In contemporary Arabic, curriculum is called minhaj because it's the method by which you receive education.

Allah ﷻ didn't just give us laws. He gave each prophet, each nation, a way to achieve success. We have laws regarding alcohol, food, clothing, prayer—these are the Shariah. But the Prophet ﷺ was given a complete way forward. He began in a society that didn't believe, completely powerless except for Allah's ﷻ Book. Yet somehow Allah ﷻ empowered him, showing him a way to gather a small group of people. Then He showed them a way to strengthen unity among themselves and become unified. Then a way to call others to this religion. Then a way to migrate to another city. Then a way to make that city a fortified community—step by step, from age forty to sixty-three. Allah ﷻ showed him a complete, clear way to success. A methodology to success. This is minhaj.

Shir'ah represents the laws that make our lives work properly and give us real life. Minhaj is the way the religion is supposed to be established, the way it becomes a means of strength. Every prophet was given something of shir'ah, and every prophet was given a different aspect of minhaj according to their nation and its needs.

The minhaj of Musa (AS) differs from the minhaj of Isa (AS), just as their Shariah differs. The minhaj of Nuh (AS) was straightforward: call people for 950 years. The Prophet Muhammad's ﷺ minhaj included elements from all previous prophets. It included the patient calling like Nuh (AS), the migration like Ibrahim (AS), and the command to fight like Musa (AS). The scholars say Ibrahim (AS) took pieces from all the legacies of previous prophets and combined them into one complete prophet ﷺ—a complete shir'ah and a complete minhaj.

The study of the Seerah is vital because it offers us the minhaj. The Quran and hadith provide the shir'ah. The study of the Prophet's ﷺ struggle gives us the minhaj. We must appreciate the role of minhaj in studying the Quran.

### Divine Wisdom in Diversity

وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً—"Had Allah ﷻ wanted, He would have made you a single nation." وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِى مَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ—"However, He made you different nations so He can test you in whatever He has given you."

Whatever prophet you were sent, whatever era you were placed in, whatever century, whatever society—each of you will face a different test. Each of us is given unique challenges.

فَٱسْتَبِقُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ—"Compete in doing good things." Here's the one commonality: no matter what year you came in, what prophet you followed, there's one thing you all have in common—you will compete in doing good. The motivation behind that competition? إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ—"To Allah ﷻ is your final place of return anyway."

جَمِيعًا—"All together." This word carries beautiful implications. Despite the different shir'ahs and minhajs—the Muslims under Musa (AS), the believers under Isa (AS), the followers of Nuh (AS)—we're all going to have one giant gathering before Allah ﷻ. Muslims living in 2012, Muslims from the fourth century, Muslims at the time of the Prophet ﷺ, and the believers under Nuh (AS)—everyone together. You'll be able to ask, "So what was your minhaj like?" And you can ask Nuh (AS) himself—he'll be right there.

فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ—"Then He will thoroughly inform you of what you used to disagree about." That's the remarkable thing. We think of all these prophets and their followers as having created separate religions through deviation. The prophets get blamed for this disunity. But Allah ﷻ is telling us they're all going to be gathered together. The prophets didn't create the differences—the followers did.

Are there different paths to the same water source? Yes. Everybody is given a shir'ah, but they all lead to the same fountain.

translation: And judge, [O Muhammad], between them by what Allah has revealed and do not follow their inclinations and beware of them, lest they tempt you away from some of what Allah has revealed to you. And if they turn away - then know that Allah only intends to afflict them with some of their [own] sins. And indeed, many among the people are defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَأَنِ ٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ وَٱحْذَرْهُمْ أَن يَفْتِنُوكَ عَنۢ بَعْضِ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْكَ ۖ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُصِيبَهُم بِبَعْضِ ذُنُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَفَـٰسِقُونَ

وَأَنِ ٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ—"And that you should pass verdicts among them with what Allah ﷻ has sent down." Notice how this command comes repeatedly. Why such emphasis? Because the conquest has already been done. The military struggle is over. Now the thing to do is rule by Allah's ﷻ law. There's no obstacle left. The work now is to pass judgments according to what Allah ﷻ has revealed.

وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ—"Don't follow their empty, vain desires" that would weaken the religion in any way.

وَٱحْذَرْهُمْ أَن يَفْتِنُوكَ عَنۢ بَعْضِ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْكَ—"Watch out for them, that they put you in difficulty regarding something Allah ﷻ has sent down." In other words, if they start asking questions or trying to go in circles to avoid obeying Allah's ﷻ command, be on guard.

### Why People Turn Away

فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُصِيبَهُم بِبَعْضِ ذُنُوبِهِمْ—"If they turn away, then you had better know Allah ﷻ only intends to afflict them because of some of the sins they've done."

Allah ﷻ is teaching us a secret about why people leave Islam. The reality is that some people do leave. Yes, there were those who endured tremendous persecution and never wavered—and there are such people today. But there are also those who left, and they exist now more than ever, particularly among young people.

Allah ﷻ explains: when they turn away, He wants to punish them. They don't deserve guidance anymore. Why? Because even when they had guidance, they committed serious sins without care for Allah's ﷻ verdict. They showed that they didn't value the gift they'd been given.

وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَفَـٰسِقُونَ—"And the truth of the matter is, without doubt, a huge multitude of people are inherently corrupt." Most people are corrupt—even if they're nominally Muslim. Just because someone has entered Islam and started the journey doesn't mean they want to continue on it.

That's the other crucial aspect of minhaj. Shir'ah is where you start. Minhaj is staying on the path—continuing the journey, not just beginning it.

translation: Then is it the judgement of [the time of] ignorance they desire? But who is better than Allah in judgement for a people who are certain [in faith]
commentary:
أَفَحُكْمَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ يَبْغُونَ ۚ وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ حُكْمًا لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ

"Is it then the judgment of ignorance that they desire? And who is better than Allah ﷻ in judgment for a people who are certain?"

Allah ﷻ poses a powerful rhetorical question: is this really what you want to return to? The word الْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ (jahiliyyah) has multiple dimensions. One meaning of jahiliyyah is جهل (jahl) - ignorance, being alone in darkness. Another profound meaning is overwhelming emotion, impulsiveness in decision-making. When someone acts purely on whatever comes to mind without reflection, they're in a state of jahiliyyah.

This connects directly to wisdom. When you're in a rush or being impulsive, can you use hikma? No. Modern marketing demonstrates this brilliantly. The entire industry is designed to make people not use wisdom when buying products. They want you to be impulsive. Marketing works by suggesting you shouldn't think - "let us do the thinking for you." 

Consider the simple example of toothpaste commercials. You see the entire toothbrush covered in a beautiful ribbon of paste. But do you know how much you actually need when brushing? About a third of that amount. The rest is waste. But they want you to see the entire brush covered so you use more, run out quicker and buy another tube faster. You don't even question it - that's modern jahiliyyah.

This is how entertainment, media and consumerism work today. You see a movie trailer and suddenly you can't get it out of your head: "I have to see that. I have to see that." It becomes an obsession. This is jahiliyyah making judgments for people's lives.

The pressure manifests in larger life decisions too. Someone thinks, "I'm still renting an apartment. I haven't bought a house yet." But where does it say you must buy a house? Why is that the measure of success? Once you buy the house, it becomes: "What about the retirement account? What about this? What about that?" People keep moving to the next thing, the next thing, constantly driven by external pressures rather than divine guidance.

Allah ﷻ asks: is this really what you want? The pre-Islamic era, the Dark Ages - do you want to go back to those days? وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ حُكْمًا - "And who is better than Allah in judgment?" Who will be more beautiful, more excellent in giving verdicts? لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ - for a nation that is truly convinced, for people with genuine certainty.

When you have yaqeen, when you're thoroughly convinced that Allah's verdicts come from Him, then there's no doubt that whatever He says is the best possible thing for you. You're not missing out on life. You're not missing out on anything. By obeying Allah's judgment, you're actually gaining everything. 

This connects beautifully to what Allah ﷻ said earlier in the surah: يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَكُمْ وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ - "Allah wants to make things clear for you and guide you." The purpose of Sharia is to lighten your burden, to make your life easy. But this understanding is only accessible to those thoroughly convinced that this guidance truly comes from Allah ﷻ. They know that Allah ﷻ is looking out for them better than they can look out for themselves.

translation: O you who have believed, do not take the Jews and the Christians as allies. They are [in fact] allies of one another. And whoever is an ally to them among you - then indeed, he is [one] of them. Indeed, Allah guides not the wrongdoing people
commentary:
۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْيَهُودَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۘ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُم مِّنكُمْ فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنْهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

"O you who have believed, do not take the Jews and the Christians as allies. They are allies of one another. And whoever is an ally to them among you - then indeed, he is one of them. Indeed, Allah ﷻ does not guide the wrongdoing people."

This ayah is commonly quoted and misunderstood, often cited by critics who claim Muslims are commanded not to have any friendship with non-Muslims. Context is essential here. We need to understand this guidance for ourselves, not necessarily to explain it to others, but to grasp its proper application.

By the time these ayahs were revealed, victory had already come. Allah ﷻ had already stated that the People of the Book were hopeless - they knew Islam would not be overcome. The only strategy left for them was to cause cracks in the Muslim civilization, to take away a little bit of a value here, a teaching there, slowly undermining the community from within.

Allah ﷻ is instructing the believers: don't look to them for protection anymore. Don't turn to them as your protectors and allies in the political and strategic sense. بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ - they are friends and protectors to each other.

Some scholars, particularly non-Muslim historians, have challenged this statement, claiming it's historically inaccurate because Jews and Christians have traditionally opposed each other. The Spanish Inquisition serves as a clear example of this historical animosity. So what does Allah ﷻ mean when He says they are allies to one another?

The principle at work is simple: the enemy of my enemy is my friend. Even if Jews and Christians had deep theological and historical disagreements, once they faced a common opponent - Islam - they were willing to set aside their differences. We can see modern manifestations of this in various geopolitical alliances. When there's a bigger perceived threat, old enemies hold hands and say, "Let's deal with this common challenge first. We'll sort out our theological differences later."

وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُم مِّنكُمْ فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنْهُمْ - "And whoever allies with them from among you, then he is one of them." This means his true allegiances become clear. Perhaps there were hypocrites secretly maintaining connections with these groups. When they're discovered in these alliances, their real loyalties are exposed. إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - certainly Allah ﷻ does not guide the wrongdoing nation.

translation: So you see those in whose hearts is disease hastening into [association with] them, saying, "We are afraid a misfortune may strike us." But perhaps Allah will bring conquest or a decision from Him, and they will become, over what they have been concealing within themselves, regretful
commentary:
فَتَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِيهِمْ يَقُولُونَ نَخْشَىٰٓ أَن تُصِيبَنَا دَآئِرَةٌ ۚ فَعَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِٱلْفَتْحِ أَوْ أَمْرٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِۦ فَيُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَسَرُّوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ

"So you see those in whose hearts is disease hastening into friendship with them, saying, 'We fear that a turn of fortune may strike us.' But perhaps Allah ﷻ will bring conquest or a decision from Him, and they will become regretful over what they have been concealing within themselves."

Allah ﷻ just mentioned the Jews and Christians, and now shifts to describe another group: فَتَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ - "Then you will see those who have a disease in their hearts." Who are these people? They're the ones who يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِيهِمْ - they rush to them, they hurry to maintain these alliances.

Their justification reveals their spiritual sickness: يَقُولُونَ نَخْشَىٰٓ أَن تُصِيبَنَا دَآئِرَةٌ - "They say, 'We fear some turn of events might come upon us.'" Their reasoning was pragmatic but faithless: "We should keep good relations with the Romans. We should maintain ties with the Abyssinians. You never know how circumstances might change. We might need their help later."

This thinking exposes a fundamental flaw in their faith. They believe protection and security come from political alliances rather than from Allah ﷻ. Their trust is misplaced, and Allah ﷻ responds to this hidden conviction.

فَعَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِٱلْفَتْحِ أَوْ أَمْرٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِۦ - "Then it very well may be that Allah will bring victory or some decision from Him." When Allah's victory comes or when He makes a decisive decree, فَيُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَسَرُّوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ - "they will become extremely humiliated and embarrassed about what they were hiding inside themselves."

What were they concealing? Their conviction that protection would come from connections with non-Muslim powers rather than from Allah ﷻ. When the situation becomes clear and Allah's decree manifests, their secret doubts and misplaced trust will be exposed, leaving them filled with shame and regret.

translation: And those who believe will say, "Are these the ones who swore by Allah their strongest oaths that indeed they were with you?" Their deeds have become worthless, and they have become losers
commentary:
وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ۙ إِنَّهُمْ لَمَعَكُمْ ۚ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ

"And those who believe will say, 'Are these the ones who swore by Allah ﷻ their strongest oaths that indeed they were with you?' Their deeds have become worthless, and they have become losers."

When these hypocrites are finally exposed, the believers' reaction is one of shocked recognition: أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ - "Are these the people who were swearing by Allah ﷻ as hard as they could?" The phrase جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ captures the intensity - they were making tremendous effort in their oaths.

This is one of the defining characteristics of a hypocrite: excessive swearing. They swear frequently and passionately because they lack genuine credibility. Think of it like a dishonest salesman: "I swear by my children, if I had the money, I would buy this car right now!" When someone starts swearing like that, you know they have no other way to establish trust. The oath becomes a last resort to manufacture believability.

These hypocrites would come to the Prophet ﷺ declaring, "We bear witness that you are absolutely the Messenger of Allah ﷻ! I swear we are with you!" Their oaths were emphatic, their protestations loud. إِنَّهُمْ لَمَعَكُمْ - "Indeed they are definitely with you" - this is what they would claim with such conviction.

But when their true allegiances are revealed, the believers reflect in astonishment: these are the same people who used to swear so passionately about their loyalty. حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ - all of their deeds have been canceled out, rendered worthless. فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ - and they have become complete losers, having wasted their efforts and sacrificed their position in both worlds.

translation: O you who have believed, whoever of you should revert from his religion - Allah will bring forth [in place of them] a people He will love and who will love Him [who are] humble toward the believers, powerful against the disbelievers; they strive in the cause of Allah and do not fear the blame of a critic. That is the favor of Allah; He bestows it upon whom He wills. And Allah is all-Encompassing and Knowing
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَن يَرْتَدَّ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِۦ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِى ٱللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُۥٓ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ يُجَـٰهِدُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَخَافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لَآئِمٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ

"O you who have believed, whoever of you turns away from his religion - Allah ﷻ will bring forth a people He will love and who will love Him, humble toward the believers, powerful against the disbelievers; they strive in the cause of Allah ﷻ and do not fear the blame of a critic. That is the favor of Allah ﷻ; He bestows it upon whom He wills. And Allah ﷻ is all-Encompassing and Knowing."

This profound ayah addresses two dimensions of turning away from faith. يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَن يَرْتَدَّ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِۦ - "O you who have believed, whoever turns away from his religion."

There are two ways to understand this turning away: leaving Islam itself, and leaving the active pursuit of iman. Iman is something that constantly fluctuates. Our faith is either growing or diminishing - it never remains stagnant. Islam, on the other hand, is more binary: you either have it or you don't. One reading of this ayah concerns those who completely abandon Islam. The other reading addresses those who turn away from actively pursuing and strengthening their iman, who no longer walk the path toward Allah ﷻ. If you're not moving toward Allah ﷻ, what's happening to your faith? It's necessarily turning away.

Allah's response is both a warning and a promise: فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِى ٱللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ - "Then soon Allah will bring a people." This isn't a threat to the religion itself - Islam doesn't need any particular group of people. Rather, it's a statement about Allah's infinite resources. If one group turns away, Allah ﷻ will replace them with others who fulfill the mission with excellence.

The characteristics of this replacement generation are beautiful and profound:

**يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُۥٓ** - "He will love them and they will love Him." This mutual love is the foundation of everything that follows. When you truly love someone, you want to do more and more for them. Your gifts increase, the time you spend increases, your concern for them grows. Think about any relationship - when love is strong, so are all the expressions of that love. When love diminishes, so do the calls, the time together, the thoughtfulness. Parents and children, spouses after twenty years of marriage - when love remains strong, the care and attention remain strong.

This is why many scholars argue this ayah is fundamentally about iman rather than just the formal category of Islam. Islam requires submission to orders, which is the starting point. But iman involves this deep love, this yearning for closeness to Allah ﷻ. That's the higher station.

**أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** - "Humble toward the believers." The word أَذِلَّةٍ (adhillah) comes from dhull, which means extremely low, someone without self-respect in front of another. It describes someone who humbles themselves completely. These believers, out of their love for Allah ﷻ, will be profoundly humble when dealing with other believers.

Their love for Allah ﷻ is so powerful that when they encounter anyone else who says "La ilaha illAllah," they experience overwhelming affection. Anyone who testifies that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah ﷺ becomes instantly beloved to them. This genuine love translates into service. The word أَذِلَّةٍ also evokes the image of a riding animal that gets used constantly, that people rely on, that bears burdens without complaint. These believers become like that - always available to serve other Muslims, constantly looking for ways to help, putting themselves at the disposal of the believing community out of pure love for Allah ﷻ.

This is the opposite of what we often see today. Many Muslims are harsh, unforgiving, cunning, judgmental and condescending toward each other, while being excessively accommodating to non-Muslims. That's completely backward. We're not commanded to hate disbelievers, but we absolutely must have an overwhelming sense of courtesy, service and genuine affection toward fellow Muslims.

**أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ** - "Powerful against the disbelievers." The word أَعِزَّةٍ (a'izzah) means showing authority and power, standing firm with dignity. This doesn't mean being aggressive toward every non-Muslim you meet. The context matters immensely. These were disbelievers who had held authority and power for a long time, who had persecuted and oppressed the Muslims. Now that the tables had turned, these believers would stand firm, reminding them through their strength and dignity that power now belonged to the believers because Allah ﷻ had granted it to them.

Every time they interact with believers, they humble themselves. Every time they deal with hostile disbelievers, they show strength and firmness. The time for soft diplomacy and appeasement had passed. Now was the time for dignified strength.

**يُجَـٰهِدُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ** - "They strive in the path of Allah ﷻ." This characteristic is especially interesting in context. Victory had already come, so what jihad remained? What struggles were left to undertake? The hypocrites would ask, "What are you doing now? The time for struggle is over. You can relax now. Why are you going out of your way to make these efforts?"

The modern manifestation of this is strikingly relevant. When someone begins taking their religion seriously - perhaps wanting to study Quran deeply, learn Arabic properly, or dedicate significant time to Islamic knowledge - they face immediate pushback. "Study Quran on weekends for twenty minutes. That's enough. Why are you always at the masjid? Why do you want to study Arabic full-time? Are you crazy? What about your career? Nobody's going to marry you like this. Why are you doing this? You're destroying your family. Have you lost your common sense? This is the worst decision you could ever make. Who's brainwashing you? Someone must have done magic on you!"

Young Muslims face this constantly. Someone wants to change majors to study humanities, Islamic studies, or something that serves the community rather than becoming another professional in a cubicle. The response? "We had such high hopes for you. You were going to make us proud as an accountant, a programmer, a doctor. What do you mean you want to study political science, history, literature, comparative religion, sociology? What are you going to do with that? How will you feed a family?"

You hear these criticisms from everyone - cousins, parents, elders, extended family. And there's sometimes an element of truth in their concerns. Sometimes people do become unbalanced when they take Islamic work seriously, neglecting practical responsibilities. Someone working for the dean and suddenly deciding they don't want education because "I just want to work for the sake of Allah ﷻ" - that's not wisdom, that's foolishness. This is precisely why parents worry - because some young people lose their balance.

If you eat food, that's dunya. Your parents work and engage with dunya so you can eat, so you can have the luxury of complaining about dunya. Get some balance in your life. But even after you achieve that balance, you will still hear criticism. People will still try to discourage you. Their words can genuinely hurt, taking the wind out of your sails, putting a serious dent in your motivation.

**وَلَا يَخَافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لَآئِمٍ** - "And they do not fear the blame of any critic." This is the defining characteristic that allows them to persist. They don't fear criticism from any blamer, regardless of who it comes from - whether enemy or loved one, stranger or family member. They're mentally prepared for blame and criticism. They're mentally prepared for opposition.

**ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ** - "That is the favor of Allah ﷻ; He gives it to whoever He wants." This entire package of qualities - the love, the humility toward believers, the strength against oppressors, the striving in Allah's path, the imperviousness to criticism - all of this is a gift from Allah ﷻ. He bestows it upon whomever He wills.

**وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ** - "And Allah ﷻ is all-Encompassing and Knowing." His vastness means He has infinite capacity to grant this favor. His knowledge means He knows exactly who deserves it, who will use it properly, and how to distribute His gifts with perfect wisdom. Allah ﷻ encompasses all things and knows all things, so we trust that His selection of those who receive this tremendous favor is absolutely perfect.

translation: Your ally is none but Allah and [therefore] His Messenger and those who have believed - those who establish prayer and give zakah, and they bow [in worship]
commentary:
إِنَّمَا وَلِيُّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُمْ رَٰكِعُونَ

Without a doubt, your only real protective friend is Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ and those who believe. But the phrase "those who believe" is broad - it includes everyone from the weakest to the strongest believer. So Allah ﷻ adds a qualification: الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُمْ رَٰكِعُونَ - those who establish the prayer and give zakah while they are in a state of humility.

The word رَٰكِعُونَ (raki'un) here is significant. While ruku' is a physical position within salah, the original meaning of the word relates to humility and being humbled. What Allah ﷻ is describing here are the truly humble believers who accomplish these acts of worship with the right attitude. It's not just about the actions of establishing prayer and giving zakah - it's about doing these things while maintaining deep humility before Allah ﷻ.

translation: And whoever is an ally of Allah and His Messenger and those who have believed - indeed, the party of Allah - they will be the predominant
commentary:
وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَإِنَّ حِزْبَ ٱللَّهِ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ

Whoever takes as a protective friend Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ and those who have true iman - as described in the previous ayah - then the party of Allah ﷻ, they are the ones who will be dominant.

The term حِزْب (hizb) refers to a strong group with a unified agenda. فَإِنَّ حِزْبَ ٱللَّهِ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ - the party of Allah ﷻ are the ones who will ultimately be victorious. This term "Hizb Allah" has obviously become associated with a particular political group in Lebanon, and now when we hear it, it's difficult to think of anyone else. But the Quranic term isn't restricted to any single group. It would be unfair for any organization to take ownership of a Quranic term and claim it exclusively refers to them.

This is a description that belongs to all of us who truly believe, who take Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ as our protectors, and who align ourselves with the genuine believers. We should make dua that Allah ﷻ makes us from among Hizb Allah - not restricted to some group in Lebanon, but rather all those who believe, who befriend Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ and the true believers.

translation: O you who have believed, take not those who have taken your religion in ridicule and amusement among the ones who were given the Scripture before you nor the disbelievers as allies. And fear Allah, if you should [truly] be believers
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَكُمْ هُزُوًا وَلَعِبًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَٱلْكُفَّارَ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

O you who believe, don't take as protective friends... who? The middle portion of this ayah tells us exactly who. Don't take الَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَكُمْ هُزُوًا وَلَعِبًا - those who have taken your religion as a joke and as play مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ - from among the People of the Book who came before you وَٱلْكُفَّارَ - and also from among the disbelievers. Don't take any of them as protective friends and close allies.

Now one door after another is being closed. First, we were told: here is your protective friend. Then: these people are not your protective friends. Don't turn toward them. Don't rush toward them as others rushed toward them. What's happening here is that these doors are being closed for the Muslims of Madinah and this newly expanding Muslim community. At this point in Islamic history, allegiances and alliances were everything, especially for an exponentially growing population. If these policies weren't strictly established, there would have been chaos because the lines between Islam and other religions would become blurred.

There's another crucial reason why these boundaries were so important early on. You had people from Christian backgrounds, Jewish backgrounds, and mushrik backgrounds who had newly entered Islam but hadn't yet received thorough education in the religion. In the early days of Islam, the Sahaba (RA) could provide private instruction easily - taking somebody into their fold one-on-one, one-on-three, one-on-four. But now hundreds of thousands were becoming Muslims, so you couldn't have private instruction for everyone. 

This meant that Islamic education and orientation were weak among many new Muslims. At this vulnerable stage, if people started making close friends and establishing deep trust with Christians and Jews, their own understanding of religion would start getting confused with those other religious perspectives. There wouldn't be purity in the faith. This posed a serious threat to befriend and get too cozy with those communities at that particular point - it was a fragile state. Victory itself was fragile, which is why these strict boundaries were placed here in Al-Maidah, and you'll find even stricter guidelines in Surah At-Tawbah.

The reason is that it did pose a direct threat to the identity of the Muslim. Now, the same principle applies today - and Allah ﷻ knows best - to a Muslim community that isn't grounded in its religious education. If we're raising a generation of Muslims who don't know much about their religion, and they're constantly hanging out with atheist, Christian, Jewish, Hindu, or other friends, the more they spend time with them, the more the boundaries blur. The ideas of where Islam stops and where those other ideologies begin start getting confused. 

As they grow up and finally learn more about Islam, it becomes alien to them. It feels foreign. Why? Because they heard different perspectives from their friends growing up, and they got used to that being "normal." Now when they're learning more about Islam, they think Islam is weird. "It's not what I thought it was. I thought it was a lot more normal than that." Instead of being turned off from those alien ideas, they become alienated from Islam itself.

Just as we talked about our children being easily influenced, people who are new to Islam are also easily swayed by outside forces. That's the context of these ayahs - protecting them at that particular vulnerable stage.

The ayah also specifies not just any People of the Book, but الَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَكُمْ هُزُوًا وَلَعِبًا - those who take your religion as a joke and something to play with. If we examine a broader lesson from this, it wouldn't even be restricted to attitudes toward Islam specifically. We're living in a time now where many people - coworkers, college students, even friends from Muslim backgrounds - think religion itself is a joke. Religion itself is obsolete in their minds. Because religion is a joke to them, everything connected to religion becomes open to scrutiny, criticism, and mockery. It's a game, something to toy around with. Allah ﷻ says you shouldn't be closely connected to people with that attitude, and you shouldn't rely on people like that.

Then, of course, the ayah mentions the staunch disbelievers as well. The command concludes: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ - and be cautious of Allah, protect yourselves from making Allah ﷻ unhappy if you are truly believers.

translation: And when you call to prayer, they take it in ridicule and amusement. That is because they are a people who do not use reason
commentary:
وَإِذَا نَادَيْتُمْ إِلَى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ٱتَّخَذُوهَا هُزُوًا وَلَعِبًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْقِلُونَ

After establishing the general principle, the Quran now provides a case study - here's exactly how they make the religion into a joke. وَإِذَا نَادَيْتُمْ إِلَى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ - whenever you call to the prayer, meaning when you give the adhan, ٱتَّخَذُوهَا هُزُوًا وَلَعِبًا - they take it as a joke and as play, something to make fun of and toy with.

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْقِلُونَ - that is because they are a people who don't understand, who don't use their intellect.

Consider how many movies today play the adhan in the background as a joke to create comic relief. They'll have characters that are Muslim, and every time they bring them on screen, they play a clip of the adhan in the background, and then the character does something foolish. This has become common now, and we dismiss it saying, "It's just comedy, it's no big deal." But look at what Allah ﷻ chose to highlight. Of all the things He could have mentioned about how they mock the religion, He specifically highlighted people who make fun of the salah and its call. This tells us something about the severity of this particular form of mockery.

translation: Say, "O People of the Scripture, do you resent us except [for the fact] that we have believed in Allah and what was revealed to us and what was revealed before and because most of you are defiantly disobedient
commentary:
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ هَلْ تَنقِمُونَ مِنَّآ إِلَّآ أَنْ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلُ وَأَنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ

Tell them, O People of the Book: هَلْ تَنقِمُونَ مِنَّآ - are you taking vengeance from us? Are you spiteful toward us? The word تَنقِمُونَ (tanqimun) comes from نَقَمَ (naqama), which means to take vengeance, to harbor spite against someone.

إِلَّآ أَنْ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ - for no other reason except that we believed in Allah وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا - and in what has been revealed to us وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلُ - and in what was revealed before? Is that why you're angry with us?

وَأَنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ - And the reality is that most of you have become corrupt. You've become so corrupted that when you see the right thing, it offends you. Is that what's happened?

This is a phenomenon that can occur even within the Muslim community. When corruption spreads widely, the one who does the right thing becomes the object of ridicule, criticism, and even hatred - precisely because they're doing the right thing. They make everybody else stand out. It's like the metaphor of one different-colored sheep in a flock - it stands out and makes the others look different by comparison.

So the corrupt don't criticize the righteous person because that person is out of line. Rather, they resent them for not being out of line like everybody else. "Why can't you just be deviated like the rest of us? That way you won't stand out so much and make us look bad." This is exactly the attitude being described here.

Sometimes this pressure manifests in heartbreaking ways. I've met groups of sisters worried about a friend who stopped wearing hijab. When she stopped wearing it and started hanging out with her old friends, they would come and invite her, but she'd say, "I can't come anymore. They're going to look at me funny. They're going to judge me." So she distances herself further and further.

One of the most powerful things that protects our faith is good company. The one who understands this better than you and I is Shaytan. First, he whispers to disobey. Then he whispers to get away from any company that might remind you of obedience. "Anyone who even looks like what you used to look like - just stay away from them." And the person convinces themselves it's because "they're going to judge me." But what it really is, is a guilty conscience. Being around them reminds you of what you were, and you don't want to deal with that reality. So you distance yourself, and the further you get away, the more isolated you become, and you're spiritually finished.

My teacher gave a powerful example: the Muslim is like a leaf on a tree. When it gets cut off, the life supply is severed. As long as the leaf stays connected to the tree, it receives nourishment. Once it's separated, it's done - there's nothing keeping it alive. That's what happens when we separate ourselves from good company.

translation: Say, "Shall I inform you of [what is] worse than that as penalty from Allah? [It is that of] those whom Allah has cursed and with whom He became angry and made of them apes and pigs and slaves of Taghut. Those are worse in position and further astray from the sound way
commentary:
قُلْ هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُم بِشَرٍّ مِّن ذَٰلِكَ مَثُوبَةً عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ مَن لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ وَغَضِبَ عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْقِرَدَةَ وَٱلْخَنَازِيرَ وَعَبَدَ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا وَأَضَلُّ عَن سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ

Tell them: shall I inform you of something even worse than that - worse as far as the payback and recompense is concerned with Allah ﷻ?

مَن لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ - The one whom Allah ﷻ cursed وَغَضِبَ عَلَيْهِ - and became angry with him. Notice that Allah ﷻ attributed both of these actions to Himself: He cursed him, He became angry with him.

وَجَعَلَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْقِرَدَةَ وَٱلْخَنَازِيرَ - And He made from among them monkeys and pigs. This was a punishment we learn about in previous surahs.

وَعَبَدَ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتَ - And the one who worshiped the Taghut, the rebellious entity that disobeys Allah ﷻ. This phrase is particularly significant because عَبَدَ (abada) - "worshiped" - goes back grammatically to مَن لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ, the one who was cursed. 

What this structure tells us is profound: the worst curse from Allah ﷻ upon someone, the ultimate punishment, is that they become a worshiper of the very entity that disobeys Allah ﷻ. That's the climax of all these punishments mentioned.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا - Those people are far worse as far as where they stand, their position وَأَضَلُّ عَن سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ - and they are the most misled, the furthest away from the even path, the straight path that leads directly to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And when they come to you, they say, "We believe." But they have entered with disbelief [in their hearts], and they have certainly left with it. And Allah is most knowing of what they were concealing
commentary:
وَإِذَا جَآءُوكُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَقَد دَّخَلُوا۟ بِٱلْكُفْرِ وَهُمْ قَدْ خَرَجُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْتُمُونَ

I'll translate this in the present tense because that's how the present-tense verb is used in Arabic - to describe something that happens repeatedly. Whenever they come to you, قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا - they say, "We believe" or "We have believed as well."

وَقَد دَّخَلُوا۟ بِٱلْكُفْرِ - But they had already entered with disbelief, meaning disbelief was already in their hearts when they came وَهُمْ قَدْ خَرَجُوا۟ بِهِۦ - and it is with that very same disbelief that they left.

This is describing a group we learned about in Surah Al-Imran. There was a contingency of Jews who became interested in Islam, and the rabbis saw this as a threat to their authority and community. So they devised a plan: they trained some of their own people to go during the day to the Prophet ﷺ and declare belief in Islam. Then by the afternoon, by the end of the day, they would leave and renounce Islam. The purpose was so that others who were genuinely considering Islam might come back out of the religion, thinking, "Even those who tried it found nothing there and left."

These are the people this ayah is discussing. They would come and say "We believe," but as they're saying it, they already have disbelief in their hearts. And at the end of the day when they leave, they announce, "We gave it a chance, but there's really nothing here. It's a false religion." It's not like they left iman - they left with exactly what they came with. They came with disbelief and they departed with disbelief.

وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْتُمُونَ - And Allah ﷻ knows best what they have been hiding.

translation: And you see many of them hastening into sin and aggression and the devouring of [what is] unlawful. How wretched is what they have been doing
commentary:
وَتَرَىٰ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَأَكْلِهِمُ ٱلسُّحْتَ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

And you will see a huge multitude among them - كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ - many of them يُسَـٰرِعُونَ - rushing, making great efforts, competing with each other فِى ٱلْإِثْمِ - in committing sins وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ - and in increasing animosity and transgression against others.

وَأَكْلِهِمُ ٱلسُّحْتَ - And they rush toward consuming السُّحْت (as-suht). The word suht refers to impermissible things acquired through impermissible means, particularly related to unlawful financial dealings and income. They're making tremendous efforts rushing toward the consumption of haram income.

لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ - What a horrible, evil thing they have been doing.

translation: Why do the rabbis and religious scholars not forbid them from saying what is sinful and devouring what is unlawful? How wretched is what they have been practicing
commentary:
لَوْلَا يَنْهَىٰهُمُ ٱلرَّبَّـٰنِيُّونَ وَٱلْأَحْبَارُ عَن قَوْلِهِمُ ٱلْإِثْمَ وَأَكْلِهِمُ ٱلسُّحْتَ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ

لَوْلَا - How come? Why don't? يَنْهَىٰهُمُ ٱلرَّبَّـٰنِيُّونَ - the Rabbaniyyun, the rabbis وَٱلْأَحْبَارُ - and the scholars - basically the religious leadership. In a modern context, we could say the du'at, the speakers, and the scholars.

عَن قَوْلِهِمُ ٱلْإِثْمَ - Why don't they stop them from saying evil things, from their sinful speech? وَأَكْلِهِمُ ٱلسُّحْتَ - And from their consumption of impermissible income?

لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ - What a horrible thing they have been manufacturing.

This ayah is extraordinarily deep because of that final word: يَصْنَعُونَ (yasna'un) - they manufacture, they build, they construct. The word صُنْع (sun') means to build something, to manufacture something with deliberate effort.

Who has been manufacturing something horrible? Two groups. On one hand, there's the audience that consumes haram - they're manufacturing something. They're manufacturing their own hellfire. They're manufacturing their haram businesses, building empires of evil. That's one meaning of "how horrible what they're building."

But on the other end are the scholars and the du'at who purposely don't speak out about these evils in order to build their own careers. They're also manufacturing something horrible - a corrupted religious leadership, a compromised message, a weakened community. 

لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ - All of them together, how horrible is what they're manufacturing.

This connects to what I've mentioned before: when religion becomes an industry, you don't talk about things that strike a nerve because you'll lose your customer base. The people who donate to your cause will go away. So you keep quiet about the hard truths.

I remember living in a certain state at a certain masjid - this is a very typical situation. They were having a fundraiser, and a brother stood up and said he was going to donate $100,000 to the masjid. That's no small amount.
