But the imam knew something about this person's source of income and said, "Wait, who's donating this?" When he found out who it was, the imam said, "No, we're not taking it from him."

Now understand, in America, the imam is essentially an employee. He can say what he wants, but at the end of the day, the board will do what they want. And if the imam makes too much noise, what happens to him? He loses his job - يَنقِمُونَ مِنكُمْ (they take vengeance against you) لَوْمَةَ لَآئِمٍ (the blame of the critics), right? This is what we do to imams when they speak out.

It turned out this person had come to the imam about a month before and said, "I own several liquor stores, and I can't get out of the business - it's making way too much money. But I'd like to donate because I feel bad about it." The imam told him, "No, we don't want your money here because that impure money will come into the masjid and bring trouble, so we don't want your money."

So the person went directly to the board and said, "I'm ready to donate $100,000." The imam tried to object, but he was careful not to expose the person's situation publicly - he had the wisdom not to embarrass him. He simply said, "We cannot accept that money." That's it.

But the board said, "No, we have to have it." When they found out it was because the donor owns liquor stores, some board members said, "So what? You're saying this because he owns a liquor store? He's donating the money. He's sponsoring the iftar for the entire month. All of us can eat. We're not going to turn it down. The parking lot needs to be finished. The Islamic school needs to be expanded. The hall needs to get bigger. We need a chandelier from Italy." How could they turn that money down?

So the imam got fired. The one who spoke out about it lost his job.

Then, of course, they had to hire another imam because Ramadan was around the corner and they needed someone to lead tarawih every night. So the orientation interview for the new imam included this: "Brother, you will not talk about A, B, and C. Don't speak against liquor stores or lottery shops or certain businesses, because a lot of people in our congregation are involved in these things and they're going to be offended. Talk about things that are either above the sky or below the earth, but anything on this earth - don't deal with it."

There are seven heavens and seven earths, right? Deal with six of the earths and all seven of the heavens, but leave one earth alone - the one you're standing on. Its issues? Don't touch them.

But what got the Prophet ﷺ in so much trouble from the very beginning? Wasn't it precisely because he touched people's nerves? The mission of the Prophets (AS) was to touch people's hearts by confronting hard truths. When the Prophet ﷺ called out people for killing baby girls - were these poor, powerless people? No. When he confronted those cheating in business - were these struggling merchants? No, these were the financial, social, and political elite of that society.

If somebody could get away with killing a baby, that means they were pretty powerful. Nobody touched them. Nobody said anything - they were untouchable, part of the upper class. So confronting them was like striking a hornet's nest. That's huge. But this is the legacy of the Prophets (AS) - to speak out against these wrongs.

لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ - What a horrible thing they are manufacturing - both those who commit these evils and those religious leaders who remain silent about them, each building their own form of corruption.

translation: And the Jews say, "The hand of Allah is chained." Chained are their hands, and cursed are they for what they say. Rather, both His hands are extended; He spends however He wills. And that which has been revealed to you from your Lord will surely increase many of them in transgression and disbelief. And We have cast among them animosity and hatred until the Day of Resurrection. Every time they kindled the fire of war [against you], Allah extinguished it. And they strive throughout the land [causing] corruption, and Allah does not like corrupters
commentary:
وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ يَدُ ٱللَّهِ مَغْلُولَةٌ ۚ غُلَّتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَلُعِنُوا۟ بِمَا قَالُوا۟ ۘ بَلْ يَدَاهُ مَبْسُوطَتَانِ يُنفِقُ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ ۚ وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم مَّآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا ۚ وَأَلْقَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ كُلَّمَآ أَوْقَدُوا۟ نَارًا لِّلْحَرْبِ أَطْفَأَهَا ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَسَادًا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ

The ayah opens with a shocking statement attributed to some among the Jews: **يَدُ ٱللَّهِ مَغْلُولَةٌ** - "Allah's hand is tied." This expression carries multiple layers of blasphemy. The word مَغْلُولَةٌ means bound or chained, suggesting limitation and restriction on Allah's power ﷻ.

This contemporary manifestation of this attitude can be witnessed even today. I once attended an interfaith gathering where a rabbi from a Reform synagogue openly stated that while they believe God revealed laws to Musa (AS) and the prophets before him, those laws were good for their time - but God also gave us minds, which means we can alter and change God's law as society's needs change. Laws we feel are no longer fair, we can remove altogether. This represents the essence of what this ayah criticizes: the claim that Allah's ﷻ guidance is limited, restricted to a particular era, while human judgment has no such restrictions.

The expression يَدُ ٱللَّهِ مَغْلُولَةٌ also carries another implication: that Allah ﷻ cannot touch or punish them because they consider themselves the chosen nation, bound by contract to remain safe regardless of their actions.

Allah's ﷻ response is immediate and severe: **غُلَّتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَلُعِنُوا۟ بِمَا قَالُوا۟**- "May their hands be tied, and they are cursed because of what they said." The word غُلَّتْ  is a powerful reversal - may their own hands become bound, useless, impaired. They accused Allah ﷻ of limitation, so they themselves will experience limitation and curse.

Then comes the magnificent declaration: **بَلْ يَدَاهُ مَبْسُوطَتَانِ يُنفِقُ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ** - "Rather, His two hands are outstretched; He spends however He wills." This phrase sparked extensive theological debates in Islamic history about the attributes of Allah ﷻ. Some scholars debated whether this should be understood literally or figuratively, leading to divisions that manifested even in how people prayed at the Kaaba.

But here's what we need to understand: this debate itself misses the point. The Quran contains expressions about Allah ﷻ that are beyond our complete comprehension. The sahaba (RA) didn't spend their time caught up in these debates. When they encountered something they couldn't fully grasp, they said "Allahu 'alam" (Allah knows best) and moved on. Their curriculum for learning about Allah ﷻ wasn't organized around philosophical categorizations - it was the Quran and the practical example of the Prophet ﷺ.

When someone once asked about where Allah ﷻ is, the response from our righteous predecessors was clear: leave these questions alone. They will do nothing but ruin your religion and turn you against your fellow believers. The central question of the Quran isn't "where is Allah?" but rather "who is Allah?" - exploring His power, His closeness to us, how He hears, how He sees, how He knows, how He helps, how He forgives.

The phrase يُنفِقُ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ- "He spends however He wills" - is the real point. Some commentators note this was revealed when Muslims were short on resources. Criticism arose: what kind of God helps you when you're still struggling financially even after victory? Allah ﷻ responds that He gives as He wills, in His wisdom.

Then comes a solemn warning: **وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم مَّآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا**. Allah ﷻ swears - and He emphasizes this oath three times with the word لَيَزِيدَنَّ- that what has been sent down to you from your Master will absolutely increase a huge number among them in طُغْيَـٰنًا (rebellion) and كُفْرًا ( disbelief). The very revelation meant to guide them will, for those whose hearts are sealed, only increase them in their vicious rejection.

**وَأَلْقَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ** - "And We have cast between them animosity and hatred until the Day of Judgment." The word أَلْقَيْنَا means "we threw" or "we cast" - Allah ﷻ has placed permanent division among them as a consequence of their rejection. This isn't until some point in history, but إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - all the way until the Day of Resurrection.

**كُلَّمَآ أَوْقَدُوا۟ نَارًا لِّلْحَرْبِ أَطْفَأَهَا ٱللَّهُ**- "Every time they kindle a fire for war, Allah extinguishes it." This is a powerful image: their desire is for conflict, for war to break out. But Allah ﷻ keeps putting out those fires, preventing those opportunities from materializing.

**وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَسَادًا**- "And they strive throughout the land causing corruption." The word يَسْعَوْنَ indicates serious effort and striving. They work hard, they make strides to create فَسَادًا (corruption) in the earth, particularly the worst kind of corruption: starting wars and creating conflict.

Are there interest groups working behind the scenes even today that would profit from war, from global conflict? It would be naive not to recognize this reality. We are studied, analyzed, categorized. Research groups have literally broken Muslim communities into demographics, studying each group's tendencies and how to manipulate them against each other. We need to wake up to these realities and not fall into the traps set for us.

When controversies are manufactured to provoke Muslims into reactions, we need wisdom. Burning cars and destroying property doesn't defend the honor of the Prophet ﷺ - it feeds into exactly what they want. It raises their ratings, increases their revenue, and makes us look foolish. We need to learn what works and what doesn't, what serves our cause and what undermines it.

The ayah concludes: **وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ**- "And Allah does not love the corrupters." Those who cause corruption, who strive to create fasad in the earth, do not have the love of Allah ﷻ.

translation: And if only the People of the Scripture had believed and feared Allah, We would have removed from them their misdeeds and admitted them to Gardens of Pleasure
commentary:
وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ لَكَفَّرْنَا عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَلَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُمْ جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ

After the stern warning, Allah ﷻ extends hope and possibility: **وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟**- "Had the People of the Book only believed and protected themselves." This is a conditional statement expressing what could have been, what is still possible.

The two conditions are clear: ءَامَنُوا۟ (believed) and ٱتَّقَوْا۟ (had taqwa, protected themselves). True iman combined with taqwa - consciousness of Allah ﷻ that manifests in righteous action and avoidance of sin.

The promise that follows is magnificent: **لَكَفَّرْنَا عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ**- "We would have buried away from them their evil deeds." The word كَفَّرْنَا means to cover, to bury, to wipe away completely. The People of the Book were engaged in serious, heavy evil deeds. But Allah ﷻ is saying that nothing is beyond forgiveness. Iman and taqwa have the power to erase even the gravest sins, to bury them so completely that it's as if they never happened.

**وَلَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُمْ جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ**- "And We would have entered them into gardens of constant blessing." The gardens are described as جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ. The word نَّعِيمِ indicates constant, endless blessing - gardens characterized by perpetual comfort and joy.

This ayah teaches us a beautiful principle: no matter how far someone has strayed, no matter how many evil deeds they've committed, the door of Allah's mercy remains open. True belief and taqwa can wipe the slate clean and open the gates of eternal blessing.

translation: And if only they upheld [the law of] the Torah, the Gospel, and what has been revealed to them from their Lord, they would have consumed [provision] from above them and from beneath their feet. Among them are a moderate community, but many of them - evil is that which they do
commentary:
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ أَقَامُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِم مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ لَأَكَلُوا۟ مِن فَوْقِهِمْ وَمِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِم ۚ مِّنْهُمْ أُمَّةٌ مُّقْتَصِدَةٌ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ سَآءَ مَا يَعْمَلُونَ

This ayah continues the theme of what could have been: **وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ أَقَامُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ**- "And had they established the Torah and the Gospel." The word أَقَامُوا۟ (aqamu) doesn't just mean to read or possess - it means to establish, to uphold, to implement fully and properly.

**وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِم مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ**- "And what was revealed to them from their Master." This phrase encompasses not just the written scriptures, but also the sunnah of their prophets - the practical example, character, legacy, and teachings that came through their messengers. Just as we have the Quran and the sunnah of our Prophet ﷺ, they had their scriptures and the sunnah of their prophets.

Then comes the promise: **لَأَكَلُوا۟ مِن فَوْقِهِمْ وَمِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِم**- "They would have eaten from above them and from beneath their feet." This is a powerful image of abundance and blessing. When blessings come from above and below, it means they're completely surrounded by Allah's provision. They would have lived a beautiful, satisfying life.

This is critical to understand: Allah ﷻ is not describing Paradise here. He's talking about this world, this life. If people simply lived by Allah's Book, if they followed the example of His prophets ﷺ, they would lead a great life right here on earth. There would be no shortage, no scarcity.

Think about the context: Allah ﷻ complains in the Quran about people making money through irregular financial means, through haram transactions, through playing around with Allah's laws for their own convenience. This ayah is saying: if you just abided by My law, there would be no shortage of provision. You don't need to take the illegal route. I'm guaranteeing you - I would have taken care of you, in this life and in the Hereafter. I'm offering you a way to live a great life in this world by simply establishing what I have revealed.

**مِّنْهُمْ أُمَّةٌ مُّقْتَصِدَةٌ**- "Among them is a nation that is moderate." The word مُّقْتَصِدَةٌ comes from قصد, which means to walk a fair line, to stay on course without deviating left or right. There are some among the People of the Book who are fair, balanced, seeking truth sincerely.

**وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ سَآءَ مَا يَعْمَلُونَ**- "But many of them - how terrible are the deeds they do!" If we were to express this in conversational English, we might say: "But a huge number of them? Man, what horrible decisions they make!" The structure سَآءَ مَا (sa'a ma) emphasizes just how bad their choices are.

translation: O Messenger, announce that which has been revealed to you from your Lord, and if you do not, then you have not conveyed His message. And Allah will protect you from the people. Indeed, Allah does not guide the disbelieving people
commentary:
۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۖ وَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

Now the attention turns directly to the Messenger ﷺ: **يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ** - "O Messenger, convey what has been revealed to you from your Master."

The word بَلِّغْ means to get the message out, to deliver it fully, to make it reach far and wide. This instruction to spread the message widely was given at the beginning of the Prophet's mission in Surah Al-Muddaththir. But notice where we are now - we're near the end of the Makkan-Madinan experience, approaching the final stages. Even at this point, even after victories, even after many have embraced Islam, Allah ﷻ is telling His Messenger ﷺ: keep going. Keep delivering.

Why? Because acceptance of Islam with the tongue doesn't automatically mean the message of this deen, the taqwa it's meant to instill, has reached all the way into people's hearts. The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to judge with justice, yes - but he cannot stop preaching. He must continue to get the word out.

**وَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُۥ**- "And if you don't do so, then you have not delivered His message at all." This is emphatic. It's not that partial delivery is acceptable - if you stop before Allah ﷻ tells you to stop, you haven't done justice to the message at all. You cannot halt your preaching until I tell you, regardless of victory or defeat. Keep spreading the message.

This teaches us something important about da'wah: it's not just for non-Muslims. The Prophet ﷺ himself, even after the conquest of Makkah, even after establishing the Islamic state, is still being told to keep delivering, keep reminding, keep preaching. To whom? To everyone - those who claim belief and those who don't. The reminder is always needed.

**وَٱللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ**- "And Allah will protect you from the people." The word يَعْصِمُكَ (ya'simuka) means to provide protection, to shield, to keep safe. Don't worry about how the people receive the message, whether they welcome it or reject it. Don't fear their plots or their harm. You keep delivering the message - I'll take care of protecting you from them.

**إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ**- "Indeed, Allah does not guide the disbelieving people." Allah ﷻ is not one to guide those who have chosen kufr, who persist in rejection despite clear proofs. Guidance is offered to all, but those who close their hearts cannot be forced to accept it.

translation: Say, "O People of the Scripture, you are [standing] on nothing until you uphold [the law of] the Torah, the Gospel, and what has been revealed to you from your Lord." And that which has been revealed to you from your Lord will surely increase many of them in transgression and disbelief. So do not grieve over the disbelieving people
commentary:
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰ تُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۗ وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم مَّآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا ۖ فَلَا تَأْسَ عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

The previous ayah gave positive reinforcement: "If you only established the Torah and Gospel, you would have eaten from above and below, you would have had a good life." Now comes the stark reality check.

**قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ** - "Say: O People of the Book, you are not upon anything." 

The expression لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ is a powerful Arabic figure of speech. We see it used in Surah Al-Baqarah when the Jews and Christians speak against each other - each claiming the other has no basis. This phrase means: to have no foundation, no basis for existence, no justification, no validity. You are essentially worthless in terms of religious standing. You are pointless.

This is a harsh statement, but Allah ﷻ explains the condition: **حَتَّىٰ تُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ** - "Until you establish the Torah and the Gospel and what has been sent down to you from your Master."

Why does Allah ﷻ call them to establish the Torah and Gospel? Because if they truly did establish them properly, those scriptures would compel them to accept Islam. The authentic Torah and Gospel point to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. So this is actually a call to Islam phrased as a call to their own books.

The phrase وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم- "what has been sent down to you" - refers primarily to the Quran, but also includes the sunnah and teachings of all the prophets.

**وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم مَّآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا**- "And certainly, what has been sent down to you from your Master will absolutely increase many of them in rebellion and disbelief."

This is the second time in these ayahs that Allah ﷻ makes this solemn statement. First it was positive encouragement, now it's stark reality after calling them out. The revelation will have one of two effects: it will either guide those whose hearts are open, or it will increase the animosity and rejection of those whose hearts are sealed.

The ayah concludes with comfort for the Messenger ﷺ: **فَلَا تَأْسَ عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ**- "So do not grieve over the disbelieving people."

The word تَأْسَ means to grieve, to be sorrowful over something. The Prophet ﷺ had a tender heart. He cared deeply about people accepting guidance. But Allah ﷻ tells him: don't let their rejection consume you with grief. You've done your job. You've delivered the message. Their choice is theirs, and the consequences are on them. Don't carry that burden in your heart.

This is a powerful reminder for anyone doing da'wah today: deliver the message with wisdom and compassion, but don't destroy yourself emotionally over those who choose to reject it. Your responsibility is to convey, not to force hearts to change. That's in Allah's hands alone.

translation: Indeed, those who have believed [in Prophet Muhammad] and those [before Him] who were Jews or Sabeans or Christians - those [among them] who believed in Allah and the Last Day and did righteousness - no fear will there be concerning them, nor will they grieve
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـُٔونَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ

This ayah opens with a significant acknowledgment: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ - "Indeed, those who believed and those who were Jews." When we examine the structure, we find something remarkable. Allah ﷻ is speaking of disbelievers, but He immediately clarifies that they're not all disbelievers. Historically, there have been believers among them, without any doubt.

The structure then shifts with وَٱلصَّـٰبِـُٔونَ (the Sabians). If we were to use modern punctuation, it would read: "Indeed, those who believed and those who were Jews..." followed by a new sentence beginning with the Sabians. The word الصَّـٰبِـُٔونَ is in the nominative case (marfu'), which indicates the start of a fresh statement. This isn't simply a continuation of the previous phrase. This grammatical choice matters because Allah ﷻ deliberately leaves the first statement open-ended.

Why this structure? Because this is a review of what has already been revealed. Students of the Quran who encounter إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ already have previous passages running through their minds. They recall the fuller statement revealed in Surah Al-Baqarah: "Indeed, those who believed and those who were Jews and the Sabians - whoever believed in Allah and the Last Day..."

Now Allah ﷻ adds a crucial group that wasn't mentioned before: وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ (and the Christians). This insertion addresses anyone who might have thought that people without a revealed book were completely lost. The ayah then establishes the universal criterion: مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا - "Whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day and does righteous deeds" - فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ - "there shall be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve."

Before proceeding to the next ayah, we must address a critical point about the validity of messengership. Some have attempted to manipulate ayat like this one to suggest that messengers are not important, that simply believing in Allah is sufficient regardless of following His messengers. This is a dangerous misunderstanding that contradicts the Quran's consistent emphasis throughout Surah Al-Maidah on the People of the Book being called out for not making verdicts by revelation.

Consider how the original audience understood these verses. When we think of Quran today, we think of a book on a shelf. When we think of Sunnah, we think of another set of books. But when the Companions (RA) heard about the Book, where did they understand it to be coming from? The mouth of the Prophet ﷺ. When they thought of guidance, where did they see it? In the behavior and speeches of the Prophet ﷺ. To them, the source was unified - they couldn't easily separate the revelation from the Messenger ﷺ the way we can today, having access to written texts.

When Allah ﷻ highlights the importance of judging by what He revealed, the Sahaba understood this to mean judging by what the Prophet ﷺ was telling them. After all, the only way to know what Allah ﷻ sent down is through the Prophet ﷺ who received and conveyed it.

translation: We had already taken the covenant of the Children of Israel and had sent to them messengers. Whenever there came to them a messenger with what their souls did not desire, a party [of messengers] they denied, and another party they killed
commentary:
لَقَدْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَأَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمْ رُسُلًا ۖ كُلَّمَا جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُهُمْ فَرِيقًا كَذَّبُوا۟ وَفَرِيقًا يَقْتُلُونَ

Allah ﷻ now shifts the focus to a historical pattern: لَقَدْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ - "We had certainly taken the covenant of the Children of Israel." The word مِيثَـٰقَ refers to a strong, clearly understood contract on both ends - not a vague agreement, but a binding commitment that was crystal clear to all parties.

وَأَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمْ رُسُلًا - "And We sent to them messengers." Notice the plural form, emphasizing the abundance of prophetic guidance sent to Bani Israel. Then comes the tragic pattern: كُلَّمَا جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُهُمْ - "Every time a messenger came to them with what their souls did not desire."

The word تَهْوَىٰٓ refers to personal preference, what appeals to the ego, what the self wants. Whenever messengers brought revelation that conflicted with their preferences, their desires, their established customs - what happened? فَرِيقًا كَذَّبُوا۟ وَفَرِيقًا يَقْتُلُونَ - "A party of them they denied, and a party they attempt to kill."

The verb يَقْتُلُونَ in this form indicates continuous attempt, ongoing effort. This is significant: when a verb takes this grammatical pattern, it implies striving toward something. Compare this to similar constructions in the Quran: يَعْقِلُونَ doesn't just mean "they know" but rather "they seek to understand, they try to comprehend." Similarly, يَقْتُلُونَ here means they persistently try to kill, they make attempts on the lives of prophets.

Some messengers they lied about, slandering them and rejecting their message. Others they physically attempted to murder. This wasn't a one-time incident but a recurring pattern throughout their history whenever divine guidance challenged their comfort zones.

translation: And they thought there would be no [resulting] punishment, so they became blind and deaf. Then Allah turned to them in forgiveness; then [again] many of them became blind and deaf. And Allah is Seeing of what they do
commentary:
وَحَسِبُوٓا۟ أَلَّا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ فَعَمُوا۟ وَصَمُّوا۟ ثُمَّ تَابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ عَمُوا۟ وَصَمُّوا۟ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ

وَحَسِبُوٓا۟ أَلَّا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ - "And they assumed that there would be no trial, no difficulty, no consequences." They thought they could engage in this behavior without facing any trouble, that there would be no fitna - no test, no punishment, no accountability.

فَعَمُوا۟ وَصَمُّوا۟ - "So they became blind and they became deaf." Notice the order: blind first, then deaf. They lost their ability to see the truth, then their ability to hear it. These aren't physical disabilities but spiritual ones - the blindness of insight and the deafness of the heart.

ثُمَّ تَابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ - "Then Allah turned to them in forgiveness." Despite their rebellion, Allah ﷻ opened the door to repentance. He restored their sight and their hearing, giving them another chance.

ثُمَّ عَمُوا۟ وَصَمُّوا۟ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ - "Then they became blind and deaf again - many of them." After being given the opportunity to return, after having their spiritual faculties restored, many among them chose to return to their previous state of blindness and deafness.

The placement of كَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ (many of them) is grammatically significant. For Arabic students: this isn't functioning as a fa'il (subject) of the preceding verbs. The verbs already have their implied subjects. Rather, كَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ serves as a specification, a clarification that intensifies the meaning: "They became blind, they became deaf - a huge number from among them."

وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ - "And Allah is Seeing of what they do." After mentioning their blindness, Allah ﷻ reminds us that He is بَصِيرٌ - All-Seeing, in full view of their actions. While they chose blindness, He maintains perfect vision of everything they do. This serves as both a warning and a comfort: nothing escapes His sight, and justice will be served.

translation: They have certainly disbelieved who say, "Allah is the Messiah, the son of Mary" while the Messiah has said, "O Children of Israel, worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord." Indeed, he who associates others with Allah - Allah has forbidden him Paradise, and his refuge is the Fire. And there are not for the wrongdoers any helpers
commentary:
لَقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ

لَقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ - "They have certainly disbelieved who say, 'Allah is the Messiah, the son of Mary.'" The لَقَدْ at the beginning emphasizes certainty - there is absolutely no doubt that this statement constitutes disbelief. This addresses one form of Trinitarian belief where Jesus (AS) himself is identified as God.

It's important to understand that throughout Christian history, various forms of Trinity emerged. One early form popular in Egypt consisted of God the Father, God the Mother (Mary), and God the Son. This theological development made sense in the Egyptian context because they already had a cultural framework of divine father-mother-child relationships in their previous belief systems. Christianity spread in Egypt partly through this adapted theology that elevated Mary (AS) to divine status alongside the Father and the Son.

Other forms of Trinity consisted of the Father, the Holy Spirit, and the Son. Yet another interpretation held that all three are actually one - that Allah is God, and Jesus is God Himself merely taking human form. Some attribute this particular understanding to the followers of Paul.

وَقَالَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ - "But the Messiah said, 'O Children of Israel, worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord.'" Notice that the very person they claim is God denied this claim himself. Isa (AS) called to the worship of Allah ﷻ alone, identifying Him as "my Lord and your Lord" - placing himself in the same category of servitude to Allah ﷻ as his followers.

إِنَّهُۥ مَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ - "Indeed, whoever associates partners with Allah..." The إِنَّهُۥ at the beginning emphasizes that what follows is an undeniable fact. The pronoun هُ (hu, "it") here doesn't translate directly into English but serves to emphasize the certainty of the statement.

فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ - "Then Allah has forbidden Paradise to him." This is the direct warning from Isa (AS) himself to his nation. The first thing he's warning them about isn't some minor issue - it's the most serious matter possible: don't forbid Paradise from yourselves through shirk.

وَمَأْوَىٰهُ ٱلنَّارُ - "And his refuge is the Fire." The word مَأْوَىٰ refers to a place of permanent residence, where one dwells for protection. There's a profound irony here: what should be a place of refuge, safety, and protection becomes the Fire. It's an oxymoron - seeking refuge in torment.

وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ - "And there are not for the wrongdoers any helpers." The word أَنصَارٍ is significant - it means helpers, supporters, those who come to your aid. The phrase مِنْ أَنصَارٍ with the من emphasizes absolute negation: not any helpers whatsoever, not even one.

Why is this point about helpers particularly important? Because of the central irony in Christian theology: on the Day of Judgment, Christians believe Jesus (AS) will intercede for them, will come to help them. Yet Isa (AS) himself is saying here that wrongdoers won't have any helpers at all. The very foundation of salvation they've built - that someone else will rescue them from accountability - is being dismantled by the one they claim will save them.

translation: They have certainly disbelieved who say, "Allah is the third of three." And there is no god except one God. And if they do not desist from what they are saying, there will surely afflict the disbelievers among them a painful punishment
commentary:
لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَـٰثَةٍ ۘ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّآ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۚ وَإِن لَّمْ يَنتَهُوا۟ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ لَيَمَسَّنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَـٰثَةٍ - "They have certainly disbelieved who say, 'Allah is the third of three.'" After addressing those who claim Jesus is God, now Allah ﷻ addresses another form of disbelief: the claim that Allah is one component of a trinity, the third of three divine entities.

وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّآ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ - "While there is no deity except one deity." The particle مَا here is for negation, and the من in مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ emphasizes the absolute nature of this negation. The إِلَّآ creates an exception - there is absolutely no deity whatsoever except one single deity. The repetition of إِلَـٰه in both parts of the statement serves to refute and correct: "You're wrong about deity. Here's the fact about deity - it is One."

وَإِن لَّمْ يَنتَهُوا۟ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ - "And if they do not desist from what they are saying..." The word يَنتَهُوا۟ means to cease, to discontinue, to stop completely. Allah ﷻ is giving them a warning with an opportunity: stop saying this.

لَيَمَسَّنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ - "There will surely touch those who disbelieve among them a painful punishment." The لَ at the beginning combined with the نَّ at the end creates a strong emphasis of certainty - this will absolutely happen.

But notice something crucial in the structure: "If they don't stop what they're saying, a painful punishment will touch those who disbelieve from among them." The phrase ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ - "those who disbelieve among them" - creates a specification. Allah ﷻ is highlighting a particular subset.

Why this specification? Because by the time this revelation came, some Christians had already come into contact with the Prophet ﷺ and heard the truth directly. Those who continue to hold onto their beliefs after being exposed to clear evidence occupy a special category of disbelief. They're not simply following what they inherited or what they were taught - they've encountered the truth from the final messenger and actively rejected it. Their punishment has a special severity because of this direct rejection after clear evidence.

translation: So will they not repent to Allah and seek His forgiveness? And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
أَفَلَا يَتُوبُونَ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

أَفَلَا يَتُوبُونَ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ - "So will they not repent to Allah?" The أَ at the beginning forms a question, and the فَ connects it to what came before: after all this warning, after all this clarification, will they not turn back?

The mention of repentance (tawbah) here is particularly significant in the Christian theological context. In much of traditional Christian theology - though modern denominations vary greatly - the concept is that repentance and salvation come through accepting Jesus as Lord and Savior. According to this framework, if you don't turn to Jesus, God will punish you. Jesus becomes a shield, an intermediary between the believer and God. You need that intermediary for God to show you mercy.

Allah ﷻ asks: أَفَلَا يَتُوبُونَ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ - "Will they not turn back to Allah Himself?" Why do you need something in between? Why not direct access?

وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَهُۥ - "And seek His forgiveness?" Why not just ask Him directly for forgiveness? This is the fundamental question Allah ﷻ poses to dismantle the entire theological framework that places intermediaries between the servant and the Creator.

وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - "And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful." These two attributes are crucial here. غَفُورٌ is an intensive form - extremely forgiving, always ready to forgive. رَّحِيمٌ means perpetually merciful. If Allah ﷻ is already this forgiving and this merciful, why would you need anyone else to access His mercy?

Understanding the psychology of shirk is essential here. One of the main reasons people engage in associating partners with Allah ﷻ is because they don't want to deal with Him directly. They want someone else to handle that relationship for them. This way, they can continue their lives without changing - they can keep running their prohibited businesses, engaging in their sins, living as they please. They'll just go worship at a shrine, make offerings to a saint, venerate a grave - not because they're truly interested in that intermediary, but because they're trying to avoid direct accountability to Allah ﷻ.

The logic goes: this righteous person will go to Allah and advocate for me. "I donated to his shrine, I'm under his protection, put me in the special VIP section of Paradise." It's about avoiding the direct relationship that demands change.

What do many Christians believe? That they can do whatever they want as long as they're "saved" by Jesus. They're not getting in trouble with God because someone will come and intercede: "Wait, hold on, Dad - they're with me." It's about having connections, which gives you an open pass to continue your lifestyle without transformation.

That's why Allah asks: أَفَلَا يَتُوبُونَ - don't they want to actually turn back and change? Don't they want to seek forgiveness? The word تَوْبَة necessarily implies changing your ways - it means "I won't do it again, I'm sorry, I'm changing my path." But if you don't actually want to change, if you want to keep doing what you're doing, that's why you don't turn directly to Allah. That's why you want the intermediary - so the intermediary handles the accountability while you maintain your lifestyle.

Allah ﷻ closes by reminding them that He is غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - directly accessible, eager to forgive, perpetually merciful. You don't need anyone else. Just come back to Him.

translation: The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger; [other] messengers have passed on before him. And his mother was a supporter of truth. They both used to eat food. Look how We make clear to them the signs; then look how they are deluded
commentary:
مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ - "The Messiah, son of Mary, was not but a messenger." The construction مَّا...إِلَّا creates an exclusive statement: he is nothing but, nothing more than, nothing other than a messenger.

قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ - "Messengers have passed on before him." The word خَلَتْ comes from khalā, meaning to pass away, to go before. Many messengers came before Isa (AS) and passed from this world. A messenger dying - or in his case, being raised - is not unprecedented. It's part of the natural course of messengership.

This is the exact same language Allah ﷻ used regarding our Prophet ﷺ in Surah Aal-e-Imran: "Muhammad is not but a messenger; messengers have passed on before him." Why is this parallel significant? Because if Christians' excessive love for Isa (AS) led them to commit shirk by elevating him to divinity, there's a real danger that Muslims' love for the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ could potentially lead down a similar path. 

So Allah ﷻ uses identical language for both messengers to keep us on the straight path, constantly bringing us back to the proper understanding: they are messengers, honored and beloved, but messengers nonetheless, not divine.

وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌ - "And his mother was a woman of truth." The word صِدِّيقَةٌ is a profound honorific. It comes from the root صدق meaning truth and honesty, but in this intensive form, it means someone who is extremely truthful, someone whose entire existence embodies truthfulness.

This tells us something crucial about the ranks of righteousness. The highest rank below prophethood is that of the Siddiqun - those of complete truthfulness. People often ask: was Maryam (AS) a prophet? According to this ayah, she holds the rank of Siddiqah, which is the highest rank beneath prophethood. She was not a prophet, but she held the station directly below it.

كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ - "They both used to eat food." This simple statement carries profound implications. Why mention something so mundane as eating? Because it should be enough for any thinking person to understand they were human beings with human limitations.

When you eat food, what necessarily follows? You get hungry - so you're dependent. You'll die without food - so you're mortal. You have to relieve yourself - so you have physical needs. There's only so much you can eat - so you have limitations. There are things you can and cannot eat - so you have restrictions. All of these are characteristics of created beings, not the Creator.

Allah ﷻ does not have any of these limitations. Just by mentioning that both Isa and Maryam (AS) used to eat lunch and dinner, Allah ﷻ provides sufficient proof of their humanity. He doesn't need to mention other biological functions - the logical implications are clear to any rational person.

This also addresses a criticism the disbelievers leveled against our Prophet ﷺ: "What kind of messenger is this? He walks around in the markets! How can he be a messenger? I saw him shopping for groceries! Shouldn't a real messenger be fed from the sky?" The Quran records their objection: "What is wrong with this messenger? He eats food and walks in the markets."

The point is simple: being human, having human needs, engaging in normal human activities - none of this contradicts being a messenger. In fact, it's necessary for messengers to be human so they can relate to and guide humanity.

ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ - "Look at how We make clear to them the signs." The command ٱنظُرْ (look, observe, consider) is directed to the Prophet ﷺ and, by extension, to all of us. Allah ﷻ is telling us: examine how I explain things, study My methodology of clarification.

ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ - "Then look at how they are deluded." After commanding us to observe how He clarifies the truth, Allah ﷻ then commands us to observe the reaction. The word يُؤْفَكُونَ comes from ifk, meaning to turn away, to be deluded, to be led astray despite clear evidence.

These two commands - "look at how We clarify" and "then look at how they turn away" - are teaching us something profound about communication and human psychology. Allah ﷻ is encouraging Muslims to be students of human behavior. Watch carefully how the message is delivered, then study the response. Understand people's reactions. Learn why they react the way they do.

After years of studying rhetoric, Arabic, and Quranic studies, one conclusion becomes clear: the Quran is ultimately about psychology. It's about the psychology of communication to perfection. Who is Allah ﷻ speaking to? What is He saying? How is He saying it? What reaction does He expect? What reaction does He actually get? 

One of the most beautiful words in the Quran is بَلِيغًا, which literally means "to reach someone's heart," to penetrate and have a psychological impact, to truly reach them. Allah ﷻ wants us to learn how He communicates so we can learn how we should communicate. He wants us to study the reactions people have so we're prepared for the reactions we'll receive when we share this message.

translation: Say, "Do you worship besides Allah that which holds for you no [power of] harm or benefit while it is Allah who is the Hearing, the Knowing
commentary:
قُلْ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

قُلْ - "Say" - Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ to deliver this rhetorical question.

أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ - "Do you worship besides Allah..." The phrase مِن دُونِ means "other than, besides, instead of." Are you enslaving yourselves to any entity other than Allah?

مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا - "Something that possesses neither power to harm you nor power to benefit you?" The word يَمْلِكُ means to possess, to own, to have authority over. These intermediaries you've set up - whether saints, prophets elevated beyond their station, or anything else - they don't possess the power to harm you or help you. They can't hurt you if they wanted to, and they can't benefit you even if they desired to.

وَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ - "While Allah - He is the Hearing, the Knowing." The pronoun هُوَ (He) is added for emphasis: Allah Himself, He specifically, is As-Sami' and Al-'Alim.

Why specifically these two attributes - Hearing and Knowing? Because they directly address the concern that drives people to intermediaries. If you make a plea to Allah ﷻ, will He hear? As-Sami' - He hears. If you're going through trouble and need help, does He know your situation? Al-'Alim - He knows. You don't need to go through someone else for your problems to be heard or understood. You have direct access to Allah ﷻ, and He's giving you His full attention.

This is profound when you consider it against human limitations. Even with someone as noble as the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, when he sat with people and listened to them, gave them his attention, could he humanly give equal attention to every single person who needed him? Even if he wanted to with all his heart, even with his elevated character, he physically couldn't. He didn't have enough time to personally address every individual's concerns in a nation of hundreds of thousands.

Someone in the back of a gathering of 100,000 people might think: "I'll never get a chance to talk to him personally. Maybe if I'm lucky, I'll see him from the third row one day, not the three-hundredth row. That's probably as close as I'll ever get." And that's a messenger - a human messenger with human limitations of time and capacity.

So you might think: if even the messenger has such limitations of accessibility, wouldn't Allah ﷻ be even more inaccessible? Wouldn't He be too great, too busy with cosmic affairs to worry about my small, personal problems?

But Allah ﷻ shatters this assumption completely. The entire purpose of the messenger was to teach you that you have direct access to Allah ﷻ - you don't need to go through anyone. The Prophet ﷺ himself was 'Abdullah - a slave of Allah - teaching us to be 'Abdullah so we can achieve closeness to Him. 

And Allah ﷻ Himself opened the invitation: "Call upon Me and I will respond." He didn't say, "Maybe I'll respond" or "Go through someone first." He said: ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ - "Call upon Me, I will answer you." He tells us: seek the means of closeness to Me - وَابْتَغُوا إِلَيْهِ الْوَسِيلَةَ - "Seek the means of drawing near to Him." He's inviting you to get closer. He's encouraging it.

Think about this: people in higher positions don't usually invite you to get closer to them. You have to work hard, prove yourself, climb ranks to maybe get access. But Allah ﷻ - the Highest, the Greatest - is saying: "I'm encouraging you. Come, get a closer spot. I'll give you a closer spot. Seek the means so you can be successful." He's looking out for you.

Allah ﷻ is looking out for each one of us individually. When we recite Quran, He's listening - As-Sami'. When we're going through struggles we haven't voiced to anyone, He knows - Al-'Alim. 

This is the spiritual reality that should motivate every Muslim who sincerely studies the Quran. When a believer looks for what Allah ﷻ is offering them, looks for the path He's showing them to get close to Him, they won't find more powerful motivation than this: Allah ﷻ Himself is offering you nearness. He's telling you, "I'm listening to you right now."

Do we really internalize what this means? When I recite Quran, Allah ﷻ is listening to me right now? He's an attentive audience to my recitation? One of the most beautiful things you can witness is someone reciting Quran with this consciousness - not reciting for the people, but reciting to Allah ﷻ. You can see the joy in it, hear it in their voice. It's palpable when someone knows with certainty that Allah ﷻ is listening, that He's pleased with what they're doing. That joy transforms the recitation into something that elevates both the reciter and everyone who hears it.

When you know Allah ﷻ is listening, when you internalize that He's السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ - the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing - you don't need intermediaries. You don't need someone else to carry your message to Him. You have His complete attention, always.

translation: Say, "O People of the Scripture, do not exceed limits in your religion beyond the truth and do not follow the inclinations of a people who had gone astray before and misled many and have strayed from the soundness of the way
commentary:
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَ قَوْمٍ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ وَأَضَلُّوا۟ كَثِيرًا وَضَلُّوا۟ عَن سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ

Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ to address the People of the Book with a critical warning about religious extremism. The word غُلُوّ means to go beyond limits, to become extreme, to take something out of proportion. This applies particularly to matters of religion where people exceed what is justified by truth.

The ayah addresses two forms of deviation. First, extremism in religion without truth (غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ) – making religious claims or practices that have no legitimate basis. Second, following the vain desires (أَهْوَآءَ) of a people who have already gone astray. The warning becomes more severe as Allah ﷻ describes these people through three stages of misguidance: قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ (they were lost before), وَأَضَلُّوا۟ كَثِيرًا (they misled many others), and وَضَلُّوا۟ عَن سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (they remain lost from the even path today). This progression shows how misguidance compounds over time – those who were themselves misled then became the cause of others going astray, and continue in their deviation.

translation: Cursed were those who disbelieved among the Children of Israel by the tongue of David and of Jesus, the son of Mary. That was because they disobeyed and [habitually] transgressed
commentary:
لُعِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَلَىٰ لِسَانِ دَاوُۥدَ وَعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ

This ayah speaks of divine curses pronounced through two prophets who brought fundamentally spiritual messages to the Children of Israel. Both Dawud (AS) and Isa (AS) were given revelations with strong spiritual dimensions – Dawud (AS) received the Psalms (Zabur), which is essentially praise and glorification of Allah ﷻ, while Isa (AS) came with a message to soften hearts that had become hardened. The Shariah was already established in the Torah, and these prophets came to confirm it while adding the spiritual dimension that had been lost.

Significantly, both prophets strongly rebuked the Bani Israel in their messages. The curse mentioned here came عَلَىٰ لِسَانِ (upon the tongue of) these prophets – through their actual pronouncements. The reason for this severe response is explained: ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ – that was on account of their disobedience and their continuous transgression of limits.

translation: They used not to prevent one another from wrongdoing that they did. How wretched was that which they were doing
commentary:
كَانُوا۟ لَا يَتَنَاهَوْنَ عَن مُّنكَرٍ فَعَلُوهُ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ

This ayah identifies the core social disease that led to their downfall: كَانُوا۟ لَا يَتَنَاهَوْنَ عَن مُّنكَرٍ فَعَلُوهُ – they would not stop each other from the evil that they did. The word يَتَنَاهَوْنَ comes from نَهَى, which means to stop or forbid. The form used here (تَنَاهَوْنَ) indicates mutual action – they didn't stop each other. This wasn't about individual wrongdoing; it was about a complete breakdown of social accountability.

When evil becomes normalized in a community because no one speaks against it, the entire society falls into corruption. The ayah concludes with emphasis: لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ – what a horrible thing they were thoughtlessly engaged in. The word choice suggests they didn't even consider the consequences of allowing evil to spread unchecked through their community.

translation: You see many of them becoming allies of those who disbelieved. How wretched is that which they have put forth for themselves in that Allah has become angry with them, and in the punishment they will abide eternally
commentary:
تَرَىٰ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا قَدَّمَتْ لَهُمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ أَن سَخِطَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَفِى ٱلْعَذَابِ هُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ

The Prophet ﷺ and the believers are told: you will observe a huge number among them befriending those who disbelieve. During the Prophet's ﷺ time in Madinah, many from the People of the Book preferred to ally themselves with the Quraysh idolaters rather than make any alliance with the Muslims. They would even say about the pagans, "These people are more guided than the Muslims."

The ayah uses powerful language to describe their choice: لَبِئْسَ مَا قَدَّمَتْ لَهُمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ – how terrible is what they have sent forward for themselves. The word قَدَّمَتْ in classical Arabic means to send something ahead, particularly used for investments whose returns you'll meet in the future. This is what they invested in for their own souls – and what an investment it turned out to be.

The consequence is stated clearly: أَن سَخِطَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ – that Allah ﷻ is extremely enraged with them. When divine anger turns into harsh punishment, it's called سَخَط. وَفِى ٱلْعَذَابِ هُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ – and in that punishment they will remain forever.

translation: And if they had believed in Allah and the Prophet and in what was revealed to him, they would not have taken them as allies; but many of them are defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلنَّبِىِّ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَا ٱتَّخَذُوهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ

This ayah explains the root cause of their misplaced loyalties: وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلنَّبِىِّ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ – had they truly believed in Allah ﷻ, the Prophet, and what was revealed to him, مَا ٱتَّخَذُوهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ – they would not have taken them as their protective friends and guardians. True faith would have prevented them from seeking alliance with those who oppose the truth.

But the reality is different: وَلَـٰكِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ – however, a huge number of them are inherently corrupt. The word فَاسِق refers to someone who has stepped outside the bounds, whose corruption has become part of their nature.

translation: You will surely find the most intense of the people in animosity toward the believers [to be] the Jews and those who associate others with Allah; and you will find the nearest of them in affection to the believers those who say, "We are Christians." That is because among them are priests and monks and because they are not arrogant
commentary:
۞ لَتَجِدَنَّ أَشَدَّ ٱلنَّاسِ عَدَٰوَةً لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلْيَهُودَ وَٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۖ وَلَتَجِدَنَّ أَقْرَبَهُم مَّوَدَّةً لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا نَصَـٰرَىٰ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيسِينَ وَرُهْبَانًا وَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ

Allah ﷻ makes a striking observation about human attitudes: لَتَجِدَنَّ أَشَدَّ ٱلنَّاسِ عَدَٰوَةً لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلْيَهُودَ وَٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ – you will certainly find the worst of people in their enmity toward those who believe to be the Jews and those who commit shirk. This isn't speaking of all Jews or all polytheists, but identifying a pattern that believers will observe. The animosity isn't merely political but runs deep in their literature, culture, and collective attitude.

The contrast comes immediately: وَلَتَجِدَنَّ أَقْرَبَهُم مَّوَدَّةً لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا نَصَـٰرَىٰ – and you will certainly find the closest in love and compassion to those who believe are those who say, "We are Christians." This refers not to politicized Christianity but to genuinely religious Christians – those whose faith practice is sincere, who engage in community service, whose hearts remain soft.

Three reasons are given for this closeness: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيسِينَ – because among them are qissiseen. This term refers to a specific rank within Christian religious hierarchy, those who have shown spiritual inclination and dedication to drawing closer to God. The word وَرُهْبَانًا describes those who have become detached from worldly pursuits out of devotion to Allah ﷻ, similar to the concept of a dervish – one whose heart is completely occupied with the divine rather than the material.

The third quality: وَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ – and they don't show arrogance. They're not seeking to elevate their worldly position or impose their superiority. This humility makes them receptive to truth.

This observation remains relevant today. Many Muslims in Western countries have found cooperation and genuine friendship with Christian communities. Churches have opened their doors for Friday prayers, formed human shields around mosques during attacks, and engaged in sincere interfaith dialogue. This stands in stark contrast to more politicized religious groups. The genuine Christian discourse, stripped of political agenda, reveals hearts that are open and receptive.

translation: And when they hear what has been revealed to the Messenger, you see their eyes overflowing with tears because of what they have recognized of the truth. They say, "Our Lord, we have believed, so register us among the witnesses
commentary:
وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا۟ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ تَرَىٰٓ أَعْيُنَهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ ٱلدَّمْعِ مِمَّا عَرَفُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ ۖ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱكْتُبْنَا مَعَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ

The response of these sincere Christians to divine revelation is described with remarkable beauty: وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا۟ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ – when they hear what was sent down to the Messenger, تَرَىٰٓ أَعْيُنَهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ ٱلدَّمْعِ – you see their eyes overflowing with tears.

The word تَفِيضُ is particularly evocative. It comes from the root فَاضَ, which describes water that trickles easily from something overfilled – water that flows without force, naturally and abundantly. Allah ﷻ doesn't simply say "they cried" but appreciates every single tear by mentioning مِنَ ٱلدَّمْعِ (from tears). This level of detail shows divine appreciation for the emotional response of a sincere heart encountering truth.

The Quran values the tears of believers in multiple places. The Prophet ﷺ taught us about the value of tears shed in fear of Allah ﷻ. There's a historical account of a group of Christians who came to hear the Quran. When it was recited to them, the entire group began crying. Abu Bakr (RA) observed this and said something striking: "This is how we used to be until our hearts became hard."

The reason for their tears: مِمَّا عَرَفُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ – from what they recognized of the truth. Recognition, not discovery. They hear something that resonates with what they already know to be true in their hearts.

Their immediate response: يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱكْتُبْنَا مَعَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ – they say, "Our Master, we have believed, so write us down among the witnesses." They don't ask for status or recognition; they simply want to be counted among those who bear witness to the truth.

This should move us to action. The Muslim community, numbering in the millions in the West, hasn't done justice to outreach with Christian communities. Many Muslims who were formerly Christian report that they only came to Islam through chance encounters – finding a copy of the Quran by accident, or meeting a Muslim friend who mentioned something about the faith. There's no organized, sincere effort to have healthy conversations.

Christians allow Mormons into their churches to explain their faith – a religion founded in the 1800s in Utah, claiming a new prophet. Yet we hesitate to present our case, to simply share what the Quran says about Isa (AS), about our love and reverence for him while explaining our theological differences. The few communities engaging in this work are already seeing remarkable results. The hearts are ready; we need only to reach out with wisdom and sincerity.

translation: And why should we not believe in Allah and what has come to us of the truth? And we aspire that our Lord will admit us [to Paradise] with the righteous people
commentary:
وَمَا لَنَا لَا نُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَا جَآءَنَا مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَنَطْمَعُ أَن يُدْخِلَنَا رَبُّنَا مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

Their declaration continues with beautiful logic: وَمَا لَنَا لَا نُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ – what would be wrong with us that we wouldn't believe in Allah ﷻ? It's phrased as a rhetorical question that assumes the obvious answer: there's no reason not to believe. وَمَا جَآءَنَا مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ – and in what has come to us from the truth.

Then they express their aspiration: وَنَطْمَعُ أَن يُدْخِلَنَا رَبُّنَا مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ – and we have real hope that our Master will enter us among the righteous people. The word طَمَع indicates serious, long-term hope – not a passing wish but a genuine aspiration that shapes their choices.

translation: So Allah rewarded them for what they said with gardens [in Paradise] beneath which rivers flow, wherein they abide eternally. And that is the reward of doers of good
commentary:
فَأَثَـٰبَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا قَالُوا۟ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

The divine response is immediate and generous: فَأَثَـٰبَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا قَالُوا۟ – Allah ﷻ rewarded them because of what they said. The word أَثَابَ means something that happens to someone as a consequence of their action – it can be positive or negative. Here it's explained as the ultimate positive consequence.

What did He pour upon them? جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ – gardens beneath which rivers flow, خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا – they'll remain in them permanently.

The concluding statement is remarkable: وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ – that is the compensation for those who excel. These people had just accepted Islam, yet Allah ﷻ immediately calls them محسنين– those who excel in goodness. They hadn't graduated to this level through years of practice. The quality of ihsan was already present in them; Islam simply validated and directed what was already there. The ihsan existed before; it just needed the framework of Islam to be complete.

translation: But those who disbelieved and denied Our signs - they are the companions of Hellfire
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ

The contrast is stark: وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ – those who disbelieved and deliberately lied against Our miraculous signs. The word كَذَّبُوا۟ implies intentional rejection and denial, not mere disbelief. أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ – those are the companions of the roaring flame, the blazing fire.

translation: O you who have believed, do not prohibit the good things which Allah has made lawful to you and do not transgress. Indeed, Allah does not like transgressors
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُحَرِّمُوا۟ طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَآ أَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ

After discussing extremism among the People of the Book, Allah ﷻ addresses the believers with a warning against the opposite extreme: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُحَرِّمُوا۟ طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَآ أَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ – O you who believe, don't make forbidden the good and pure things that Allah ﷻ has made permissible for you.

This is a critical balance. On one hand, violating Allah's ﷻ law means making things halal that He made haram. But the ayah warns against the opposite danger: becoming so "religious" that you become more restrictive than the Quran itself, where everything becomes haram until proven halal.

The fundamental principle of Islamic law is clear: Allah ﷻ says هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا – "He created for you whatever is on the earth" (2:29). The default assumption is that things are permissible until you find a specific reason they're impermissible. The Quran isn't full of long lists of what's available to you; it contains a short list of what's forbidden. When Allah ﷻ remains silent about something, He has left it open for us.

This doesn't mean there are no ethical boundaries. Some things, while not explicitly forbidden as food or business transactions, have ethical implications that affect character. When someone declares "Facebook is haram" or "YouTube is haram," it becomes problematic. These platforms contain both benefits and harms. If someone has a tendency toward the harmful elements, it may be better for them personally to avoid them. But making blanket declarations of haram creates unnecessary burden.

This pattern repeats throughout history. First the internet was declared haram, before that cartoons, before that cell phones, then software on phones, then listening to Quran recitation on tape in the car, then MP3 players. Our first reaction to new things has often been to ask: "Is it haram?" This creates a mindset where Islam becomes synonymous with prohibition.

Even children absorb this. They talk to each other this way: "What are you eating?" "Jolly Ranchers." "That's haram!" Everything gets a haram sticker tag, as if we're labeling products at a grocery store. This isn't the mentality Islam wants to cultivate. There are clear prohibitions, there are things about which Allah ﷻ said nothing (and thus left open), and there are gray areas where individual conscience must guide based on one's track record and tendencies.

The reality is that most people who seek fatwas about what's haram don't follow them anyway. They use these rulings primarily to pass judgment on others – a sickness far more serious than the matter being questioned.

The ayah concludes: وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ – and don't cross limits. Don't transgress by making things haram or halal beyond what Allah ﷻ has established. Certainly, Allah ﷻ does not love those who cross boundaries.

translation: And eat of what Allah has provided for you [which is] lawful and good. And fear Allah, in whom you are believers
commentary:
وَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنتُم بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ

The positive command follows: وَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ – consume from what Allah ﷻ has provided for you. Two qualities are emphasized: حَلَـٰلًا – that which is clearly permissible and open, and طَيِّبًا – good and pure.

It's significant that حَلَـٰل and طَيِّب appear together frequently in the Quran. This pairing implies that whatever Allah ﷻ has made halal is, in fact, good and pure. It's good for you and pure for you. The two concepts are inseparable in the divine law.

The ayah concludes: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنتُم بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ – be mindful of Allah ﷻ, the One you have now believed in. Taqwa frames the entire discussion of halal and haram. It's not about legal technicalities but about consciousness of the One who established these boundaries.

translation: Allah will not impose blame upon you for what is meaningless in your oaths, but He will impose blame upon you for [breaking] what you intended of oaths. So its expiation is the feeding of ten needy people from the average of that which you feed your [own] families or clothing them or the freeing of a slave. But whoever cannot find [or afford it] - then a fast of three days [is required]. That is the expiation for oaths when you have sworn. But guard your oaths. Thus does Allah make clear to you His verses that you may be grateful
commentary:
لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا عَقَّدتُّمُ ٱلْأَيْمَـٰنَ ۖ فَكَفَّـٰرَتُهُۥٓ إِطْعَامُ عَشَرَةِ مَسَـٰكِينَ مِنْ أَوْسَطِ مَا تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ أَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ ثَلَـٰثَةِ أَيَّامٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ كَفَّـٰرَةُ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ إِذَا حَلَفْتُمْ ۚ وَٱحْفَظُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ

Allah ﷻ concludes this section with guidance about oaths. لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ – Allah ﷻ will not hold you accountable for casual, pointless talk in your oaths. Among Arabs, friends commonly use oath language loosely: "Wallahi, I'll never talk to you again!" A guest prepares to leave: "Are you going? Let me walk you out." "Wallahi, no need, I'm fine." This casual usage of oath language isn't what Allah ﷻ holds us accountable for.

وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا عَقَّدتُّمُ ٱلْأَيْمَـٰنَ – However, He will hold you accountable for the oaths you deliberately tied up and made binding. This refers to when you make a serious, intentional commitment: "I swear by Allah ﷻ, after today I will never step foot in your house again." You leave with full intention. Then later you're hungry and want to return. Now you have a problem.

The expiation is detailed: فَكَفَّـٰرَتُهُۥٓ إِطْعَامُ عَشَرَةِ مَسَـٰكِينَ – the opening up of that oath requires feeding ten poor people. But not just any feeding: مِنْ أَوْسَطِ مَا تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ – from the average of what you feed your own family. The word أَوْسَط means the mean, the middle. You can't gather ten people and give each one a potato chip from a fifty-cent bag and call it done. How much does each member of your family actually eat? Base the portions on that standard. Don't take the baby's portion as your measure, and don't take someone who eats an exceptional amount. The actual average of what your family consumes.

أَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ – or their clothing. Again, the same principle applies: clothe them as you would clothe your own family. Don't buy rags. Shop for those ten people as you would shop for your own household.

أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ – or the freeing of someone enslaved or bound in some form of servitude or debt. فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ – whoever cannot find the financial means, couldn't find someone to free, فَصِيَامُ ثَلَـٰثَةِ أَيَّامٍ – then three days of fasting.

ذَٰلِكَ كَفَّـٰرَةُ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ إِذَا حَلَفْتُمْ – that is the expiation for your oaths when you've taken them. Let's understand the terminology. The word أَيْمَـٰن comes from يَمِين, meaning the right hand. Arabs would shake hands when serious about an oath, giving a firm handshake that made a loud sound. The right hand became a figurative expression for the oath itself.

Later, another word for oath developed: حَلَفَ. The root خَلَفَ means to make a commitment to something, particularly to stay out of trouble or not fight with someone. A قَبِيلَة that is حَلِيف is one that won't fight with you – there's an understanding of peace. A حَلِيف was also a foreigner who, being outside tribal protection, would normally be an open target, but having taken an oath with someone, came under their protection.

What Allah ﷻ says here is that when you've made a serious oath – when you've really made an agreement that you won't mess around with oaths anymore – and you still break it, this is what will open it up: three days of fasting.

But the final instruction is most important: وَٱحْفَظُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ – guard your oaths to begin with. Don't look for these excuses. Don't say, "Well, this is convenient. I've been meaning to lose weight anyway, so I'll just break my oath and do three days of fasting." Don't make a mockery of Allah's ﷻ law.

This mirrors other divine instructions. When Allah ﷻ gave rules about divorce, the final instruction was: don't get divorced. The rules are there for when it becomes necessary, but the goal is to avoid reaching that point. Similarly with oaths: the expiation is provided, but the ultimate instruction is simply don't do it. Guard your oaths from the beginning.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ – This is how Allah ﷻ clarifies His miraculous signs for you, so that you may be grateful. Why would this make us grateful? Because if you took an oath and broke it, that's a significant matter before Allah ﷻ. It's mentioned as one of the final qualities of righteousness: وَٱلْمُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِهِمْ إِذَا عَـٰهَدُوا۟ – those who fulfill their promises when they make them.

You might never have expected to get yourself off the hook for that commitment you made. Will breaking that oath be held against you on Judgment Day? Absolutely. But Allah ﷻ gave you a way out – a way to pay the dues for breaking that oath before you meet Him. One day, when you see the consequences you would have faced, you'll be extremely grateful that you had the opportunity to settle this account in this life. That's when the full meaning of لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ will manifest – when you realize the mercy contained in being given a way to make amends.

translation: O you who have believed, indeed, intoxicants, gambling, [sacrificing on] stone alters [to other than Allah], and divining arrows are but defilement from the work of Satan, so avoid it that you may be successful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا ٱلْخَمْرُ وَٱلْمَيْسِرُ وَٱلْأَنصَابُ وَٱلْأَزْلَـٰمُ رِجْسٌ مِّنْ عَمَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ فَٱجْتَنِبُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ

Some people attempt to rationalize consuming alcohol by arguing that certain types don't cause intoxication, or that the Quranic prohibition only applies to approaching prayer while drunk. They construct elaborate justifications for their choices. This ayah removes all ambiguity and delivers the final verdict.

Allah ﷻ addresses the believers directly: "O you who have believed." The word الخمر, which we translate as wine or alcohol, derives from the Arabic root meaning "to cover" or "to veil." Just as خمار refers to the head covering women wear, khamr covers and veils the mind, obscuring one's ability to judge clearly. Any intoxicant that covers the intellect falls under this category.

The ayah lists four things together: الخمر (alcohol), الميسر (gambling), الأنصاب (stone altars or idols), and الأزلام (divining arrows used for fortune-telling). The word أنصاب comes from نصب, meaning to place something as a monument or marker. In pre-Islamic times, these monuments evolved from simple markers of important events into statues commemorating great people. Gradually, people began venerating these statues, placing flowers before them, burning incense, and eventually worshipping them as shrines.

This progression teaches us an important lesson about how seemingly innocent practices can deteriorate. A statue might begin as a simple memorial, but when people start elevating it, bringing pictures of it into their homes, and treating it with excessive reverence, they open the door to something more dangerous. This is why Islam emphasizes not elevating objects to positions of respect that could lead to spiritual confusion.

The أزلام were arrows used in pre-Islamic lottery systems and fortune-telling. People would shoot arrows into the air to determine portions of meat or to make decisions about marriage and other matters based on where the arrows landed. Modern equivalents include fortune cookies, palm reading, and various forms of superstition where people attribute supernatural significance to random events.

Allah ﷻ declares all four of these practices رِجْسٌ - an abomination, something utterly despicable and unthinkable. They are من عمل الشيطان (from the work of Shaytan). This isn't merely saying they're inadvisable or somewhat problematic; they are fundamentally products of Shaytan's deception.

The command that follows is decisive: فَٱجْتَنِبُوهُ - avoid them completely. The verb اجتنب comes from جنب, meaning "side," suggesting you should sidestep these things entirely, not even approaching them. Don't get involved, don't get close, don't rationalize them. Stay away completely so that you may succeed (لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ).

translation: Satan only wants to cause between you animosity and hatred through intoxicants and gambling and to avert you from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer. So will you not desist
commentary:
إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَن يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ فِى ٱلْخَمْرِ وَٱلْمَيْسِرِ وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَعَنِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّنتَهُونَ

This ayah reveals Shaytan's actual objective behind promoting these practices. The word يُوقِعَ means to install, situate, or firmly establish something. Shaytan wants to يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ - to install among you animosity and hatred.

Consider the observable social consequences. Does alcohol create fights in bars? Does it fuel abuse within households? Do people under its influence say things they don't mean, creating lasting feuds and destroying relationships? The answer is undeniably yes.

Gambling operates similarly. Imagine someone entering a casino with their life savings, convinced they'll win, only to watch an expert player systematically take everything through games of chance. This person sees their financial security evaporate before their eyes while someone else walks away with wealth they didn't truly earn. This breeds resentment, rage, and violence. Statistics consistently show that areas with casinos experience spikes in crime rates, suicide rates, and domestic violence.
