The societal argument that gambling revenue can fund schools and infrastructure conveniently ignores these devastating consequences. Yes, it creates jobs and generates tax revenue, but at what cost? The human toll in broken families, addiction, and violence far outweighs any economic benefit.

Yet Allah ﷻ identifies an even greater harm than the social destruction: وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَعَنِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ - these things obstruct you from the remembrance of Allah and from Salah. When someone becomes addicted, their every thought revolves around the next drink, the next bet, the next fix. The design of casinos illustrates this perfectly: no windows, no clocks, oxygen pumped in to keep people alert. They want you to lose all track of time, to become so absorbed that hours pass without awareness.

We can observe a similar phenomenon with video games in rooms without clocks. People intending to play for 20 minutes suddenly realize five hours have passed. When you're engrossed in gambling, the call to prayer comes but you're in the middle of a game. You tell yourself you'll pray later, but later never comes. The addiction doesn't just distance you from remembering Allah ﷻ periodically throughout your day; it removes Salah from your life entirely.

The ayah concludes with a powerful rhetorical question: فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّنتَهُونَ- "Then are you going to stop or what?" This isn't a question seeking information; it's the tone a parent uses with a misbehaving child: "Are you going to stop?" The expected answer is immediate compliance. There's no room for negotiation or rationalization.

This represents the third and final stage of the prohibition. First, Allah ﷻ revealed that there is sin in alcohol and gambling, and that their sin outweighs their benefit. Then came the command not to approach prayer while intoxicated. But people misunderstood, thinking the solution was simply to skip prayer rather than give up intoxicants. Now comes the definitive verdict: Stop. Period.

translation: And obey Allah and obey the Messenger and beware. And if you turn away - then know that upon Our Messenger is only [the responsibility for] clear notification
commentary:
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَٱحْذَرُوا۟ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا عَلَىٰ رَسُولِنَا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

Following the firm prohibition, Allah ﷻ reinforces the fundamental principle of submission: وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ - obey Allah and obey the Messenger. This isn't a new topic but a continuation of the same conversation, emphasizing that accepting Allah's commands and the Messenger's ﷺ guidance isn't optional for believers.

The command وَٱحْذَرُوا۟ means "be careful" or "watch out." It carries a sense of warning, urging believers to be cautious about their response to these prohibitions.

Then comes a stark warning: فَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ - if you turn away. The verb تولى means to turn one's back, to reject, to walk away from guidance. فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ - then you had better know, you had better understand something clearly: أَنَّمَا عَلَىٰ رَسُولِنَا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ - the only responsibility upon Our Messenger is البلاغ المبين 
This phrase البلاغ المبين is significant. بلاغ means communication or delivery of a message, and مبين means clear, manifest, and clarifying. The Messenger's ﷺ sole obligation is to communicate Allah's message in a way that becomes absolutely clear and clarifies all issues. He has fulfilled this duty. The message about alcohol and gambling is crystal clear. There's no ambiguity left.

If people choose to turn away after receiving such clear guidance, that's their choice, but they cannot claim they didn't know. The responsibility for the consequences falls entirely on those who reject the message, not on the Messenger ﷺ who delivered it with perfect clarity.

translation: There is not upon those who believe and do righteousness [any] blame concerning what they have eaten [in the past] if they [now] fear Allah and believe and do righteous deeds, and then fear Allah and believe, and then fear Allah and do good; and Allah loves the doers of good
commentary:
لَيْسَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَا طَعِمُوٓا۟ إِذَا مَا ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّأَحْسَنُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

When the prohibition of alcohol was revealed, some companions (RA) became deeply concerned. There were believers whose entire bodies had been saturated with alcohol before Islam, who had consumed it regularly for years. One companion worried, "There's not a vein in my body that isn't filled with alcohol. Does this mean I'm completely condemned?"

Allah ﷻ addresses this anxiety with immediate reassurance: لَيْسَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَا طَعِمُوٓا۟ - there is no harm, no sin, no blame upon those who believed and did righteous deeds in whatever they consumed in the past. Let the past be the past.

Notice the precision: those who believed and were doing righteous deeds. They were already engaged in good works; their only shortcoming was that they hadn't yet received explicit guidance about alcohol. The moment they received clear knowledge, they were expected to change, but they wouldn't be held accountable for what they did in ignorance.

The ayah then maps out a beautiful progression of spiritual development through three stages, with تقوى (taqwa) serving as the catalyst for each elevation.

The first stage: إِذَا مَا ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ - when they exercised taqwa, believed, and did righteous deeds. This describes the initial stage of faith where someone embraces Islam. When faith (iman) and righteous deeds are mentioned together, this represents Islam in its practical sense: faith expressed through action. They were protecting themselves from what displeased Allah ﷻ, they believed, and they acted on that belief.

The second stage: ثُمَّ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّءَامَنُوا۟ - then they continued to have taqwa and their iman deepened. Notice that righteous deeds are no longer mentioned separately. Instead, the focus shifts to iman itself. This represents a maturation where faith becomes more than just compliance with commands; it becomes genuine conviction.

Sometimes people do good deeds simply because they're good things to do, without deep reflection on their significance. But as believers maintain their taqwa and continue in their practice, they begin witnessing the profound benefits of these deeds in their lives. They develop true certainty about their value. This is the transition from Islam (outward submission) to Iman (inner conviction).

The progression can be compared to peeling layers of an onion. Each layer of character flaws you work on reveals another layer beneath. Each time you recognize and address a shortcoming, that's an act of taqwa. You're protecting yourself from what displeases Allah ﷻ while drawing closer to Him. Every layer removed brings increased self-awareness and spiritual sensitivity.

The third stage: ثُمَّ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّأَحْسَنُوا۟ - then they maintained taqwa and reached the level of احسان, excellence or perfection in faith. This is the highest station, where you worship Allah ﷻ as though you see Him, and though you don't see Him, you know He sees you. You maintain constant awareness of His presence.

The word احسان comes from حسن (beauty, goodness, excellence). A محسن (muhsin) is someone who not only does what's right but does it beautifully, excellently, with complete sincerity and consciousness of Allah's presence.

Notice what remains constant throughout all three stages: تقوى. Taqwa is the stepping stone, the active mechanism that enables progression. Your beliefs are passive—you've already come to believe certain things. But taqwa is how you refresh and strengthen those beliefs throughout your day. It's the exercise of caution in what you say, what you do, where you go, when you sleep, when you wake, whom you respond to, how you respond to ignorance or jokes, what you eat, how you spend money.

These daily exercises of taqwa maintain and elevate your connection with Allah ﷻ. As your taqwa increases, you experience a new quality in your prayers. That enhanced experience of Salah leads to even greater taqwa, which then elevates you toward ihsan.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ - and Allah loves the people of ihsan. This is the ultimate goal: to be among those whom Allah ﷻ loves, not merely those who obey, but those who excel in their obedience with consciousness, sincerity, and beauty.

translation: O you who have believed, Allah will surely test you through something of the game that your hands and spears [can] reach, that Allah may make evident those who fear Him unseen. And whoever transgresses after that - for him is a painful punishment
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَيَبْلُوَنَّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنَ ٱلصَّيْدِ تَنَالُهُۥٓ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَرِمَاحُكُمْ لِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَخَافُهُۥ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۚ فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

The surah now returns to the theme of hunting restrictions during pilgrimage, introduced earlier. Allah ﷻ warns the believers: لَيَبْلُوَنَّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ - Allah will certainly test you, most definitely, thoroughly. The emphasis in the Arabic makes this a firm declaration, not a possibility.

With what will He test them? بِشَىْءٍ مِّنَ ٱلصَّيْدِ - with some game animals, some huntable creatures. Picture the scenario: Muslims traveling through the desert on their way to perform Hajj. They've been traveling for days, their food supplies are running low, and suddenly a deer appears—a beautiful, plump deer, walking casually right next to them, so close they could reach out and touch it.

تَنَالُهُۥٓ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَرِمَاحُكُمْ - your hands can reach it, and your spears can reach it. You don't have to chase it or hunt it down. It's right there within arm's reach. The temptation is immense. You're hungry, your family is hungry, it's legitimate food, it's halal meat, and it's right there requiring minimal effort. All the rational justifications line up perfectly.

But you're in ihram, in a sacred state, traveling to the sacred House of Allah ﷻ. The rules of ihram explicitly forbid hunting. This is the test.

لِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَخَافُهُۥ بِٱلْغَيْبِ - so that Allah may know who fears Him in the unseen. This phrase operates on two levels. First, الغيب (the unseen) refers to Allah ﷻ Himself—you fear Him though you cannot see Him. Second, it refers to your own concealment—you yourself are unseen by others, alone with this temptation, and you still fear Allah ﷻ.

This highlights a crucial distinction in faith. It's one thing to maintain righteousness in the masjid, surrounded by other believers, with social accountability and community expectations. It's quite different when you're alone, when nobody else sees you, when you could easily rationalize your choice and no one would know. Allah ﷻ wants to know: when you're completely alone with temptation, do you still fear Him?

This test isn't limited to hunting during pilgrimage. While most of us won't face that specific scenario, we constantly face parallel situations. Haram is perpetually dangled before us in modern life. Free downloads of copyrighted material—it's right there, just one click. Opportunities to lie when it would benefit us—nobody would know. Chances to look at what we shouldn't—we're alone with our devices. These are our deer in the desert, our tests of whether we fear Allah ﷻ in the unseen.

The ayah continues: فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ - then whoever transgresses after that, whoever crosses the line despite the clear prohibition. فَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ - for him is a painful punishment. The consequence isn't just stated; it's emphasized as أَلِيمٌ (painful), driving home the seriousness of violating Allah's boundaries when you've been given clear knowledge and explicit warning.

translation: O you who have believed, do not kill game while you are in the state of ihram. And whoever of you kills it intentionally - the penalty is an equivalent from sacrificial animals to what he killed, as judged by two just men among you as an offering [to Allah] delivered to the Ka'bah, or an expiation: the feeding of needy people or the equivalent of that in fasting, that he may taste the consequence of his deed. Allah has pardoned what is past; but whoever returns [to violation], then Allah will take retribution from him. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Owner of Retribution
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْدَ وَأَنتُمْ حُرُمٌ ۚ وَمَن قَتَلَهُۥ مِنكُم مُّتَعَمِّدًا فَجَزَآءٌ مِّثْلُ مَا قَتَلَ مِنَ ٱلنَّعَمِ يَحْكُمُ بِهِۦ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ هَدْيًۢا بَـٰلِغَ ٱلْكَعْبَةِ أَوْ كَفَّـٰرَةٌ طَعَامُ مَسَـٰكِينَ أَوْ عَدْلُ ذَٰلِكَ صِيَامًا لِّيَذُوقَ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِۦ ۗ عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا سَلَفَ ۚ وَمَنْ عَادَ فَيَنتَقِمُ ٱللَّهُ مِنْهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو ٱنتِقَامٍ

After warning about the test, Allah ﷻ makes the prohibition explicit: لَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْدَ وَأَنتُمْ حُرُمٌ - do not kill game while you are in a state of ihram. The command is direct and unambiguous.

But Allah ﷻ is also merciful and practical. The ayah that just came down warned against this act. What about someone who, before this revelation or in a moment of weakness, already committed this violation? Some scholars suggest this ayah addresses someone who couldn't help themselves despite knowing the prohibition.

وَمَن قَتَلَهُۥ مِنكُم مُّتَعَمِّدًا - and whoever among you kills it deliberately, intentionally. فَجَزَآءٌ مِّثْلُ مَا قَتَلَ مِنَ ٱلنَّعَمِ - then the compensation is an animal similar to what was killed, from among livestock. If you killed a deer, you must sacrifice a comparable animal from cattle, sheep, or goats.

يَحْكُمُ بِهِۦ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ - the judgment of equivalency will be made by two just people from among you. This introduces an element of community oversight and fairness. You don't get to decide for yourself whether your compensation is adequate. Two fair, just individuals must verify that the animal you're offering truly matches what you killed.

هَدْيًۢا بَـٰلِغَ ٱلْكَعْبَةِ - it should be an offering that reaches the Kaaba, meaning it must be sacrificed at the Haram and dedicated as a sacrifice reaching Makkah.

Then Allah ﷻ provides alternatives for those who cannot afford such a sacrifice: أَوْ كَفَّـٰرَةٌ طَعَامُ مَسَـٰكِينَ - or the expiation can be feeding poor people. The number of people to be fed relates to the value of the animal. أَوْ عَدْلُ ذَٰلِكَ صِيَامًا - or the equivalent of that in fasting.

These options recognize economic diversity within the community. Not everyone has the means to sacrifice a large animal. Islam provides alternatives that allow everyone to make proper expiation according to their capability.

لِّيَذُوقَ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِۦ - so that he may taste the consequences of his deed. The word وَبَال refers to the burden, the weight, the negative consequences of an action. Through this expiation—whether sacrifice, feeding the poor, or fasting—the person experiences a tangible consequence that impresses upon them the seriousness of what they did. It's not merely punitive; it's educational and transformative.

عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا سَلَفَ - Allah has pardoned whatever occurred before this. This echoes the reassurance given earlier about alcohol consumption before its prohibition. Allah ﷻ doesn't hold people accountable for what they did before receiving clear knowledge. Whatever hunting violations happened before this revelation are forgiven.

But then comes a firm warning: وَمَنْ عَادَ - and whoever returns to it, whoever does it again. The first violation, if done in ignorance or weakness, can be expiated through sacrifice, feeding, or fasting. But if someone deliberately repeats the offense after being clearly warned?

فَيَنتَقِمُ ٱللَّهُ مِنْهُ - then Allah will take revenge from him, Allah will avenge Himself against him. This isn't the community punishing the person; this is Allah ﷻ Himself dealing with the transgressor. The message is stark: don't test Allah's mercy by deliberately and repeatedly violating His boundaries.

وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو ٱنتِقَامٍ - and Allah is the Ultimate Authority, possessor of the power of retribution. The name عَزِيزٌ means the Mighty, the Irresistible, the One whose authority cannot be challenged. ذُو ٱنتِقَامٍ emphasizes that He possesses the full capability to take revenge, to hold accountable, to enforce consequences.

The first time, Allah ﷻ provides a path to make amends. The second time, the matter escalates to a level where Allah ﷻ Himself will deal with the offender. Don't push it. Don't presume upon Allah's mercy by deliberately repeating violations.

translation: Lawful to you is game from the sea and its food as provision for you and the travelers, but forbidden to you is game from the land as long as you are in the state of ihram. And fear Allah to whom you will be gathered
commentary:
أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ ٱلْبَحْرِ وَطَعَامُهُۥ مَتَـٰعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِلسَّيَّارَةِ ۖ وَحُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ صَيْدُ ٱلْبَرِّ مَا دُمْتُمْ حُرُمًا ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ

After establishing the prohibition on hunting land animals during ihram, Allah ﷻ clarifies an important exception that addresses practical concerns: أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ ٱلْبَحْرِ وَطَعَامُهُۥ - lawful to you is the game of the sea and its food.

This clarification is essential because believers take Quranic commands seriously. Consider Muslims traveling by sea to perform Hajj—a journey that could take weeks or even months in earlier times. If they heard that hunting is prohibited during ihram without this clarification, they might literally starve before reaching land. The only way to survive such extended sea voyages is through fishing and consuming what the ocean provides.

مَتَـٰعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِلسَّيَّارَةِ - it is provision for you and for travelers. The word مَتَـٰع means something to utilize, a provision, something beneficial for survival. This isn't just for the individual pilgrim but for entire caravans, whole ships full of passengers. Everyone benefits from this permission. It ensures that the journey to fulfill one of Islam's most important obligations doesn't become impossible due to circumstances of travel.

وَحُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ صَيْدُ ٱلْبَرِّ مَا دُمْتُمْ حُرُمًا - but forbidden to you is the game of the land as long as you are in ihram. The contrast is clear: sea game is permitted, land game is forbidden, specifically during the state of ihram. Once you complete your pilgrimage and leave the state of ihram, land hunting becomes permissible again.

The ayah concludes with a powerful reminder: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ - and be conscious of Allah, toward whom you are all going to be gathered.

This ending is particularly meaningful given the context. Pilgrimage itself is a simulation, a rehearsal of the Day of Judgment. During Hajj, people from all nations gather, wearing simple white garments—the same clothes they'll be buried in. They're herded together toward sacred sites, just as humanity will be herded toward Allah ﷻ for final accountability.

So Allah ﷻ says: you're going to perform this ritual that symbolizes your gathering toward Me. Remember, you actually will be gathered toward Me. This isn't just a symbolic exercise. Your ultimate gathering before your Lord is real and certain. The taqwa you exercise now—restraining yourself from hunting that deer, following these seemingly minor rules about what you can and cannot do—reflects the consciousness you should maintain always, knowing you'll stand before Allah ﷻ and account for every choice, every boundary crossed or respected.

The connection between observing the rules of ihram and consciousness of the final gathering is direct: if you can't respect Allah's boundaries in this temporary sacred state that lasts days or weeks, what does that say about your awareness of the permanent sacred reality that you're journeying toward Him? If the prospect of being gathered to Allah ﷻ doesn't motivate you to observe His limits now, when will it?

translation: Allah has made the Ka'bah, the Sacred House, standing for the people and [has sanctified] the sacred months and the sacrificial animals and the garlands [by which they are identified]. That is so you may know that Allah knows what is in the heavens and what is in the earth and that Allah is Knowing of all things
commentary:
۞ جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكَعْبَةَ ٱلْبَيْتَ ٱلْحَرَامَ قِيَـٰمًا لِّلنَّاسِ وَٱلشَّهْرَ ٱلْحَرَامَ وَٱلْهَدْىَ وَٱلْقَلَـٰٓئِدَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِتَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

This ayah draws together several themes from the preceding verses and elevates our understanding of why Allah ﷻ establishes sacred spaces, times, and symbols.

جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكَعْبَةَ ٱلْبَيْتَ ٱلْحَرَامَ قِيَـٰمًا لِّلنَّاسِ - Allah has made the Kaaba, the Sacred House, a place of standing for people. The word قِيَـٰمًا is rich with meaning. It comes from قام (to stand, to establish, to maintain). It suggests that the Kaaba is something people can stand upon, rely upon, find stability in. It's a foundation, an anchor, something permanent and dependable in a changing world.

The term البيت الحرام (the Sacred House, the Forbidden House) indicates that certain ordinary actions become forbidden there out of respect. Things typically permissible are restricted in this sacred space, elevating its status and marking it as different, special, set apart for Allah ﷻ.

وَٱلشَّهْرَ ٱلْحَرَامَ - and the sacred month. Just as there are sacred spaces, there are sacred times. The sacred months in Islam are periods when warfare was traditionally forbidden, when pilgrimage could be performed safely, when the focus shifts from worldly conflicts to spiritual devotion. Like the sacred space of the Kaaba, these sacred times provide stability and structure for the community.

وَٱلْهَدْىَ وَٱلْقَلَـٰٓئِدَ - and the sacrificial animals and the garlands. These are the animals dedicated for sacrifice, marked by garlands around their necks to indicate they're consecrated, set apart for Allah ﷻ. These animals, once marked, become inviolable. You don't touch them, hunt them, or harm them. They're destined for the House of Allah ﷻ as offerings.

Allah ﷻ has established all of these: a sacred place (the Kaaba), a sacred time (the sacred months), sacred animals (those designated for sacrifice), and sacred markers (the garlands that identify dedicated animals).

Then comes the profound purpose statement: ذَٰلِكَ لِتَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - this is so that you may know that Allah knows everything in the heavens and everything in the earth.

This is remarkable. Allah ﷻ isn't saying these rules exist because of practical benefits or social utility, though those exist. He's saying: I've given you all these regulations—what you can hunt, what you can't hunt, when you can hunt it, which animals are protected, which spaces are sacred—so that you may know that I know everything in the heavens and the earth.

In other words, when you inevitably ask "Why?"—why can't I hunt this animal during ihram? Why is this place special? Why these particular months? Why must this animal be marked?—the fundamental answer is: because you need to understand who you're dealing with. You need to know your place in relation to your Creator.

Allah ﷻ possesses complete, comprehensive knowledge of all things. He knows the wisdom behind every command, even when He chooses not to explain it to you. These rules exist partly to teach you trust, submission, and recognition of His infinite wisdom versus your limited understanding.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ - and that Allah is Knowing of all things. The name عَلِيمٌ (Aleem) emphasizes the completeness and perfection of Allah's knowledge. بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ - He is All-Knowing of everything, every single thing, nothing excluded.

This ending reframes our entire relationship with divine law. Yes, Allah ﷻ often explains wisdom behind His commands—we've seen throughout this passage how alcohol creates enmity, how gambling destroys communities, how these sacred spaces and times create stability for believers. But ultimately, we follow Allah's commands not because we've comprehended all the wisdom behind them, but because He is Aleem and we are not.

The believer's posture is: I trust that You know what I don't know. I trust that Your wisdom far exceeds my understanding. I trust that these boundaries You've set, even when I don't fully grasp their purpose, are for my benefit and for the benefit of humanity. You know what's in the heavens and the earth; I know only what You've taught me.

This is the essence of Islam: submission based on trust in Allah's perfect knowledge and wisdom, even when our limited intellects cannot fully comprehend the reasons behind every command.

translation: Know that Allah is severe in penalty and that Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

This ayah establishes a fundamental balance in our understanding of Allah's ﷻ attributes. The command اعْلَمُوٓا۟ ("know with certainty") demands more than casual awareness; it requires deep conviction about two complementary aspects of divine justice.

Allah ﷻ is شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ - intense in executing consequences. This phrase captures the severity and precision of divine accountability. Yet immediately following this weighty reminder, we find غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ - exceedingly forgiving, always merciful. These aren't contradictory qualities but rather demonstrate the completeness of divine justice. Allah ﷻ holds us accountable while simultaneously offering forgiveness and mercy.

This duality appears throughout the Quran, reminding believers that while they should fear Allah's ﷻ justice, they should never despair of His mercy. The messenger's role in this context is simply to communicate this message clearly, without adding or subtracting from it.

translation: Not upon the Messenger is [responsibility] except [for] notification. And Allah knows whatever you reveal and whatever you conceal
commentary:
مَّا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُونَ

The responsibility of the Messenger ﷺ is precisely defined: مَّا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ- there is no responsibility on the messenger except to communicate the message clearly. The word بَلَـٰغُ emphasizes clarity and completeness in delivery. The Prophet's ﷺ job isn't to force acceptance or guarantee results; it's to ensure the message reaches people in its pure, unadulterated form.

The ayah then shifts focus to divine knowledge: وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُونَ- Allah ﷻ knows what you reveal and what you conceal. This comprehensive awareness encompasses both our public declarations and our hidden thoughts. While the messenger delivers the message, Allah ﷻ alone knows the true response of each heart.

translation: Say, "Not equal are the evil and the good, although the abundance of evil might impress you." So fear Allah, O you of understanding, that you may be successful
commentary:
قُل لَّا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْخَبِيثُ وَٱلطَّيِّبُ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ كَثْرَةُ ٱلْخَبِيثِ ۚ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ

This ayah addresses a dangerous psychological phenomenon: the normalization of wrong through repetition and prevalence. لَّا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْخَبِيثُ وَٱلطَّيِّبُ - the filthy and the pure are never equal, never the same in essence or value.

The crucial insight comes in the phrase وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ كَثْرَةُ ٱلْخَبِيثِ - even if the abundance of the filthy impresses you. This captures a profound truth about human nature: when evil becomes widespread and common, it stops appearing evil. We become desensitized. If you live in an environment where vulgar language is constantly used, the first time shocks you. After hearing it repeatedly, it becomes background noise. Eventually, it slips into your own vocabulary without conscious thought.

This is why maintaining the purity of religious practice matters so deeply. Sometimes cultural practices creep into our understanding of Islam. These practices might deviate slightly from what we're meant to do, but because "everybody does it," the practice becomes justified by its popularity alone. This is precisely the mentality Allah ﷻ warns against: following the majority simply because they are the majority.

The ayah calls to يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ- O people of sound minds. The word لُبّ refers to minds free from vanity, prejudice, and blind following. These are people who can think independently, who don't measure truth by popularity or peer pressure. They evaluate practices and beliefs on their own merit, regardless of how common or rare they might be.

فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ- be conscious of Allah ﷻ, maintain taqwa. This consciousness enables us to see through the illusion of numbers and remain committed to what is genuinely good. لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ- so that you may achieve success. True success comes not from following the crowd but from following the truth, even when standing alone.

translation: O you who have believed, do not ask about things which, if they are shown to you, will distress you. But if you ask about them while the Qur'an is being revealed, they will be shown to you. Allah has pardoned that which is past; and Allah is Forgiving and Forbearing
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ عَنْ أَشْيَآءَ إِن تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ وَإِن تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ عَنْهَا حِينَ يُنَزَّلُ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَنْهَا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ

This ayah addresses the etiquette and wisdom of asking questions, particularly during the era of revelation. لَا تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ عَنْ أَشْيَآءَ- don't go asking about certain matters. This isn't a blanket prohibition on seeking knowledge, but rather guidance about the nature and intention behind our questions.

إِن تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ- if they were exposed to you, they would distress you. The word تَسُؤْكُمْ means more than mere dislike; it indicates genuine harm or burden. Sometimes we ask questions knowing deep down that we won't like the answer, or worse, that we won't follow it even if we receive it.

A narration from the time of the Prophet ﷺ illustrates this principle. A companion repeatedly asked whether Hajj was mandatory every year. The Prophet ﷺ turned away each time, until finally he said, "If I said yes, it would become obligatory, and you wouldn't be able to do it." The point being: don't ask questions that will bind you to obligations you're unprepared to fulfill, especially when what has already been revealed is sufficient.

This teaching also addresses a modern phenomenon: shopping for fatwas. Someone learns a practice is haram from one scholar but doesn't like that answer. So they ask another scholar, hoping for a different response. Then another, and another, until they find someone who gives them the permission they were seeking all along. Then they claim, "There's a difference of opinion on this issue," using that as justification to follow their desires.

The ayah distinguishes between two types of questioning. If your intention is genuine - that you'll accept whatever authentic answer comes - then ask sincerely. But if you're only seeking confirmation of what you already want to do, that's problematic. There's also a third category: when you receive an answer but find it unconvincing because it seems oversimplified or lacks proper depth. In that case, seeking a more thorough explanation from qualified scholarship is not only permissible but necessary. An answer that includes an ayah or hadith doesn't automatically make it correct; it depends on proper understanding and application.

حِينَ يُنَزَّلُ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ- during the time when the Quran is being revealed. This phrase highlights the unique responsibility of the Prophet's ﷺ generation. When we ask contemporary scholars, they can reference established texts and precedents. But the Companions (RA) asked the Prophet ﷺ directly, and his answers could become part of the religion itself. They carried an immense responsibility to be cautious about what they asked.

عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَنْهَا - Allah ﷻ has pardoned these matters. This indicates divine mercy toward questions that might have led to additional obligations. Some matters remained unaddressed not out of oversight, but out of mercy. By not asking excessively, the early Muslims prevented unnecessary complications from entering the religion.

وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ- Allah ﷻ is exceedingly forgiving, supremely forbearing. He knows your struggles, your confusions, your genuine need for guidance. What He has revealed is sufficient because He understands your reality completely. His forbearance means He doesn't burden you beyond what you can bear.

translation: A people asked such [questions] before you; then they became thereby disbelievers
commentary:
قَدْ سَأَلَهَا قَوْمٌ مِّن قَبْلِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَصْبَحُوا۟ بِهَا كَـٰفِرِينَ

This ayah provides historical context and warning. قَدْ سَأَلَهَا قَوْمٌ مِّن قَبْلِكُمْ - indeed, people before you asked such questions. Previous communities pushed for answers, demanded clarifications, sought additional details beyond what was necessary.

ثُمَّ أَصْبَحُوا۟ بِهَا كَـٰفِرِينَ- then they became disbelievers because of them. When they received answers they didn't like, rather than submit, they rejected the entire message. Their excessive questioning wasn't rooted in genuine seeking but in resistance. They hoped to find loopholes, contradictions, or reasons to dismiss divine guidance altogether.

This serves as a cautionary tale: the problem isn't asking questions itself, but the spirit in which questions are asked. When questioning becomes a tool for avoidance rather than understanding, it leads away from faith instead of toward it.

translation: Allah has not appointed [such innovations as] bahirah or sa'ibah or wasilah or ham. But those who disbelieve invent falsehood about Allah, and most of them do not reason
commentary:
مَا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنۢ بَحِيرَةٍ وَلَا سَآئِبَةٍ وَلَا وَصِيلَةٍ وَلَا حَامٍ ۙ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ ۖ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ

The ayah returns to the theme of dietary restrictions and invented religious practices. It lists four categories of animals that pre-Islamic Arabs had made sacred: بَحِيرَةٍ ,سَآئِبَةٍ ,وَصِيلَةٍ, and حَامٍ.
Based on authentic narrations, bahirah refers to an animal whose milk was dedicated to idols - considered sacred, untouchable. Sa'ibah was an animal released in an idol's name, sometimes wearing a token of that idol, making it forbidden for ordinary use. Wasilah was a she-camel that had given birth to multiple female camels; after a certain number, it was declared sacred because of its value as a source of future wealth. Ham was a male camel used for breeding that had produced many offspring, reaching a kind of "stud" status that made it untouchable.

When Islam arrived and these idols were destroyed, these animals remained, and people still treated them as sacred out of habit and cultural conditioning. Muslims were uncertain: can we use these animals now, or do they remain forbidden because of their previous dedication?

مَا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ- Allah ﷻ did not make, did not authorize, did not establish any validity to these categories. None of these distinctions came from divine legislation. وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ- but those who disbelieve fabricate lies against Allah ﷻ.

This is a crucial principle: shirk cultures often feature elaborate, intricate rituals. They have precise steps, specific gestures, detailed requirements - wiggle the bell this way, pour exactly three drops of water, recite at this particular moment. The complexity itself seems to give these practices legitimacy. People think, "This must be authentic because it's so detailed and specific."

But the Quran dismantles this illusion: these are fabricated lies. Complexity doesn't equal authenticity. وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ- most of them don't even use their intellect. They follow these intricate practices without thinking, without questioning, without examining the basis of what they're doing.

translation: And when it is said to them, "Come to what Allah has revealed and to the Messenger," they say, "Sufficient for us is that upon which we found our fathers." Even though their fathers knew nothing, nor were they guided
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ وَإِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ قَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا مَا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ ۚ أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ

The word تَعَالَوْا۟ carries profound imagery. Its root means "height" or "elevation." The command form suggests someone standing at the top of a valley calling down to people at the bottom: "Come on up!" 

Imagine a person who has climbed to a vantage point and sees something magnificent. He doesn't want to keep this vision to himself - he wants those below to experience it too. But those at the bottom can't see what he sees. The only way they'll make the effort to climb is by trusting him. Without trust, why bother? "I'm comfortable where I am."

تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ وَإِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ- come up to what Allah ﷻ has revealed, come up to the messenger. The messenger is inviting them to elevate themselves, to rise from the اسْفَلَ سَافِلِينَ (lowest of the low) to احْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ (the best form). He's asking them to become better human beings, to raise themselves morally and spiritually, just as one physically ascends a mountain.

Their response? حَسْبُنَا مَا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ- what we found our forefathers committed to is enough for us. "We're down in the valley, living in the mud, surrounded by filthy practices, and we're comfortable. We don't know what's beyond this valley, and frankly, we don't care to find out. We're not even curious. Whatever our ancestors left us is sufficient."

This isn't rejection after investigation. It's rejection without even attempting to understand. Too much effort to find out. Too comfortable with the status quo. The tragedy is that their comfort with ignorance prevents them from even glimpsing the possibility of something better.

The word وَجَدْنَا reveals another layer. This verb can mean "to find," but it also suggests finding something accidentally, stumbling upon it. The root appears in Surah Al-Baqarah with the word أَلْفَى, which describes how a small child mindlessly picks up whatever is on the ground - rocks, bugs, feathers - and immediately tries to taste it. You have to constantly watch them: "What's in your hand? Show me your hands!"

This imagery shows how mindlessly they follow their ancestors. Their forefathers stumbled upon these practices without thinking, picked them up like a child grabbing whatever catches their eye, and now the descendants do the same. There's no reasoning, no evaluation, no critical thought - just blind replication.

أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ- what if their fathers didn't understand anything at all and weren't committed to any kind of guidance? The rhetorical question exposes the flaw in their logic. They're at the bottom of the valley refusing to climb because their ancestors were at the bottom. But their ancestors were also ignorant! They couldn't do the thinking for you because they never thought for themselves. They didn't know any better, and they weren't guided either.

This is a devastating critique of blind tradition. You're refusing divine guidance to follow people who themselves had no guidance, who didn't think, who didn't know. This ayah illustrates the tragedy of generational ignorance compounding itself, each generation justifying its ignorance by pointing to the previous generation's ignorance.

translation: O you who have believed, upon you is [responsibility for] yourselves. Those who have gone astray will not harm you when you have been guided. To Allah is you return all together; then He will inform you of what you used to do
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۖ لَا يَضُرُّكُم مَّن ضَلَّ إِذَا ٱهْتَدَيْتُمْ ۚ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

This ayah was significantly misunderstood even during the time of the Companions (RA), which led to important clarifications. عَلَيْكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ - upon you is your own selves, you are responsible for yourselves.

When this was revealed later in the Medinan period, some people began saying, "This means all those earlier ayahs about commanding good and forbidding evil, about giving dawah, about calling others to Islam - that's all cancelled now. Allah ﷻ is telling us to just mind our own business and worry about ourselves."

This misunderstanding was so concerning that Abu Bakr (RA) himself had to give a khutbah addressing it. He said, "This is not what the ayah means. It means each of you will be answerable to Allah ﷻ individually. Your first responsibility is to your own self, and you'll be asked whether you took this religion seriously or as a joke. You can't pass blame to others."

The critical phrase is لَا يَضُرُّكُم مَّن ضَلَّ إِذَا ٱهْتَدَيْتُمْ - the one who has gone astray will not harm you if you remain committed to guidance. This doesn't mean "ignore everyone else." Rather, it establishes that each person bears their own burden. You're not accountable for others' misguidance if you've fulfilled your duty to guide them and remain steadfast yourself.

This connects to the earlier discussion about tradition. Before this, people relied on religion being handed down from ancestors. "This is our tradition, this is what we inherited." But now Allah ﷻ establishes individual accountability. عَلَيْكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ - you yourselves will answer. You won't be able to say, "That was just my culture, that's how I was raised."

This principle applies directly to us. Our religion cannot be passed down mindlessly from parents to children, because if it is, we become worthy of the same criticism we level at others. Our religion must be passed down with بَصِيرَةٍ- with eyes open, with clear sight and understanding.

Every generation must have an original, direct relationship with Islam. Their justification for faith shouldn't be "my parents are Muslim" but rather the same evidence and reasoning that convinced the first generation. When religion becomes nothing more than "I do this because my parents did it," it becomes as weak and vulnerable as any other cultural practice. A stronger culture can easily sweep it away.

Is this happening to Muslims today? The vast majority of Muslim youth - who represent the future of Islam on this planet - don't know why they're Muslim except that their parents are Muslim. They don't understand that they're part of something profound, something they should feel honored by, committed to upholding, carrying forward, and conveying to others. They just know they go to the masjid occasionally, attend Sunday school where they mostly sleep, and pray sometimes at home.

What's shocking, then, when these youth reach college and suddenly have doubts about their religion? They never owned it in the first place. It was just cultural inheritance, not conscious conviction.

إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا - to Allah ﷻ alone is your return, all of you together. فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ- then He will thoroughly inform you of everything you used to do. Every action, every intention, every choice will be examined individually. No one will carry another's burden, and no one will be able to hide behind tradition or culture.

translation: O you who have believed, testimony [should be taken] among you when death approaches one of you at the time of bequest - [that of] two just men from among you or two others from outside if you are traveling through the land and the disaster of death should strike you. Detain them after the prayer and let them both swear by Allah if you doubt [their testimony, saying], "We will not exchange our oath for a price, even if he should be a near relative, and we will not withhold the testimony of Allah. Indeed, we would then be of the sinful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ شَهَـٰدَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ حِينَ ٱلْوَصِيَّةِ ٱثْنَانِ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ أَوْ ءَاخَرَانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ إِنْ أَنتُمْ ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۚ تَحْبِسُونَهُمَا مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِٱللَّهِ إِنِ ٱرْتَبْتُمْ لَا نَشْتَرِى بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ ۙ وَلَا نَكْتُمُ شَهَـٰدَةَ ٱللَّهِ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْـَٔاثِمِينَ

This ayah establishes detailed procedures for witnessing wills, particularly in unusual circumstances. شَهَـٰدَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ حِينَ ٱلْوَصِيَّةِ - the testimony among you when death presents itself to one of you at the time of making a will.

The basic requirement: ٱثْنَانِ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ- two people of justice and fair caliber from among you. Ideally, witnesses should be Muslims from your own community, people whose character is known and established, whose fairness and integrity are recognized.

However, travel creates complications: أَوْ ءَاخَرَانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ إِنْ أَنتُمْ ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - or two others not from among you if you're traveling through the land. The phrase ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (literally "striking in the earth") describes journeying far from home. 

فَأَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ and the calamity of death strikes you. You're far from your community, about to die, and need to leave a will. The only witnesses available might be strangers - perhaps non-Muslims, perhaps Muslims from another tribe whose character you don't know.

What happens when these witnesses return to your community claiming the deceased left everything to them? How do we verify their truthfulness? The ayah provides a mechanism: تَحْبِسُونَهُمَا مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ - you detain them after the prayer.

This is remarkable wisdom. Rather than calling a special town hall meeting, the natural gathering time for the Muslim community is Salah. After the prayer concludes, these two witnesses are detained - not allowed to leave until the matter is resolved. They're put on the spot before the congregation.

فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِٱللَّهِ إِنِ ٱرْتَبْتُمْ - then they must both swear by Allah ﷻ if you have doubts about them. If the community doesn't know these people and questions their honesty, they must take an oath before Allah ﷻ.

The content of their oath: لَا نَشْتَرِى بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا- we will not sell this testimony for any price. We're not mixing our own interests or agenda into what we're reporting. وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ - even if it was for a close relative. Even if telling the truth benefits or harms someone related to us, we're reporting exactly what we witnessed.

وَلَا نَكْتُمُ شَهَـٰدَةَ ٱللَّهِ - and we will not conceal the testimony that belongs to Allah ﷻ. Calling it شَهَـٰدَةَ ٱللَّهِ (Allah's testimony) elevates witnessing to a sacred responsibility. Hiding or distorting this testimony becomes tantamount to lying about Allah ﷻ Himself, equivalent to disbelief in its severity.

إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْـَٔاثِمِينَ - indeed, we would then be among the sinful. The witnesses acknowledge that violating this oath would place them firmly among those who commit grave sins, those who have earned divine displeasure.

This entire procedure demonstrates how Islamic law balances practical necessity with moral integrity. When ideal conditions aren't available - when you can't find known Muslim witnesses - the system adapts while maintaining accountability through public testimony, communal oversight, and sacred oaths. The masjid becomes the courthouse, the congregation becomes the jury, and the oath before Allah ﷻ becomes the ultimate safeguard against deception.

translation: But if it is found that those two were guilty of perjury, let two others stand in their place [who are] foremost [in claim] from those who have a lawful right. And let them swear by Allah, "Our testimony is truer than their testimony, and we have not transgressed. Indeed, we would then be of the wrongdoers
commentary:
فَإِنۡ عُثِرَ عَلَىٰۤ أَنَّهُمَا ٱسۡتَحَقَّاۤ إِثۡمࣰا فَـَٔاخَرَانِ یَقُومَانِ مَقَامَهُمَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِینَ ٱسۡتَحَقَّ عَلَیۡهِمُ ٱلۡأَوۡلَیَـٰنِ فَیُقۡسِمَانِ بِٱللَّهِ لَشَهَـٰدَتُنَاۤ أَحَقُّ مِن شَهَـٰدَتِهِمَا وَمَا ٱعۡتَدَیۡنَاۤ إِنَّاۤ إِذࣰا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِینَ
This ayah addresses what happens when witnesses are discovered to have been dishonest in their testimony. The phrase فَإِنْ عُثِرَ عَلَىٰٓ (if it becomes apparent, if it is found out, if it is determined) introduces a crucial safeguard in Islamic law. When it's discovered that the two witnesses استحقا إثمًا (have deserved sin), meaning they testified falsely despite swearing by Allah ﷻ, the community is not left without recourse.

What makes this significant is the dual responsibility it establishes. The witnesses have their duty—to swear by Allah ﷻ and speak truthfully. But the community also has a responsibility: investigation doesn't stop just because someone swore an oath. The community must still conduct due diligence. They might return to the deceased's tribe to verify what kind of people these witnesses are. They might seek corroboration from others who heard the conversation. The oath creates accountability, but verification remains essential.

When dishonesty is uncovered, Allah ﷻ provides the remedy: فَـَٔاخَرَانِ يَقُومَانِ مَقَامَهُمَا (then two others stand up where they were standing) مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَوْلَيَـٰنِ (from those who deserve more to stand there than the first two). Perhaps family members who knew the deceased better step forward, saying: "He didn't speak to us at the deathbed, but we know him, and this is what he would have wanted. Let us speak on his behalf."

These replacement witnesses then declare: فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِٱللَّهِ لَشَهَـٰدَتُنَآ أَحَقُّ مِن شَهَـٰدَتِهِمَا (we swear by Allah, our testimony is more truthful than their testimony). Notice the emphasis on comparative truthfulness—they're not just claiming to be honest, but explicitly more honest than those who lied. They conclude with وَمَا ٱعْتَدَيْنَآ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (we haven't crossed any lines, and if we were to have crossed lines, then we would be from the wrongdoers).

Who stands up in this situation? People the community already knows to be reliable, those who already have established credibility. This built-in social accountability ensures justice can be restored even when the initial process fails.

translation: That is more likely that they will give testimony according to its [true] objective, or [at least] they would fear that [other] oaths might be taken after their oaths. And fear Allah and listen; and Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people
commentary:
ذَ ٰ⁠لِكَ أَدۡنَىٰۤ أَن یَأۡتُوا۟ بِٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ عَلَىٰ وَجۡهِهَاۤ أَوۡ یَخَافُوۤا۟ أَن تُرَدَّ أَیۡمَـٰنُۢ بَعۡدَ أَیۡمَـٰنِهِمۡۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱسۡمَعُوا۟ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا یَهۡدِی ٱلۡقَوۡمَ ٱلۡفَـٰسِقِینَ
The word أَدْنَىٰٓ means "closer" but carries the implication of being closer to safety or correctness. Allah ﷻ explains that this system of allowing counter-testimony is ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهَآ (safer, closer to ensuring that testimony comes at face value). The phrase عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهَآ literally means "on its face"—testimony that can be taken at face value rather than requiring endless investigation and suspicion.

This system creates two powerful deterrents. First, witnesses know their testimony won't simply be accepted without scrutiny. Second, أَوْ يَخَافُوٓا۟ أَن تُرَدَّ أَيْمَـٰنٌۢ بَعْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ (they fear that their oath will be rejected even after they've taken it). The possibility that others might stand up and contradict them with greater credibility gives their oath weight and consequence. This framework elevates the value and seriousness of testimony in Islamic law.

Allah ﷻ then commands وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ (have fear of Allah and listen well). This وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ is particularly important. When witnesses testify, the community must listen carefully. Not selective hearing, not immediate debate where people argue "I don't think he said that" or "I think he meant this." Turn your distractions off and pay attention, because corruption can break out from poor listening and misunderstanding.

The ayah concludes وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (Allah does not guide the corrupt nation). When a people become fasiqin—corrupted in their character and dealings—they lose divine guidance in establishing justice.

translation: [Be warned of] the Day when Allah will assemble the messengers and say, "What was the response you received?" They will say, "We have no knowledge. Indeed, it is You who is Knower of the unseen
commentary:
۞ يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ مَاذَآ أُجِبْتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ

This ayah marks a dramatic shift. We've reached the final passage of instruction in Surah Al-Ma'idah, right before its conclusion. Just as the end of Surah An-Nisa contained extensive discussion about Isa (AS), now at the end of Al-Ma'idah—the sister surah to An-Nisa—we find another powerful dimension of his story.

يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرُّسُلَ (The Day on which Allah ﷻ will gather the messengers). Picture this scene: all the prophets and messengers gathered before Allah ﷻ on the Day of Judgment. فَيَقُولُ مَاذَآ أُجِبْتُمْ (He will say to them: How were you responded to? What answers were you given?). Allah ﷻ knows the answer, but He asks to establish testimony publicly.

The response is striking: قَالُوا۟ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ (They all say: We don't know anything). These are the prophets—the best of humanity, the ones who carried Allah's message and endured tremendous hardship. Some were rejected, mocked, called liars and magicians. They could list the grievances, the struggles, the persecution. Yet their response is لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ—we have no knowledge.

Why this answer? Because the magnitude of that Day terrifies even the prophets. The haqiqah (ultimate reality) has now manifested, and they stand in absolute humility before their Creator. They defer completely to His knowledge: إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ (You, and You alone, are the Knower of all that is hidden).

This is the same gathering where, as mentioned in other passages, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ will testify that his nation abandoned the Quran. That's terrifying. When all the prophets say "we don't know," it reveals the weight of accountability on that Day.

translation: [The Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, remember My favor upon you and upon your mother when I supported you with the Pure Spirit and you spoke to the people in the cradle and in maturity; and [remember] when I taught you writing and wisdom and the Torah and the Gospel; and when you designed from clay [what was] like the form of a bird with My permission, then you breathed into it, and it became a bird with My permission; and you healed the blind and the leper with My permission; and when you brought forth the dead with My permission; and when I restrained the Children of Israel from [killing] you when you came to them with clear proofs and those who disbelieved among them said, "This is not but obvious magic
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَـٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱذْكُرْ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَتِكَ إِذْ أَيَّدتُّكَ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ تُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًا ۖ وَإِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ ۖ وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِى فَتَنفُخُ فِيهَا فَتَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَتُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ تُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ كَفَفْتُ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَنكَ إِذْ جِئْتَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ

Now comes the crucial conversation. Notice Allah ﷻ doesn't say "O Musa" or address other prophets. He singles out إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَـٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ (when Allah said: O Isa, son of Maryam). Why this emphasis? Because Christianity is the largest religion in the world. Christians await Judgment Day believing Jesus will save them, that he'll intercede on their behalf because he's the son of God. So they're looking around anxiously for Isa (AS), and their hopes are high. "Here he is! He'll tell God to let us go."

But Allah ﷻ doesn't ask him about salvation or sonship. Instead: ٱذْكُرْ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَتِكَ (Remember My favor upon you and upon your mother). Every miracle, every extraordinary event Christians attribute to divine nature? Allah ﷻ frames each one as a favor He granted.

إِذْ أَيَّدتُّكَ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ (When I aided you, when I gave you a hand by means of the Holy Spirit)—meaning Jibreel (AS). تُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًا (You spoke to people in the cradle and in middle age). That miraculous speech as an infant? My favor. Your continued guidance later? My favor.

وَإِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ (When I taught you the Book and wisdom, the Torah and the Injeel). The word الْكِتَـٰبَ here refers to literacy itself—the ability to read and write. Then wisdom (الْحِكْمَةَ), which represents understanding and insight. Why did He teach him the Torah? So Isa (AS) could challenge the rabbis. How could he critique their distortions if he didn't know what they knew? He knew more than the rabbis themselves. The wisdom and the revelation of the Injeel grew from that foundation.

Then Allah ﷻ recounts the miracles, but notice how He frames them: وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِى (When you were creating from clay the shape of a bird by My permission). In other places in the Quran, the emphasis is on blowing life into the bird. Here, Allah ﷻ emphasizes the shape itself—you couldn't even make the shape of a bird without My permission. Even forming the clay was an accomplishment only possible through divine aid.

فَتَنفُخُ فِيهَا فَتَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِى (Then you blew into that shape, and it became a bird by My permission). Notice: blowing into the shape, and the transformation—both بِإِذْنِى (by My permission). Every stage required divine intervention.

وَتُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ بِإِذْنِى (You healed the blind and the leper by My permission). وَإِذْ تُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِى (When you brought the dead back to life by My permission). Every miracle that led people to deify him? Allah ﷻ attributes it explicitly to His permission, His power, His favor.

Then something remarkable: وَإِذْ كَفَفْتُ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَنكَ (When I held the hands of Bani Israel from you, when I kept them from attacking you). Earlier in the previous surah, Allah ﷻ mentioned holding the hands of disbelievers from attacking the Muslims. Now He reveals He did the same for Isa (AS)—just as He protected the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from the Quraysh, He protected Isa (AS) from Bani Israel.

إِذْ جِئْتَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ (When you came to them with the clearest proofs) فَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (those bent upon disbelieving looked at the clearest proof and said: This is nothing but obvious magic). The irony is devastating. The very miracles Christians now use to prove divinity were called magic by those who witnessed them.

translation: And [remember] when I inspired to the disciples, "Believe in Me and in My messenger Jesus." They said, "We have believed, so bear witness that indeed we are Muslims [in submission to Allah]
commentary:
وَإِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى ٱلْحَوَارِيِّـۧنَ أَنْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِى وَبِرَسُولِى قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّنَا مُسْلِمُونَ

The reminder continues: وَإِذْ أَوْحَيْتُ إِلَى ٱلْحَوَارِيِّـۧنَ (And when I revealed to the disciples). The word وحي (revelation) is used here, indicating these close followers of Isa (AS) received divine inspiration. What was that revelation? أَنْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِى وَبِرَسُولِى (That you should believe in Me and in My messenger).

This is critical. The teaching given to the disciples wasn't "the messenger is God." It was: believe in God and His messenger. Two distinct entities. The messenger is the one sent by God, not God Himself.

Their response: قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا (We believe!) وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّنَا مُسْلِمُونَ (And bear witness that we are Muslims—that we are in complete submission).

Imagine the Christian population listening to this conversation between God and Jesus. They're shocked. The disciples—the original followers of Christ, the ones Christians claim to follow—testified that they were Muslims? Yes. مُسْلِمُونَ. Those in complete submission to the One God.

translation: [And remember] when the disciples said, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, can your Lord send down to us a table [spread with food] from the heaven? [Jesus] said," Fear Allah, if you should be believers
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ يَـٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ أَن يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةً مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ قَالَ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

Allah ﷻ continues reminding Isa (AS): إِذْ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ يَـٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ (When the disciples said: O Isa, son of Maryam). Notice again—even his closest followers, his true believers, never called him anything but "son of Maryam." Not son of God. Son of Maryam.

هَلْ يَسْتَطِيعُ رَبُّكَ أَن يُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةً مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ (Is your Master capable of sending us a table spread from the sky?). These were the true believers, the companions of Isa (AS). They were under persecution, facing intense opposition from the Romans and increasing animosity from the Israelites. Isa (AS) could literally feel the intensity of kufr (disbelief) around them—حَسَّ (he felt) has a physical dimension, like feeling heat. In this pressure, they needed something to fortify their faith.

This is reminiscent of Ibrahim (AS), who asked to see how Allah ﷻ gives life to the dead—not from doubt, but to satisfy his heart. There's precedent for such requests from the sincere.

But Isa's (AS) immediate response? قَالَ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (He said: Have taqwa of Allah if you are believers). His first instinct wasn't to immediately accommodate their request. He knew the dangers. Allah ﷻ says elsewhere: لَا تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ عَنْ أَشْيَآءَ إِن تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ (Don't ask about things that if made clear to you, would trouble you) (5:101). 

Previous nations had asked for miracles and then failed the test that came with them. Isa (AS) knew that history. He's the scholar of all nations, knowing what happened to those before him. So his response is: if you have iman, you should be careful about what you're asking for.

translation: They said, "We wish to eat from it and let our hearts be reassured and know that you have been truthful to us and be among its witnesses
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ نُرِيدُ أَن نَّأْكُلَ مِنْهَا وَتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُنَا وَنَعْلَمَ أَن قَدْ صَدَقْتَنَا وَنَكُونَ عَلَيْهَا مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ

The disciples clarify their intention: قَالُوا۟ نُرِيدُ أَن نَّأْكُلَ مِنْهَا (We want to eat from it). They're not asking out of frivolity or testing Allah ﷻ like previous nations did. 

وَتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُنَا (And so our hearts will become satisfied, completely at rest). The word تَطْمَئِنَّ suggests deep tranquility and peace. They're facing tremendous external pressure—the Romans are coming, the Israelites are intensifying their opposition. They need internal strength.

وَنَعْلَمَ أَن قَدْ صَدَقْتَنَا (And we'll know deep inside that you've truly told us the truth). The knowledge will be certain, unshakeable.

وَنَكُونَ عَلَيْهَا مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (And we will be absolute witnesses to it). We won't just believe—we'll testify with certainty to what we've seen.

Their explanation shows this isn't like those who asked Musa (AS) to show them Allah ﷻ directly, or who kept demanding signs out of stubbornness. This is genuine spiritual need in the face of overwhelming opposition.

translation: Said Jesus, the son of Mary, "O Allah, our Lord, send down to us a table [spread with food] from the heaven to be for us a festival for the first of us and the last of us and a sign from You. And provide for us, and You are the best of providers
commentary:
قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ٱللَّهُمَّ رَبَّنَآ أَنزِلْ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةً مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ تَكُونُ لَنَا عِيدًا لِّأَوَّلِنَا وَءَاخِرِنَا وَءَايَةً مِّنكَ ۖ وَٱرْزُقْنَا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ

After their clarification, Isa (AS) was convinced of their sincerity. So he makes dua: قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ٱللَّهُمَّ رَبَّنَآ (Isa son of Maryam said: O Allah, our Master). 

أَنزِلْ عَلَيْنَا مَآئِدَةً مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ (Send down upon us a table spread from the sky). Then he describes what this would mean: تَكُونُ لَنَا عِيدًا (It will be a celebration for us). 

لِّأَوَّلِنَا وَءَاخِرِنَا (For the first of us and the last of us—for all our generations). If You send this, generation after generation will make mention of this great favor. We'll celebrate it—like how Christians reference "the Last Supper." It would become a defining moment of gratitude.

وَءَايَةً مِّنكَ (And a miraculous sign from You). 

وَٱرْزُقْنَا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلرَّٰزِقِينَ (Provide for us, for You are the best of providers). Notice the softness of this dua. He doesn't demand or insist—he acknowledges Allah's ultimate authority as the provider.

This is beautiful: Isa's (AS) first response was "Are you crazy? I'm not asking for that!" But they reasoned with him, showed their sincerity, and he was moved to make this humble request on their behalf.

translation: Allah said, "Indeed, I will sent it down to you, but whoever disbelieves afterwards from among you - then indeed will I punish him with a punishment by which I have not punished anyone among the worlds
commentary:
قَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مُنَزِّلُهَا عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بَعْدُ مِنكُمْ فَإِنِّىٓ أُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًا لَّآ أُعَذِّبُهُۥٓ أَحَدًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Allah's response is immediate and unequivocal: قَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مُنَزِّلُهَا عَلَيْكُمْ (Allah said: I am sending it down to you). The particle إِنِّى emphasizes absolute certainty—"I am definitely sending it, guaranteed, no doubt about it."

But then comes the condition: فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بَعْدُ مِنكُمْ (So whoever disbelieves after this among you). This isn't regular food. This is a miraculous sign carrying immense responsibility. The word مُنَزِّلُهَا (that which is sent down) is conditional—it's sent down with conditions attached.

فَإِنِّىٓ أُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًا (Then I will torture him with a torture). The present tense أُعَذِّبُهُۥ suggests continuous, ongoing punishment. 

Then Allah ﷻ emphasizes: لَّآ أُعَذِّبُهُۥٓ أَحَدًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (A torture I have never inflicted on anyone from all the nations of the world). Nobody has ever been tortured like this. And notice—Allah ﷻ doesn't delegate this to angels or hellfire. The implication is: I will personally see to it that this punishment happens.

This is perhaps the harshest warning of punishment in the entire Quran. There's no description of fire, burning skin, scalding water, or any physical details. Just: "I will do it. I will do it. And I have never done it to anyone before." That's the ultimate warning.

The severity makes sense: this is directed at a nation that received more miracles than any other. They received the final clear sign—the table from heaven. To disbelieve after that level of certainty is to reach a depth of ingratitude unmatched in human history.

This echoes earlier warnings: لَا تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ عَنْ أَشْيَآء (Don't ask about things). When Bani Israel came to the Prophet ﷺ saying, "We won't believe you're a prophet unless the sacrifice you make gets burnt up into the sky"—if they had gotten that sign and then disbelieved, they would have been finished. That would have been their end.

translation: And [beware the Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?'" He will say, "Exalted are You! It was not for me to say that to which I have no right. If I had said it, You would have known it. You know what is within myself, and I do not know what is within Yourself. Indeed, it is You who is Knower of the unseen
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَـٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ءَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ ٱتَّخِذُونِى وَأُمِّىَ إِلَـٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لِىٓ أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِحَقٍّ ۚ إِن كُنتُ قُلْتُهُۥ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُۥ ۚ تَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِى وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِكَ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ

Now comes the central confrontation. Christians have been listening intently to this exchange. They heard about the severe punishment for those who disbelieve after receiving miracles. But it still hasn't been made explicitly clear what happened. Now Allah ﷻ asks directly:

وَإِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَـٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ (When Allah said: O Isa, son of Maryam)—again emphasizing his identity as Maryam's son, not God's son.

ءَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ ٱتَّخِذُونِى وَأُمِّىَ إِلَـٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ (Did you say to people: Take me and my mother as two gods to be worshipped besides Allah?).

This question devastates the entire theological foundation of Christianity. God is asking His messenger publicly, in front of all creation, on the Day of Judgment: Did you tell them to do this?

The response of Isa (AS): قَالَ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ (He said: Glory be to You! How far above that claim You are!). Before defending himself, he glorifies Allah ﷻ. The very suggestion is so far from the truth that it must be rejected with complete reverence.

مَا يَكُونُ لِىٓ أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِحَقٍّ (It is not appropriate for me, it is not becoming of me to say something I have no right to say). Notice he doesn't use the past tense—"it wasn't appropriate." He uses يَكُونُ, which indicates continuous state. It wasn't appropriate then, it's not appropriate now, it will never be appropriate. How could I ever say such a thing? It's impossible.

Then, with remarkable humility: إِن كُنتُ قُلْتُهُۥ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُۥ (If I had said it—if perhaps my memory fails me, if I'm mistaken, if I somehow said something crazy like that and don't even remember—then You definitely would have known it). 

This is crucial. Remember, when Allah ﷻ first asked all the messengers "How were you responded to?" what did they say? لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ (We have no knowledge). Isa (AS) continues in that spirit: I'm human. Maybe I forget. Maybe there's a possibility I made a terrible mistake. If I did, You would know.

تَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِى (You know what's inside me). وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِكَ (And I don't know what's in You—what knowledge You have, what You know about me even).

This destroys the concept of the Trinity. Christians claim God and Jesus are one, that Jesus is in God and God is in Jesus. But here Isa (AS) says: No. You know everything inside me. I don't know what's inside You. There's a complete separation.

إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ (You, and no one else, are the Knower of all unseen things). The emphasis on أَنتَ (You alone) is absolute. Only Allah ﷻ has this quality.

translation: I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me up, You were the Observer over them, and You are, over all things, Witness
commentary:
مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَمَرْتَنِى بِهِۦٓ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۚ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِى كُنتَ أَنتَ ٱلرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ

Isa (AS) clarifies his message explicitly: مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَمَرْتَنِى بِهِۦٓ (I never said to them except what You commanded me to say). Not what I wanted to say, not what they wanted to hear—only what You commanded.

What was that command? أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ (To worship Allah, my Master and your Master). Notice: رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ (my Master and your Master)—putting himself in the same category as them. Not "worship me" or "I am your master." He's a slave of Allah ﷻ just as they are.

وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ (And I was a witness over them so long as I remained among them). مَّا دُمْتُ emphasizes duration—for as long as I was with them, I witnessed their actions.

فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِى (Then when You took me away—when You caused me to die or raised me). كُنتَ أَنتَ ٱلرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ (You were the only Watcher over them). After I left, they were under Your watch, not mine.

وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (And You are a witness over all things). You witnessed what they did after I was gone.

There's a subtle point here. It might seem like Isa (AS) is saying "That happened on Your watch, not mine"—shifting responsibility. But the next ayah shows he doesn't cross that line.

translation: If You should punish them - indeed they are Your servants; but if You forgive them - indeed it is You who is the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
إِن تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبَادُكَ ۖ وَإِن تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ فَإِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

This is one of the most powerful statements in the Quran. إِن تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبَادُكَ (If You torture them, they are Your slaves). You have complete authority over Your slaves. Whatever You decide, You have that right.

وَإِن تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ (And if You forgive them)—here's where Christians listening expect the typical names: "You're Ar-Rahman, Ar-Rahim." Those are the attributes they've built their entire theology around. "God is so merciful, He guaranteed us forgiveness. God is so merciful, He sacrificed His son for us."

But Isa (AS) doesn't mention those names. Instead: فَإِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (Then certainly You are the Ultimate Authority, the All-Wise).

This is devastating. Why doesn't he mention mercy and forgiveness? Because those are the names Christians manipulated. They took Allah's mercy and turned it into a guarantee, an entitlement. They abused those beautiful names to justify their shirk (associating partners with Allah ﷻ).

So when Isa (AS) speaks, those names become inappropriate to mention in their favor. Instead, he emphasizes: You are Al-Aziz (the Mighty, the Ultimate Authority)—whatever You decide, You have full power to execute. And You are Al-Hakim (the All-Wise)—whatever decision You make is full of wisdom.

The Christian listening to this realizes: Jesus himself didn't invoke God's mercy on their behalf. He deferred completely to Allah's authority and wisdom. The very foundation they built their salvation upon—guaranteed mercy through Jesus—collapses.

translation: Allah will say, "This is the Day when the truthful will benefit from their truthfulness." For them are gardens [in Paradise] beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever, Allah being pleased with them, and they with Him. That is the great attainment
commentary:
قَالَ ٱللَّهُ هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ يَنفَعُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ صِدْقُهُمْ ۚ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ رَّضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ

After that complete testimony from Isa (AS), Allah ﷻ responds: قَالَ ٱللَّهُ هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ يَنفَعُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ صِدْقُهُمْ (Allah said: This is the Day when the truthful will benefit from their truthfulness). 

Notice what we were expecting. Earlier, Allah ﷻ said whoever disbelieves after receiving the table spread, فَإِنِّىٓ أُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًا لَّآ أُعَذِّبُهُۥٓ أَحَدًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (I will torture him with a torture I've never inflicted on anyone). We expect to hear about that punishment now. But Allah ﷻ doesn't even mention it. He goes straight to the reward of the truthful.

لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ (They will have gardens beneath which rivers flow). خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا (Remaining in them forever).

رَّضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ (Allah is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him). This mutual pleasure—Allah's pleasure with His slaves and their pleasure with Him—is the ultimate reward, even beyond Paradise itself.

ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (That is the ultimate success).

Imagine what's happening in the heart of someone who believed Jesus was God. They just witnessed the complete interrogation, the public clarification, the separation between Creator and creation. What are they feeling? The open justice of this court proceeding means everyone sees exactly why they ended up where they ended up. No surprises, no hidden judgments. Complete transparency.

translation: To Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth and whatever is within them. And He is over all things competent
commentary:
لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا فِيهِنَّ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌۢ

The surah concludes with a magnificent declaration: لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (To Allah alone belongs the dominion, the kingdom of the skies and the earth).

وَمَا فِيهِنَّ (And whatever is in them). Everything, without exception.

وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌۢ (And He is in complete control over all things, has power over all things).

This conclusion is remarkable when we consider the broader context. Which nation did Muslims have the most interaction with historically, particularly toward the end of the Prophet's ﷺ life and continuing afterward? The Roman Empire—the Christian nations. The Abyssinians, the Romans—these Christian powers continued to shape the geopolitical landscape the Muslim Ummah navigated.

As we reached the end of the Prophet's ﷺ prophetic career, we saw the beginning of international relations, interfaith dialogue, and the message spreading beyond Arabia. This surah contains ethical guidance for engaging with the People of the Book, particularly Christians. It's the foundation for how Islam educates the world.

Surah Al-Ma'idah ends by firmly establishing the Oneness of Allah ﷻ, the prophethood (not divinity) of Isa (AS), and the complete authority and wisdom of Allah ﷻ in judgment. This isn't just historical correction—it's an ongoing call to truth, an invitation to recognize the One God who alone deserves worship, and a warning about the consequences of distorting divine revelation.

May Allah ﷻ guide us to the truth and keep us firm upon it. May He make us among those whose truthfulness benefits them on that Day, and may He grant us His pleasure and Paradise. Ameen.
